title: CLAUDE.md
text: Guidance Claude Code (LLM assistants) working repository.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Bias toward caution speed. trivial edits, use judgment; anything substantive, four hold. Adapted karpathy-skills CLAUDE.md (https://github.com/forrestchang/andrej-karpathy-skills).

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Don’t assume. Don’t hide confusion. Surface tradeoffs. State assumptions explicitly; uncertain, ask. multiple interpretations exist, present — don’t pick silently. simpler approach exists, say . Push back warranted. something unclear, stop. Name ’s confusing. Ask. repo particularly means: audit result ambiguous, flag ambiguity instead forcing PASS/FAIL verdict; R-spec change implies Julia-side change (vice versa), say editing either side.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Minimum code solves problem. Nothing speculative. features beyond asked. abstractions single-use code. configurability wasn’t requested. error handling impossible scenarios. wrote 200 lines 50, rewrite . repo: don’t add new spec fields “flexibility”. new spec commitment test, document, maintain forever.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Touch must. Clean mess. Don’t “improve” adjacent code, comments, formatting. Don’t refactor things aren’t broken. Match existing style, even ’d differently. notice unrelated dead code, mention — don’t delete . Remove imports/variables/functions changes made unused. Don’t remove pre-existing dead code unless asked. repo: fixing single vignette audit report, don’t sweep nearby files “related” issues commit — separate PRs. don’t hand-edit NAMESPACE man/*.Rd; run devtools::document() commit generated output.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Define success criteria. Loop verified. “Add X” → “Write test X, make pass.” “Fix bug” → “Write test reproduces , make pass.” “Refactor X” → “Ensure tests pass .” multi-step tasks, state brief plan: repo: audit claims need 8+ seeds + viability_report() guard called PASS; kernel changes need relevant tests/testthat/test-*.R still green; figure regenerations reproduce claimed Δ pushed.
code: 1. [step] → verify: [check] 2. [step] → verify: [check]

title: CLAUDE.md
text: clade R package providing agent-based evolutionary simulation Julia backend. R user-facing interface (specs, runs, analysis, plots); per-tick simulation kernel lives Julia called per run_alife() via JuliaConnectoR, R↔︎Julia boundary crossed per run, per tick. Key facts: - R package layout (DESCRIPTION, NAMESPACE, R/, man/, tests/testthat/, vignettes/). - Julia kernel inst/julia/ (Project.toml/Manifest.toml, sources inst/julia/src/). - Cached simulation outputs Rdata/*.rds (used vignettes/figures render without re-running). - pkgdown site config: _pkgdown.yml. Docs published https://itchyshin.github.io/clade/.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: R-side (run repo root): Quick smoke run: Julia-side (needed kernel debugging):
code: # Load package for interactive dev (no install required) devtools::load_all()  # Run all tests devtools::test()  # Run a single test file testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test-config.R")  # Rebuild documentation from roxygen comments devtools::document()  # Full check (CRAN-style) devtools::check()  # Build pkgdown site locally (writes to docs/, gitignored) pkgdown::build_site() library(clade) julia_is_ready()         # first call compiles Julia kernel (~60–90 s) specs <- default_specs() specs$n_agents_init <- 40L specs$max_ticks     <- 300L env <- run_alife(specs) plot_run(get_run_data(env)) cd inst/julia julia --project=. -e 'using Pkg; Pkg.instantiate()'

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Exports managed roxygen → edit NAMESPACE hand; edit @export tags R/*.R run devtools::document(). Public R API surface everything listed NAMESPACE (export(...)). Specs plain named lists — see default_specs() canonical schema vignettes/parameter-reference.Rmd documentation. Modules toggled via boolean flags specs list (e.g. specs$disease <- TRUE); see README “Biological modules” table. Cached .rds files Rdata/ checked git vignettes render cheaply — regenerate underlying simulation changes meaningfully.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: running parallel sweeps, batch jobs, anything CPU/memory-heavy machine, cap ≤200 cores target ≲300 GB memory (machine ~1 TB keep headroom). Flag job exceed either launching.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: repo also edited second machine. making changes: 1. git pull --rebase verify git status clean. 2. Prefer feature branches committing directly main. 3. Push frequently machine can rebase work. 4. Avoid editing file machine concurrently — ask unsure area flight.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Don’t commit large regenerated artifacts (pkgdown docs/, *.Rcheck/, .Rhistory — already gitignored, double-check). Don’t hand-edit NAMESPACE man/*.Rd (regenerated roxygen). Don’t bypass JuliaConnectoR — exactly one R↔︎Julia boundary crossing per run_alife() call.

title: Self-hosted GitHub Actions runner — setup guide
text: clade private repo Free plan, caps Actions 3 000 min/month. Public repos unlimited minutes, making clade public pre-publication isn’t table. CI self-hosted runner uses zero GitHub minutes. runner small Docker-free agent runs Linux machine (≈ 1 TB RAM, 200+ cores per CLAUDE.md). Capacity dwarfs anything GitHub’s shared runners offer. Combined paths-ignore already added R-CMD-check.yaml (docs-PRs skip CI entirely), self-hosted brings monthly bill effectively zero.

title: The Baldwin Effect: within-lifetime learning accelerating evolution
text: Baldwin Effect (Baldwin 1896; Simpson 1953) neo-Darwinian mechanism within-lifetime learning can accelerate genetic evolution without Lamarckian inheritance. logic four steps: novel variable environment, genetically fixed behaviours suboptimal. Individuals can adjust behaviour within lifetime survive long enough reproduce. learners reproduce , genetic variants predispose faster deeper learning spread — learning ability becomes selected. generations, fitness benefit learning shrinks genome gradually encodes -learned behaviour directly. endpoint genetic assimilation (Waddington 1942): learned now innate. organism longer needs learn . clade, BNN brain type (brain_type = "bnn") makes process directly observable. agent’s neural network carries prior standard deviation σ synaptic weights. High σ = exploration (wide Thompson sampling, broad behavioural flexibility). Low σ = canalization (narrow prior, behaviour largely determined genome). population mean mean_prior_sigma therefore continuous readout evolution placed population exploration–assimilation axis.

title: The Baldwin Effect: within-lifetime learning accelerating evolution
text: Two conditions, 1000 ticks, 150 agents 25×25 grid (grass_rate = 0.10). Two readouts: mean_prior_sigma (assimilation signal — high σ = exploration, low σ = canalization) genetic_diversity (mean pairwise genome distance). Top: mean BNN prior sigma 1000 ticks. Baseline (blue dashed): sigma rises 0.5 exploration ceiling — canalization dynamic foraging world. Epigenetics (orange): sigma declines toward zero TEI propagates within-lifetime learning across generations. Bottom: genetic diversity (mean pairwise genome distance) rises conditions — epigenetic canalization sigma cause selective sweep genome; different genotypes can encode canalized behaviour. Baseline: σ rises ceiling (0.5) stays . foraging landscape shifts continuously — grass grows stochastically, population density creates local competition, single fixed policy consistently outcompetes broad sampling. Exploration evolutionarily stable strategy. Baldwin Effect occur default dynamic world. Epigenetics: σ declines 0.5 toward zero 1000 ticks. mechanism two-step chain: (1) within-lifetime learning contracts agent’s weight posterior lifetime, narrowing σ agent forages; (2) transgenerational epigenetic inheritance (TEI) passes narrowed priors offspring. generation starts canalized last, selection reinforces direction. genetic assimilation via Baldwin Effect — achieved Lamarckian-adjacent transmission mechanism (TEI), pure Darwinian selection stable fitness peak. Genetic diversity: conditions show rising diversity 1000 ticks. notable result: epigenetic canalization σ sweep genome. Different weight matrices can encode low-σ (canalized) foraging policy, genetic variation maintained even phenotypic trait (σ) converges. five systematic experiments ask: canalization occur, disrupts ?
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) library(patchwork)  base <- default_specs() base$brain_type    <- "bnn" base$n_agents_init <- 150L base$max_ticks     <- 1000L base$grid_rows     <- 25L base$grid_cols     <- 25L base$grass_rate    <- 0.10 base$random_seed   <- 42L # Condition 1: BNN baseline — genetic evolution only env_base <- run_alife(base)  # Condition 2: BNN + epigenetics (TEI) # Within-lifetime learning narrows the BNN prior; TEI transmits the # narrowed priors to offspring. No stable landscape required. s_epi <- base s_epi$epigenetics            <- TRUE s_epi$epigenetic_inheritance <- 0.5 s_epi$methylation_rate       <- 0.2 env_epi <- run_alife(s_epi) df <- bind_rows(   transform(get_run_data(env_base)$ticks, condition = "BNN baseline"),   transform(get_run_data(env_epi)$ticks,  condition = "BNN + epigenetics") ) df$condition <- factor(df$condition,   levels = c("BNN baseline", "BNN + epigenetics"))  pal <- c("BNN baseline"      = "#377eb8",          "BNN + epigenetics" = "#e6550d") lty <- c("BNN baseline"      = "dashed",          "BNN + epigenetics" = "solid")  pA <- ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_prior_sigma, colour = condition, linetype = condition)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, colour = "grey70", linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.5) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, na.rm = TRUE) +   scale_colour_manual(values = pal, name = NULL) +   scale_linetype_manual(values = lty, name = NULL) +   scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +   scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.55)) +   labs(x = NULL, y = expression("Mean prior " * sigma)) +   theme_classic(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.key.width = unit(1.4, "cm"))  pB <- ggplot(df, aes(t, genetic_diversity, colour = condition, linetype = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, na.rm = TRUE) +   scale_colour_manual(values = pal, name = NULL) +   scale_linetype_manual(values = lty, name = NULL) +   scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +   labs(x = "Tick", y = "Genetic diversity\n(mean pairwise genome distance)") +   theme_classic(base_size = 11) + theme(legend.position = "none")  pA / pB + plot_layout(heights = c(2, 1))

title: The Baldwin Effect: within-lifetime learning accelerating evolution
text: original computational demonstration Baldwin Effect (Hinton & Nowlan 1987) used needle---haystack fitness landscape: one correct genotype gave high fitness, others gave near-zero fitness, learning (random search) provided intermediate fitness signal. Canalization occurred agents correct alleles needed less learning find needle — σ declined correct alleles accumulated. clade provides fixed_patch module implements closest spatial equivalent: permanent high-value resource cell never moves. Agents genetically encode navigation toward patch need less within-lifetime exploration, creating selection pressure high σ. carrying capacity environment must match patch size: 49 patch cells sparse background, starting population 50–80 agents allows selection act without crashing extinction. Parameter tuning (patch radius, grass_rate, n_agents_init) determines whether Hinton–Nowlan result (σ declines) population crash occurs — reflecting sensitivity genetic assimilation fitness landscape geometry. five systematic experiments used fixed_patch = FALSE (dynamic world ) map boundary conditions canalization clade’s default environment.
code: # Hinton & Nowlan (1987) analogue: sparse background, large fixed patch s_hn <- default_specs() s_hn$brain_type         <- "bnn" s_hn$grass_rate         <- 0.02   # sparse — patch becomes essential s_hn$fixed_patch        <- TRUE s_hn$fixed_patch_value  <- 5.0 s_hn$fixed_patch_radius <- 3L     # 7×7 = 49 cells at grid centre s_hn$fixed_patch_x      <- 13L s_hn$fixed_patch_y      <- 13L s_hn$max_ticks          <- 2000L env_hn <- run_alife(s_hn)

previous_headings: Systematic experiments: when does canalization occur?
title: The Baldwin Effect: within-lifetime learning accelerating evolution
text: Question. Mayley (1996) showed Baldwin Effect operates fastest fitness landscape single, stable peak. varying resource abundance temporal variability produce condition canalization occurs? Design. 3 × 3 factorial: grass_rate ∈ {0.05, 0.10, 0.20} × seasonal_amplitude ∈ {0, 0.4, 0.8}; 5 seeds × 1000 ticks = 45 runs. condition seed, linear regression mean_prior_sigma ~ tick fitted; slope measure canalization (negative = assimilation, positive = exploration selected). found. Every one 9 conditions produced positive σ slope — canalization cell. 9 converged ceiling (σ = 0.5) tick 1000. ‡ Population-crash artefact (high variance across seeds), canalization. Crucially, ordering inverse Baldwin Effect prediction: resource scarcity (grass = 0.05) produced steepest positive slopes, abundance (grass = 0.20) produced shallowest — yet still positive. foraging difficult, agents high σ can explore broadly strong survival advantage; tight priors fatal. bold row canalization-favourable cell tested; still reaches ceiling. Phase diagram mean_prior_sigma slope (×10⁻⁴) across 9 resource-abundance × seasonality conditions. cells positive (red). predicted canalization signal (green) appear cell. Interpretation. competitive foraging world spatial heterogeneity population-density feedbacks, single foraging policy universally optimal. fitness landscape shifts continuously, stable peak canalization track. Selection maintains ability learn (high σ) ESS across resource seasonality levels tested.

what: Experiment 2 — Is the sigma ceiling an artefact of run length?
title: The Baldwin Effect: within-lifetime learning accelerating evolution
text: Question. σ plateaus 0.5 within 300 ticks baseline. canalization emerge given substantially longer evolutionary time — 2000 5000 ticks? Design. canalization-favourable condition Experiment 1 (grass = 0.20, seasonal = 0.8): 5 seeds 1000, 2000, 5000 ticks = 15 runs. found. σ slope decelerates markedly run length: slope shrinks 21-fold 1000 5000 ticks. final_sigma remains 0.50 cases. deceleration ceiling saturation: σ presses upper bound (0.5), observable slope must approach zero ceiling reached — regardless whether canalization occurring. ceiling parameter (prior_sigma_max) raised, σ continue rising. Sigma trajectories 1000, 2000, 5000 ticks (mean ± SE across 5 seeds). runs reach 0.5 ceiling; slope decelerates 21-fold never reverses. dashed line marks ceiling. Interpretation. Run length limiting factor. near-zero slope 5000 ticks looks superficially like onset canalization measurement artefact ceiling constraint. biological conclusion unchanged: canalization occurs even five times evolutionary time baseline.

previous_headings: Systematic experiments: when does canalization occur?
what: Experiment 4 — Social modifiers: can they break the ceiling?
title: The Baldwin Effect: within-lifetime learning accelerating evolution
text: Question. genetic evolution within-lifetime RL fail produce canalization, can social transmission mechanisms ? Three candidates: kin-based altruism (energy transfers relatives), prestige-biased social learning (copying successful neighbours’ policy weights), epigenetic inheritance (transgenerational transmission within-lifetime weight contractions). Design. 4 modules × 2 environments × 5 seeds × 1000 ticks = 40 runs. two environments stable-abundant (grass = 0.20, seasonal = 0.0) scarce-stable (grass = 0.05, seasonal = 0.0). found. Stable abundant (grass = 0.20, seasonal = 0): Scarce stable (grass = 0.05, seasonal = 0): Epigenetic inheritance mechanism substantially reduces σ. stable-abundant environment, reduces final σ 0.500 0.342 — 32% ceiling. scarce-stable environment effect stronger: σ reaches 0.140, 72% reduction 0.5 ceiling 65% baseline final value 0.400. Final mean prior sigma social module environment. Epigenetics (red dot) mechanism substantially reduces sigma 0.5 ceiling (dashed line). modules indistinguishable baseline stable environment provide moderate reductions scarce environment. Interpretation. epigenetic mechanism transgenerational epigenetic inheritance (TEI): agent contracts weight posterior lifetime (σ narrows learns), offspring inherit partially-contracted priors probability tei_prob. Lamarckian shortcut — within-lifetime narrowing transmitted directly descendants, bypassing slow route requires mutations fix via selection many generations. contrast: Social learning significant effect stable environment copying output-layer weights successful neighbours propagates , certain . σ prior width, policy weight, social copying transmit . Kin selection predicted retard canalization (energy subsidies buffer cost exploration, maintaining high σ). effect negligible stable environment; kin altruism detectable effect σ dynamics parameter values. prediction reversed: epigenetics produced largest effect across four experiments. Social learning, predicted accelerate assimilation via rapid spread optimal policies, effect σ.

Low-σ solutions found — σ low 0.028 — every low-σ cell low genetic diversity (gd < 0.15 cells σ < 0.3). σ–gd correlation across archive +0.593. five highest-scoring cells (highest genetic diversity) σ > 0.40. low-σ + high-gd cell found anywhere archive. Low sigma found low genetic diversity (bottom-left region). bottom-right quadrant — low sigma + high diversity, required Baldwin Effect proper — empty. Interpretation. Baldwin Effect proper requires low σ coexisting high genetic diversity: genome converged specific adaptive weight configurations (canalization key loci) maintaining diversity others. Low σ archive entirely explained genetic drift fixation: small bottlenecked population becomes genetically homogeneous (low gd), heterozygosity collapses everywhere, driving σ toward zero byproduct fixation, selection canalized behaviour. pseudo-canalization via drift — looks like genetic assimilation σ readout alone, mechanism demographic, adaptive. what: Addendum: a calibrated regime where canalization emerges
title: The Baldwin Effect: within-lifetime learning accelerating evolution
text: five original experiments, ran clade’s built-CMA-ES search (clade::search_cmaes, driven harness dev/audit/calibration/) objective “negative slope mean_prior_sigma” — .e. reward parameter regime makes σ narrow. Full writeup dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md. search found regime produce canalization: resulting σ trajectory small consistently negative slope (observable fitness ratio 1.2× default baseline calibration report, sign-flip exploration-dominated regime). Canalization weaker Hinton & Nowlan’s prediction even specs, clade foraging world truly stable peak — shifting optimum driven population competition. result confirms canalization accessible; just requires scarcer resources slower within- lifetime learning displayed defaults. Biological reading: environments foraging costly learning slow, selection favours agents whose genome already encodes close--optimal policy. Agents wider priors (high σ) waste energy sampling low-reward actions outcompeted. abundant / fast-learning regime, high σ protective exploration finds good cells starvation. sign selection σ therefore hinges ratio exploration cost exploration benefit — exactly Mayley (1996) framing. five original experiments establish commonly used parameter defaults, canalization occur. addendum establishes canalization mechanistically impossible clade — just sits narrower parameter neighbourhood broader exploration regime.

what: A worked example: Hamilton’s rule under neonatal foraging deficit
title: Your first research project with clade
text: Let’s say specific question: Hamilton’s rule cooperation still predict evolution helping offspring unable forage full efficiency birth? neonatal foraging deficit hypothesis (Aiello & Wheeler 1995; Isler & van Schaik 2009) argues altricial offspring create energetic crisis makes parental kin-based provisioning essential. empirical question becomes: helping evolve readily neonatal deficit active? kind mechanistic question clade built . ’s ’d answer .

title: Your first research project with clade
text: Every research project start audited baseline. Browse Scenarios find closest match question. kin-selection question, start s-kin — already reproduces Hamilton’s rule default conditions. Always confirm baseline runs reproduces expected result modifying . Copy audit script dev/audit/fidelity/ directory GitHub want exact published configuration.
code: library(clade)  # The baseline: audited to reproduce Hamilton 1964 / Hamilton's rule specs <- default_specs() specs$kin_selection       <- TRUE specs$cooperation_evolution <- TRUE

title: Your first research project with clade
text: question, add neonatal foraging deficit. Consult Parameter reference exact flag name: Now create second specs list null comparison (deficit ):
code: # Active neonatal foraging deficit: young agents forage at 50% of adult rate # until they graduate at t_graduation ticks old specs$neonatal_foraging_deficit <- 0.5 specs$parental_care             <- TRUE    # required for offspring to survive the deficit specs_null            <- specs specs_null$neonatal_foraging_deficit <- 0.0 # `parental_care` can stay on to hold that variable constant

title: Your first research project with clade
text: single run never evidence. Use batch_alife() least 8 seeds pilot, 16–64 publication. Keep runs short debug, scale pipeline working. Important: mclapply Julia kernel don’t compose — PSOCK safe choice. batch_alife() handles internally; just set n_cores. see “connections use”, drop n_cores 64.
code: # Pilot: 8 seeds × 500 ticks per condition — takes a few minutes pilot_deficit <- batch_alife(   specs   = specs,   n_reps  = 8,   seeds   = 1:8,   n_cores = 8    # PSOCK backend; see `?batch_alife` )  pilot_null <- batch_alife(   specs   = specs_null,   n_reps  = 8,   seeds   = 1:8,   n_cores = 8 )

title: Your first research project with clade
text: interpreting result, confirm conditions produced populations actually persisted reproduced. simulation crashed 50 ticks signal report. look : Populations survive: final n_agents > 10 seeds. Heritability nonzero: trait variance didn’t collapse. Diversity positive: least genetic lineages remain. viability_report() flags failures, specs probably harsh (mortality high, food scarce) inconsistent (disease without recovery rate, kin selection without diploidy). See Troubleshooting common fixes.
code: # Attach run data and check rd_deficit <- lapply(pilot_deficit, get_run_data) rd_null    <- lapply(pilot_null, get_run_data)  via_deficit <- viability_report(rd_deficit) via_null    <- viability_report(rd_null)  print(via_deficit) print(via_null)

title: Your first research project with clade
text: Hamilton’s rule cooperation evolving — measure frequency helping-tendency alleles end run, compare conditions. Tier-C audit-style report, want |t| > 2 16+ seeds predicted direction. see |t| < 2 pilot 8, scale 16; ’s still borderline, scale 32. direction wrong, model telling something — don’t just chase bigger sample.
code: helper_freq <- function(rd) {   tail(rd$helper_tendency_mean, 1)  # final-tick mean of helper allele freq }  end_deficit <- sapply(rd_deficit, helper_freq) end_null    <- sapply(rd_null,    helper_freq)  # Welch's t-test across seeds t_result <- t.test(end_deficit, end_null) print(t_result)  # Effect size: Δ ± 2×SE delta <- mean(end_deficit) - mean(end_null) se_d  <- sqrt(var(end_deficit)/length(end_deficit) +               var(end_null)/length(end_null)) cat(sprintf("Δ helper-allele frequency = %+.3f ± %.3f (t = %+.2f)\n",             delta, 2 * se_d, t_result$statistic))

title: Your first research project with clade
text: Publication-quality figures show distribution, just means. Boxplots + jittered points beat bar charts. Save ggsave("hamiltons_rule_neonatal_deficit.pdf", width = 5, height = 4).
code: library(ggplot2)  df <- data.frame(   helper_freq = c(end_deficit, end_null),   condition   = rep(c("neonatal deficit", "no deficit"),                     each = length(end_deficit)) )  ggplot(df, aes(condition, helper_freq)) +   geom_boxplot(width = 0.3, outlier.shape = NA) +   geom_jitter(width = 0.08, alpha = 0.6) +   labs(     y = "Final helper-tendency allele frequency",     x = NULL,     title = "Does neonatal foraging deficit promote the evolution of helping?",     subtitle = sprintf("8 seeds per condition; Δ = %+.3f ± %.3f (t = %+.2f)",                        delta, 2 * se_d, t_result$statistic),     caption = "clade v0.5.18; specs: default_specs() + kin_selection + cooperation + parental_care"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: Your first research project with clade
text: Three sentences enough paper’s methods section: used clade (v0.5.18; Nakagawa 2026) simulate populations heritable-genome agents kin selection, cooperative tendency, parental care enabled. test whether neonatal foraging deficit (Aiello & Wheeler 1995) promotes evolution helping, ran two conditions — neonatal_foraging_deficit = 0.5 (altricial offspring) vs. 0 (deficit) — 8 seeds 500 ticks. Final helper-tendency allele frequencies differed Δ = +X.XX ± Y.YY (t = +Z.Z; p < 0.05), supporting prediction altriciality amplifies selection kin-based provisioning. Cite package: — importantly — cite biological theory ’re testing (Hamilton 1964; Aiello & Wheeler 1995). clade instrument; theory question.
code: @misc{clade2026,   author  = {Nakagawa, Shinichi},   title   = {clade: evolve behaviour, minds, and brains in R},   year    = {2026},   note    = {R package},   url     = {https://github.com/itchyshin/clade} }

title: Your first research project with clade
text: Getting started gets “ran baseline”. walkthrough added: Hypothesis framing: picking specific mechanism test writing code. Paired conditions: always null compute differential. Multi-seed sweep: batch_alife() ≥ 8 seeds; scale publication. Viability check: never interpret simulation didn’t actually persist. Differential statistic: Δ ± 2×SE t-statistic. Reporting “|t| > 2 predicted direction across N seeds” fidelity audits use, ’s defensible standard papers. Honest plotting: show distribution, just mean. next level — parameter sweeps, MAP-Elites, CMA-ES — see Parameter search guides. reproducing specific published prediction, start paper-reproduction template.

title: Your first research project with clade
text: Compare three conditions (e.g. low / medium / high deficit): three specs lists, three batch_alife() calls, ANOVA continuous regression. Use seasonal_amplitude want continuous environmental gradient. Ask parameter-search question (“deficit intensity maximally selects helping?”): use search_cmaes() helper frequency fitness function. Explore trait space (“combinations deficit, care, clutch size produce helping?”): use search_map_elites() tile trait space. Run worktree: reproducibility, tag specs list + clade version output .rds filename. Future-thank .

title: Getting started with clade
text: clade R package agent-based simulation evolution foraging ecology. Digital organisms carry heritable neural-network genomes (haploid diploid) one several brain types, evolve generations natural selection behaviour, life history, cognition. package designed evolutionary biologists want run controlled silico experiments without writing low-level simulation code. clade successor experimental alifeR package. alifeR embedded simulation hot path Rcpp, clade runs entire simulation Julia (via JuliaConnectoR). R–Julia boundary crossed per run_alife() call, per tick. 200 agents 1,000 ticks roughly 25× faster Rcpp-based alifeR backend, makes runs 100,000 agents practical. Julia backend also enables MAP-Elites quality-diversity search derivative-free optimisation (CMA-ES), neither feasible scale Rcpp simulator. vignette orientation, reference. introduces workflow, parameter list, brain types, biological modules can toggled . code chunks shown evaluated, Julia backend available package check vignette build.

title: Getting started with clade
text: default configuration small grid world single brain type core ecological loop. Three calls enough produce tidy result diagnostic plot: env clade_env object. print method gives one-line summary: plot_run() produces six-panel dashboard: population size, mean energy (±SD ribbon), genetic diversity, births deaths per tick, grass coverage, BNN prior sigma. get_run_data() returns list two tidy data frames: data$ticks — one row per logged tick, population-level summaries (mean energy, mean body size, genetic diversity, grass cover, births, deaths, column per active module). data$deaths — one row per agent death, cause, age, energy, final body size, lifetime offspring count. canonical inputs every analysis function package.
code: library(clade)  specs <- default_specs()  # Modules are off by default; enable any combination independently, e.g.: # specs$disease <- TRUE # specs$body_size_evolution <- TRUE  env  <- run_alife(specs) data <- get_run_data(env)  plot_run(data) print(env) #> clade_env  [500 ticks | 193 agents | genetic_diversity: 0.319]

title: Getting started with clade
text: default_specs() returns long named list (roughly 90 parameters 0.4.0). see changed, use print_specs(diff_only = TRUE): full parameter listing types defaults, see vignette("parameter-reference") help page ?default_specs.
code: specs <- default_specs() specs$kin_selection     <- TRUE specs$complex_landscape <- TRUE specs$max_ticks         <- 400L  print_specs(specs, diff_only = TRUE) #> -- clade specs (~90 parameters) [diff only] -- #> #>   Grid & population #>     max_ticks                              400 * #> #>   Kin selection #>     kin_selection                         TRUE * #> #>   Complex landscape #>     complex_landscape                     TRUE *

what: Simulation timescale: choosing the right spec preset
title: Getting started with clade
text: single important choice designing clade run many generations simulate. Fisher (1930) showed beneficial allele needs roughly 1/s1/s generations fix, ss selection coefficient. modest selection (s≈0.05–0.10s \approx 0.05\text{–}0.10), 20–100 generations. Weaker selection needs hundreds. default_specs(), generation time roughly max_age / 2 ≈ 100 ticks plus maturation delay, effective generation time ~190 ticks. 500-tick run therefore ~2.6 generations — short see evolutionary dynamics realistic selection strengths. single largest source “weak effect” reports early clade audits. package ships three presets make trade-explicit: fast_specs() achieves short generation time lowering max_age 30 ticks min_repro_energy 60 (versus 200 120 default). MATLAB ancestor clade (Bulitko 2023) ran — agents matured quickly turned fast, exactly gives selection enough time work. Rule thumb. phenomenon want see requires allele change (heritable trait evolution, genetic assimilation, cooperation signal elaboration), use fast_specs(). demographic ecological phenomenon unfolds within single generation (predator-prey cycles, disease spread, seasonal grass dynamics), default_specs() fine. See dev/docs/timescale-analysis.md biological reference table (E. coli elephant) full MATLAB-ancestor comparison motivated presets.
code: # Evolutionary scenario: use fast_specs evo <- fast_specs()               # max_ticks = 2000, ~66 generations evo$phenotypic_plasticity <- TRUE run_alife(evo)  # Within-generation ecology / LV demo: default_specs is appropriate ecol <- default_specs() ecol$max_ticks <- 500L ecol$n_predators_init <- 5L run_alife(ecol)

title: Getting started with clade

Two names reserved future implementation currently error requested: "transformer" (self-attention) "synthesis" (symbolic rule extraction evolved weights). "bnn" default admits evolvable prior width captures Baldwin-style learning–evolution interactions (Baldwin 1896, Mouret & Clune 2015) cleanly. 0.4.0, BNN’s prior-width can drawn heterozygosity (legacy), fixed value, evolved plasticity trait — see bnn_sigma_source ?default_specs.

title: Getting started with clade
text: body_size_evolution = TRUE, agents evolve heritable body size trait. Larger agents eat per grass cell pay higher metabolic surcharge; smaller agents eat less cheaper run. Reference size 1.0 metabolic correction. trait inherited via genome bounded body_size_min (default 0.3) body_size_max (default 3.0).
code: specs                       <- default_specs() specs$body_size_evolution   <- TRUE specs$body_size_init_mean   <- 1.0 specs$body_size_mutation_sd <- 0.08  env  <- run_alife(specs) data <- get_run_data(env)  # Time series of mean and SD of body size head(data$ticks[, c("t", "n_agents", "mean_body_size", "sd_body_size")])

title: Getting started with clade
text: dispersal_evolution = TRUE, agents evolve heritable probability taking one step away birthplace tick. Dispersal reduces inbreeding kin competition cost dispersal_cost energy.
code: specs                     <- default_specs() specs$dispersal_evolution <- TRUE specs$dispersal_init_mean <- 0.2  env  <- run_alife(specs) data <- get_run_data(env)  # Total dispersal events sum(data$ticks$n_dispersal_events)

title: Getting started with clade
text: produce SIR-style epidemic population, set disease flag use dedicated plotting helper: plot_disease_dynamics() reads n_infected n_new_infections columns data$ticks overlays susceptible, infected, recovered classes time. transmission probability, infectious period, immune duration controlled default_specs().
code: specs         <- default_specs() specs$disease <- TRUE env           <- run_alife(specs) data          <- get_run_data(env)  plot_disease_dynamics(data)

title: Getting started with clade
text: batch_alife() runs list specs across R worker processes. recommended way compare parameter regimes, since Julia process stays warm slice batch: systematic parameter exploration (CMA-ES, MAP-Elites, viability search), see parameter search guide.
code: specs_list <- lapply(c(0.05, 0.10, 0.20), function(gr) {   s <- default_specs()   s$grass_rate <- gr   s$max_ticks  <- 200L   s })  results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 3L)  # Compare mean genetic diversity across grass rates sapply(results, function(env) {   mean(get_run_data(env)$ticks$genetic_diversity, na.rm = TRUE) })

title: Getting started with clade
text: clade crosses R–Julia boundary exactly per run_alife() call, regardless number ticks number agents. entire tick loop — sensing, neural network forward pass, action selection, movement, eating, reproduction, death — runs inside single Julia process. 200 agents 1,000 ticks roughly 25× faster Rcpp-based alifeR backend, scales smoothly populations ~100,000 agents workstation. first call R session pays one-start-cost (~60–90 s) Julia compilation. Subsequent calls reuse warm Julia session return results immediately.

Biology. corresponding biological process real organisms. Citations apply. Audit findings. learned fidelity audit (35 scenarios, see dev/audit/fidelity/STATUS.md) whether rule produces predicted behaviour. Skipped rule never tested. Variants worth considering. Alternative biological rules also defensible, trade-offs.

title: Kernel as biology — overview
text: tick.jl chapter — Per-tick agent update: sense → decide → move → eat → age. hot path. Start . death.jl chapter, genome.jl chapter)*

title: Kernel as biology — overview
text: need handful Julia idioms read documents: function name!(env) — trailing ! Julia convention meaning “function modifies argument place” (mutates rather returning new value). Python’s list.append() vs sorted(list). ag env.agents — iterate every agent. ag loop variable (one agent time). ag.alive || continue — read “alive, skip agent.” || short-circuit ; continue jumps next iteration. shorthand “skip dead agents.” ag.energy -= 1.0f0 — subtract 1.0 energy field. f0 means “Float32” (single precision); just read 1.0. Int32(0) — explicit type cast. Read just 0. Float32(get(specs, "move_cost", 1.0)) — look "move_cost" specs dictionary; absent, use 1.0. cast Float32. Read “move_cost parameter (default 1.0).” inp = sense_agent(ag, env) — call function sense_agent arguments ag env, store result inp. min(grass[x, y], max_bite) — take whichever smaller. ... elseif ... else ... end — Python’s /elif/else. ’s enough read 90% kernel. Ask anything else opaque.

what: Known kernel properties that affect audits
title: Kernel as biology — overview
text: Three cross-cutting findings 2026-04 audit cycle users know interpreting scenario whose claim depends diploid sex, BNN posterior sigma, within-lifetime learning. documented detail relevant chapter (linked), plus dev/audit/fidelity/ reports. Default reproduction used structurally asexual every ploidy-2 scenario. _find_mate short-circuited nothing whenever signal_dims = 0 (default). Offspring produced empty paternal haplotype, making effectively haploid. Fixed 0.5.10. See reproduce.jl §“Mate finding” genome.jl §6.5. Consequence: pre-0.5.10 audits heritability, heterozygosity, pedigree-kin, sex-vs-asex measuring haploid mechanics diploid label. 0.5.11 re-audit 12 diploid-sensitive ✅ scenarios found 11 still hold one demoted (stress-hypermutation). BNN posterior sigma couples genome heterozygosity behaviour. "heterozygosity" mode (default), sigma = |mat − pat| / 2 weight — genetically diverse agents act variably. Pre-0.5.5 fully coupled three channels (action noise, learning rate, posterior uncertainty) made Baldwin / plasticity claims untestable. Now decoupled via bnn_action_noise_scale bnn_sigma_lr_scale, Hinton-Nowlan prediction testable — direction clade comes REVERSED DeWitt 2004 (stable > seasonal) tested seasonal amplitudes. s-plasticity s-baldwin sit honest 🟠 diagnosis. See genome.jl §5 BNN-sigma--heterozygosity. Real diploid sex currently viability-negative default reproduction costs. 0.5.10 re-audit mating-systems found real sex enabled, 20/32 seeds crash sex-vs-asex comparison shows Δn = −88 t = −29. default repro_cost parameters implicitly tuned effective asex (pre-0.5.10 default). Sex-cost calibration open design question — see genome.jl §6.5 “Variants worth considering”. aren’t errors user “work around”; ’re kernel properties determine audit claims defensible need re-interpretation. STATUS.md ledger individual fidelity reports already reflect three.

text: says. Run per-agent tick logic every prey agent (documented detail tick.jl chapter). run predator update loop. Biology. per-tick action lives. prey agent senses, decides, moves, eats, pays metabolic costs. predator plus attack logic. See tick.jl chapter full breakdown. ordering — prey first, predators — matters: means predators react post-move prey positions, pre-move. Real predator-prey interactions messier (moving simultaneously), discrete-tick model order picked. Putting predators second models predators faster decision-makers, biologically reasonable ambush predators.

title: Clade.jl — the main loop, in biological order
text: says. series optional modifications applied basic agent tick. -op unless flag . Examples: apply_body_size! corrects metabolic cost based agent’s evolved body size. apply_brain_size_evolution! adds expensive-brain surcharge. apply_dispersal! performs additional movement based evolved dispersal trait. apply_toxicity_costs! deducts metabolic cost producing toxins (mimicry scenario). Biology. call corresponds specific biological hypothesis can turned independently. structure layered: core biology runs tick_agents!; trait-evolution modules add post-hoc corrections. kernel architecture choice biological consequences: Pro: modules can tested isolation; turning flag doesn’t require kernel changes. Con: modules can interact unintended ways. body_size metabolic_rate correct movement cost, order correction matters. Audit findings. Body-size audit found predation produces smaller bodies, larger, opposite Shine 2011 cane-toad prediction. Diagnosis: clade’s predator detection signal-strength based (larger prey detectable), predators preferentially remove largest agents. different biological prediction Shine’s, valid, audit-flagged vignette prose update.
code: apply_body_size!(env)             # metabolic + foraging correction apply_brain_size_evolution!(env)  # expensive brain + cognitive foraging apply_ann_regularization!(env)    # L1/L0 weight complexity penalty apply_dispersal!(env)             # natal dispersal away from birthplace apply_habitat_preference!(env)    # secondary move toward preferred habitat apply_seasonal_mortality!(env)    # winter death probability apply_toxicity_costs!(env)        # mimicry: per-tick toxicity energy cost apply_signal_costs!(env)          # signal evolution: per-tick signal cost apply_signal_evolution!(env)      # signal drift mutation (when enabled)

text: says. Run optional biology module turn. module’s flag , returns immediately without anything. Biology. extensions library. Disease (SIR), kin altruism (Hamilton 1964), inclusive fitness, scavenging, public-goods cooperation, epigenetic inheritance, parental care, cooperative breeding — separate biological theory implemented opt-module. order encodes biological assumptions timing within tick: Disease seeds tick 1, transmits/recovers tick . Kin altruism happens agent tick (donors made move consumed food, check relative needs help). Scavenging carrion decay paired — agents consume carrion first, remaining carrion decays. (Reverse order mean carrion always decays eaten — biologically odd.) Parental care three sub-steps: cost (parent pays), feeding (juvenile gains), aging (juvenile gets older). Order: cost → feed → age means parent pays juvenile benefits, realistic flow. Audit findings. passed cleanly (kin ρ=0.97, cooperation ρ=1.00, disease ρ=1.00, niche +22 agents, scavenging +5 agents). didn’t (mimicry scalar memory, parental- investment coupling) separate kernel issues already 0.4.0 backlog.

previous_headings: death.jl — when agents die and why
title: death.jl — when agents die and why
text: says. Read four mortality parameters top: starvation_threshold — die energy falls value (default 0). max_age — die age reaches value (default 200). senescence_rate — Gompertz mortality coefficient (default 0, meaning ). life_history — "iteroparous" (default) "semelparous". latter triggers post-reproduction death. scavenging — whether deposit carrion agent dies. Biology. demographic accounting. Every population biology model decide dies . clade exposes four orthogonal mortality processes user can combine: Starvation = ecological mortality (resource limitation). Age cap = hard maximum lifespan (simplification — real animals don’t fixed cap, just vanishing survival probability). Senescence = Gompertz mortality (Gompertz 1825) — exponentially rising death probability age, standard demographic model vertebrate aging. Semelparous = post-reproductive death (salmon, annual plants, insects). default starvation age cap active. Senescence semelparous opt-. Audit findings. s-pace--life ✗ flagged always-max_age = 200 cap masks pace--life dynamics — every agent dies cap metabolic-rate-driven differences accumulate. Tier 2 fix planned: scale max_age metabolic_rate fast-pace species shorter lifespans (Réale 2010).
code: function kill_dead!(env::Environment)     specs     = env.specs     starv_th  = Float32(get(specs, "starvation_threshold", 0.0))     max_age   = Int(get(specs, "max_age", 200))     senes_r   = Float32(get(specs, "senescence_rate", 0.0))     semel     = get(specs, "life_history", "iteroparous") == "semelparous"     scav_on   = Bool(get(specs, "scavenging", false))

text: says. living agent: ask _death_cause whether agent dies tick (cause). yes: Mark agent dead. Update global death cause-specific counters. Log death record (id, age, energy, cause, body size, lifetime offspring). scavenging , leave carrion deposit agent’s cell. Biology. death decision per-tick, per-agent, deterministic given agent’s state parameters (randomness Gompertz senescence). death record retained env.deaths serves analyses like life-table reconstruction fitness analysis. carrion deposit natural consequence death scavenging ecosystems — corpses resources agents (DeVault 2003). Without scavenging, carcass “lost system” — biological simplification (real ecosystems decomposers always recycle energy). Audit findings. s-scavenging ✅: carrion deposition + decay sustains population +5 agents scarcity. Mechanism wired biologically correct.
code: for ag in env.agents     ag.alive || continue     cause = _death_cause(ag, starv_th, max_age, senes_r, semel, env.rng)     if cause != :alive         ag.alive = false         env.n_deaths += Int32(1)         cause == :starvation && (env.n_starvations += Int32(1))         cause == :age        && (env.n_age_deaths  += Int32(1))         _log_death!(env, ag, cause)         scav_on && deposit_carrion!(env, ag)     end end

what: 3. The cause function — applied in priority order
title: death.jl — when agents die and why
text: says. Check death causes priority order. Return first one fires: Starvation — energy threshold. Age cap — age reached maximum. Gompertz senescence — random draw age-dependent death probability. Semelparous — agent reproduced tick life history set semelparous. none fires, return :alive. Biology. four causes encode four distinct biological processes:
code: function _death_cause(ag::Agent, starv_th::Float32, max_age::Int,                        senes_r::Float32, semel::Bool, rng)::Symbol     # 1. Starvation     ag.energy < starv_th && return :starvation      # 2. Age cap     ag.age >= max_age && return :age      # 3. Gompertz senescence     if senes_r > 0.0f0         # Scaled by heritable aging_rate: faster-aging genotypes die sooner         eff_r = senes_r * ag.aging_rate         p_die = Float32(1.0 - exp(-Float64(eff_r) * exp(Float64(eff_r) * Float64(ag.age))))         rand(rng) < p_die && return :senescence     end      # 4. Semelparous     semel && ag.reproduced && return :semelparous      :alive end

text: Gompertz mortality law: per-tick death probability rises exponentially age. formula : r senescence_rate × aging_rate aging_rate agent’s heritable trait. Biology. standard demographic model vertebrate aging (Gompertz 1825 — yes, old). exponential shape captures empirical observation mortality rises 8-10% per year adult humans. Senescence evolved — natural selection’s force weakens age (Hamilton 1966, J. Theor. Biol. 12:12-45), deleterious late-life mutations accumulate. clade lets senescence_rate population-level constant aging_rate per-agent heritable trait — together implement evolved senescence. Audit findings. Senescence default (senescence_rate = 0). user enables formula works correctly. bottleneck s-pace--life — ’s always-max_age cap, even senescence .

code: function _log_death!(env::Environment, ag::Agent, cause::Symbol)     push!(env.deaths["id"],        Int(ag.id))     push!(env.deaths["t"],         Int(env.t))     push!(env.deaths["age"],       Int(ag.age))     push!(env.deaths["energy"],    Float64(ag.energy))     push!(env.deaths["cause"],     string(cause))     push!(env.deaths["body_size"], Float64(ag.body_size))     push!(env.deaths["num_offspring"], Int(ag.num_offspring)) end

Keeping separate makes effects testable independently.

text: biggest file kernel (~540 lines). implements genetic layer simulation — structures operations determine heritable information passes parents offspring. clade’s genome model unusually elaborate evolutionary ABM: implements proper diploid meiosis chromosomes, recombination, independent assortment, mutation, configurable dominance. “intentional improvement vs alifeR” change documented baseline three-way audit — alifeR used flat-weight inheritance, clade textbook Mendelian-Morgan genetics. file long, document walks conceptual structure rather line--line. kernel-0.4.0 changelog describes future edits detail. what: 1. The DiploidGenome struct — what an agent inherits
title: genome.jl — meiosis, traits, inheritance
text: (Defined types.jl, reproduced conceptually .) says. agent genome consisting two “strands” brain weights two strands scalar traits, plus metadata (network architecture, chromosome count). haploid agents (ploidy = 1), paternal strands empty — agents inherit one strand one parent. Biology. diploid genome standard biological form. maternal/paternal split central object Mendelian genetics. Two strands per locus → two alleles → potential heterozygosity, dominance, recombination. 15 scalar traits heritable phenotypic traits independent brain — body size, metabolic rate, aging rate, cooperation level, dispersal tendency, mutation rate, plasticity, etc. give clade richer genome just “brain weights” — biological scenarios need heritable life-history trait one evolve. n_chromosomes parameter sets many independently-recombining segments brain genome split . n_chromosomes = 1, weights recombine together (low effective recombination). n_chromosomes = 16, weights group 16 unlinked clusters. gives user control genetic architecture brain.
code: struct DiploidGenome     maternal_weights ::Vector{Float32}   # one set of brain weights from mother     paternal_weights ::Vector{Float32}   # one set from father (empty if haploid)     maternal_traits  ::Vector{Float32}   # 15 scalar traits from mother     paternal_traits  ::Vector{Float32}   # 15 from father (empty if haploid)     architecture     ::Tuple             # ANN structure (input, hidden, output sizes)     n_chromosomes    ::Int               # how many separately-recombining segments end

what: 2. Trait initialisation — drawing the founding population’s genes

text: says. 15 traits, check whether evolution flag . , sample initial value truncated normal (mean = init, sd = mutation_sd, clamped [min, max]). , use fixed default value (often 1.0 0.0). Biology. founder population sampling. initial agents heritable variation traits user activated; unused traits “biologically neutral” default (e.g., metabolic rate 1.0 = baseline, body size 1.0 = reference, cooperation 0.0 = cooperation). truncated normal standard model biological trait distributions — traits cluster around mean, spread, biological min/max bounds (can’t negative body size). Audit findings. Two scenarios flagged layer: s-mutation-rate (audit yet): TRAIT_MUTATION_SD field evolvable mutation_rate_evolution = TRUE, allowing populations evolve mutation rate (Sniegowski et al. 1997). s-toxicity (mimicry audit): TRAIT_TOXICITY found surprisingly insensitive selection default parameters — related scalar predator memory issue, trait initialisation.

title: genome.jl — meiosis, traits, inheritance
text: says. haploid parent: copy single strand weights, mutate , return. diploid parent: n_chromosomes chunks weights: Flip coin decide strand (maternal paternal) start — independent assortment (Mendel’s second law). Sample Poisson number crossover positions. Walk chromosome, switching strands crossover. chromosomes assembled, mutate whole gamete Gaussian noise. Biology. textbook diploid meiosis (Charlesworth & Charlesworth 2010, Chapter 5). three classical steps: Independent assortment — chromosome chooses parent strand independently. Mendel discovered pea plants (second law). Recombination / crossover — within chromosome, gamete alternates two parental strands random crossover points. shuffles linked genes within generation. Poisson model rate crossover_rate standard continuous-time approximation. Mutation — recombination, Gaussian noise added every weight. generates new variation natural selection acts . biologically realistic part kernel clade-specific improvement alifeR (used flat-weight crossover chromosome structure). Audit findings. s-pop-genetics ✅ — heritability proxy (lag-1 autocorrelation mean body_size) 0.992, exactly working Mendelian inheritance system produce. Variants worth considering. Sex chromosomes — currently sex-linked inheritance. Adding “sex chromosome” (one copy males, two females, hemizygous expression) let clade test sex-linked trait scenarios. Variable crossover rate — currently constant per simulation. Real species variable recombination rates; heritable crossover_rate trait small extension. Inversions — large structural rearrangements suppress recombination locally. Important many real-species scenarios (e.g., supergenes). implemented.
code: function meiosis(parent::DiploidGenome, specs, rng)::Vector{Float32}     if is_haploid(parent)         return _mutate_weights(copy(parent.maternal_weights), specs, rng)     end      n       = length(parent.maternal_weights)     gamete  = Vector{Float32}(undef, n)     nc      = Int(parent.n_chromosomes)     seg_len = div(n, nc)      λ = Float64(get(specs, "crossover_rate", 1.0))      for c in 1:nc         lo = (c - 1) * seg_len + 1         hi = c == nc ? n : c * seg_len          # Choose starting strand by independent assortment         using_mat = rand(rng) < 0.5          # Sample crossover positions (Poisson number)         k = _rpois(rng, λ)         xpts = sort!(rand(rng, lo:hi, k))          pos = lo         for xp in xpts             src = using_mat ? parent.maternal_weights : parent.paternal_weights             gamete[pos:xp] .= src[pos:xp]             pos = xp + 1             using_mat = !using_mat         end         # Fill remainder of segment         src = using_mat ? parent.maternal_weights : parent.paternal_weights         gamete[pos:hi] .= src[pos:hi]     end      _mutate_weights(gamete, specs, rng) end

what: 4. Trait inheritance — separate logic for scalar traits
title: genome.jl — meiosis, traits, inheritance
text: says. scalar traits (body size, metabolic rate, etc.), inheritance simpler: trait independently chooses one two parent alleles, gets mutated. chromosomes, recombination — trait unlinked locus. Biology. 15 scalar traits treated 15 independent loci. simplification — real organisms many traits share chromosomes (linked, recombining together). clade’s purposes (testing single-trait evolutionary predictions), unlinked inheritance cleaner trait can evolve independently without genetic correlations linkage. simplification biologically defensible scalar traits interpretable (maps specific phenotype documented vignettes/parameter-reference.Rmd). linked, evolutionary correlations confounded. Variants worth considering. Linkage groups traits — bundle related traits (e.g., metabolic_rate + aging_rate “pace--life chromosome”) evolve correlated fashion. let clade test whether pace--life syndromes (Réale 2010) emerge genetic architecture.
code: function meiosis_traits(parent, specs, rng)::Vector{Float32}     if is_haploid(parent)         return _mutate_traits(copy(parent.maternal_traits), specs, rng)     end      # Each trait inherits from a randomly chosen parent allele     gamete = Vector{Float32}(undef, N_SCALAR_TRAITS)     for i in 1:N_SCALAR_TRAITS         gamete[i] = rand(rng) < 0.5 ?                     parent.maternal_traits[i] : parent.paternal_traits[i]     end     _mutate_traits(gamete, specs, rng) end

title: genome.jl — meiosis, traits, inheritance
text: says. Convert diploid genome (two strands) single phenotype (one set weights actually used brain). Three modes: Haploid — phenotype just maternal strand (choice needed). Dominant — weight, randomly pick maternal paternal. Implements random-allele-expression interpretation dominance. Additive (default) — phenotype average maternal paternal. Implements codominant / additive model standard quantitative genetics. Biology. Dominance one central concepts Mendelian genetics. real organisms: traits dominant/recessive (one allele’s phenotype dominates). codominant (alleles contribute, additive phenotype). intermediate (incomplete dominance). clade lets user pick model. Default "additive" quantitative-genetics standard traits many small contributing loci (Falconer & Mackay 1996). Brain weights explicitly quantitative — additive right default. BNN brain special interpretation diploid genome: maternal paternal weights interpreted “two estimates optimal weight”, BNN sigma (uncertainty) computed difference (heterozygosity). couples genetic diversity behavioural flexibility. Audit findings (updated 2026-04-18, 0.5.11). BNN-sigma--heterozygosity coupling central mechanism four separate audit findings worth stating together: Pre-0.4.0 (🔴 Baldwin). earliest kernel, sigma computed heterozygosity controlled () action noise (b) effective learning rate bnn_update!. full coupling, stable-env selection narrower sigma (Baldwin canalization) fought behavioural-variance cost, net direction wrong. Addressed 0.5.5 / 0.5.6 bnn_action_noise_scale bnn_sigma_lr_scale specs let two channels decoupled. Pre-0.5.10 (silent haploid conversion). _find_mate short-circuited signal_dims = 0, every default-config diploid run produced offspring pat_w = Float32[] → is_haploid(g) == true → sigma = fill(bnn_sigma_init, n) weights, regardless selection. BNN-sigma-- heterozygosity expression wired correctly ploidy pipeline upstream secretly converting everything haploid. Fixed 0.5.10 (_find_mate rewrite + mate_search_radius spec). Post-0.5.10 direction. ploidy fix , mean_prior_sigma now evolves bnn_sigma_init selection-drift equilibrium (~0.33). Direction however REVERSED canonical DeWitt / Hinton-Nowlan prediction: tested parameters, stable envs maintain heterozygosity seasonal envs (t = −1.41 direction, stable > seasonal). code: function express_weights(g::DiploidGenome, dominance_model::String, rng)     if is_haploid(g) || dominance_model == "dominant"         if is_haploid(g)             return copy(g.maternal_weights)         end         # Dominant: random allele per weight         n = length(g.maternal_weights)         result = Vector{Float32}(undef, n)         for i in 1:n             result[i] = rand(rng) < 0.5 ?                         g.maternal_weights[i] : g.paternal_weights[i]         end         return result     end     # Additive (default): midpoint of maternal and paternal     return (g.maternal_weights .+ g.paternal_weights) ./ 2.0f0 end

code: function _mutate_weights(w::Vector{Float32}, specs, rng)     sd = Float32(get(specs, "mutation_sd", 0.1))     sd > 0.0f0 || return w     @inbounds for i in eachindex(w)         w[i] += sd * randn(rng)     end     w end

code: function make_offspring_genome(parent1::DiploidGenome,                                parent2::Union{DiploidGenome, Nothing},                                specs::Dict{String,Any},                                rng::AbstractRNG)::DiploidGenome     mat_w = meiosis(parent1, specs, rng)     mat_t = meiosis_traits(parent1, specs, rng)      selfing = Bool(get(specs, "self_fertilization_fallback", false))     if specs["ploidy"] == 2 && parent2 !== nothing         pat_w = meiosis(parent2, specs, rng)         pat_t = meiosis_traits(parent2, specs, rng)     elseif specs["ploidy"] == 2 && selfing         pat_w = meiosis(parent1, specs, rng)         pat_t = meiosis_traits(parent1, specs, rng)     else         pat_w = Float32[]      # <-- effectively haploid offspring         pat_t = Float32[]     end     DiploidGenome(mat_w, pat_w, mat_t, pat_t,                   parent1.architecture, parent1.n_chromosomes) end

text: says. Compute normalised Euclidean distance two agents’ expressed weight phenotypes. Used : Speciation module (reproductive isolation distance > threshold). Diversity tracking genetic_diversity reporting. Social learning (preferentially copy genetically similar individuals — distant ones, depending parameter). Biology. Genetic distance foundational metric population genetics. many measures (Nei’s distance, Fst, Wright’s, etc.); clade uses Euclidean expressed weights, appropriate quantitative-trait genomes (Lynch & Walsh 1998). 1/sqrt(n) normalisation makes distance comparable across networks different sizes — important hidden_layers varies across runs. code: function genome_distance(g1::DiploidGenome, g2::DiploidGenome,                           dominance_model::String = "additive")::Float32     p1 = express_weights(g1, dominance_model, default_rng())     p2 = express_weights(g2, dominance_model, default_rng())     n  = max(length(p1), length(p2))     n == 0 && return 0.0f0     sqrt(sum(abs2, p1 .- p2)) / sqrt(Float32(n)) end

Proportional cost is the biological default per     # Smith & Fretwell (1974) and all life-history theory: parents in     # better condition have more to invest. Fixed-cost mode preserved for     # reproducibility of pre-0.4.0 runs.     repro_cost_mode = String(get(specs, "repro_cost_mode", "proportional"))     repro_cost      = Float32(get(specs, "repro_cost",          30.0))     repro_cost_frac = Float32(get(specs, "repro_cost_fraction", 0.5))     off_energy_base = Float32(get(specs, "offspring_energy",    60.0))     off_energy_mode = String(get(specs, "offspring_energy_mode", "proportional"))     off_energy_frac = Float32(get(specs, "offspring_energy_fraction", 0.25))     max_ag    = Int(get(specs, "max_agents",           500))     care      = Bool(get(specs, "parental_care",       false))     allee_th      = Int(get(specs, "allee_threshold",  0))     min_repro_age = Int32(get(specs, "min_repro_age",  0))

Biology. Four biological gates: Alive. Dead agents don’t reproduce. already reproduced tick. agent reproduces per tick. (semelparous species reproduced flag used death.jl kill parent next tick.) Age threshold. min_repro_age, reproduction. Default 0 (clade) — age can reproduce — differs ancestors (MATLAB alifeR required minimum age). simplification can reversed user. Real animals universally juvenile period. Energy threshold. Must least effective_repro_threshold energy. effective_ prefix means plasticity-adjusted: phenotypic_plasticity = TRUE, threshold can vary per agent. Allee effect (optional, default). critical neighbour density, reproduction stops. Models species depend group cohesion breeding (Allee 1931). Audit findings. s-life-history audit ✅ verified eligibility logic. Semelparous iteroparous strategies produce distinct demographic signatures (mean age 13 vs 102, births 4.2 vs 0.9 per tick). reproduced flag interaction death.jl’s semelparous trigger wired correctly. text: says. Determine clutch size reproduction event. clutch_size_evolution , sample truncated normal (mean = init_mean, sd = mutation_sd, clamped [clutch_size_min, clutch_size_max]). Otherwise use fixed max_clutch_size. Update bookkeeping counters. Biology. Clutch size one -studied life-history traits (Lack 1947, Ibis 89:302-352). trade-: many small offspring vs large ones. Lack proposed optimal clutch size one maximises raised offspring (just born). clade’s implementation lets clutch size evolve heritable trait, sampled per parent per reproduction event. Truncated-normal sampling introduces stochastic variation — biologically reasonable, since real clutch sizes vary among individuals even identical genotypes. Audit findings. s-clutch-size ✅ recovered bell-shaped r/K response: clutch rises grass_rate (r-arm) 0.15, falls population hits cap density-dependent K-selection takes . Clean recovery MacArthur-Wilson r/K theory population level. Variants worth considering. Iteroparous birth distribution — currently every agent gives birth full clutch one tick, waits reproduced flag clear next tick. Real iteroparous species often spread births across days/weeks within season. Maternal effects — clutch size real species influenced maternal condition, age, even prior reproductive history. Currently genome-encoded clutch trait matters.
code: clutch = if Bool(get(specs, "clutch_size_evolution", false))     lo = Int(get(specs, "clutch_size_min", 1))     hi = Int(get(specs, "clutch_size_max", 5))     mu = Float64(get(specs, "clutch_size_init_mean", 1.0))     sd = Float64(get(specs, "clutch_size_mutation_sd", 0.3))     clamp(round(Int, mu + randn(env.rng) * sd), lo, hi) else     Int(get(specs, "max_clutch_size", 1)) end env.n_repro_events += Int32(1) env.n_clutch_total += Int32(clutch)

text: says. offspring clutch: Find mate (set mate nothing asexual reproduction). Compute cost_paid — either fixed repro_cost repro_cost_fraction * parent_energy, depending mode. Deduct parent’s energy. mate present, deduct half mate . Compute offspring’s starting energy — either fixed offspring_energy offspring_energy_fraction * cost_paid, per second mode flag. Biology. parental investment trade-— mathematical core life-history theory. Smith & Fretwell (1974) showed optimal allocation parental resources depends marginal fitness gain per unit invested single offspring. practice means: Parents better condition can invest total per-offspring. unit investment yields diminishing returns offspring fitness — single super-rich offspring isn’t necessarily fit two moderately-rich ones. 0.4.0 default ("proportional") implements directly: cost_paid = 0.5 * parent.energy (default repro_cost_fraction = 0.5) offspring_energy = 0.25 * cost_paid (default offspring_energy_fraction = 0.25) 75% cost “lost development” — eggshells, gestation inefficiency, placental drain, etc. 25-40% conversion efficiency documented many vertebrates. mate paying half cost models biparental investment — stable many vertebrates birds. Asexual single-parent species get full cost. Audit findings. Pre-0.4.0, s-parental-investment audit 🟠 female_investment didn’t couple outcomes. 0.4.0 proportional cost, — cost_paid chain off_energy_actual. Re-audit pending. Variants worth considering. Asymmetric mate cost — currently mate pays exactly half. species different investments (e.g., brood-parasitic birds: mate pays 0%; eusocial workers: queen pays 100%). Quality-vs-quantity selection — fixed cost_paid, varying offspring_energy_fraction upward produces fewer high-quality offspring (since cost > offspring energy means “lost development” inefficiency rises). testable Smith-Fretwell knob.
code: for _ in 1:clutch     length(env.agents) + length(new_agents) >= max_ag && break      # Find mate (or reproduce asexually)     mate = _find_mate(ag, env)      # 0.4.0: parental cost     #   "fixed"        — deduct constant `repro_cost` (legacy)     #   "proportional" — deduct `repro_cost_fraction * parent.energy`     #                    (Smith & Fretwell 1974; default in 0.4.0)     cost_paid = if repro_cost_mode == "proportional"         repro_cost_frac * ag.energy     else         repro_cost     end     ag.energy -= cost_paid     mate !== nothing && (mate.energy -= cost_paid * 0.5f0)      # 0.4.0: offspring birth energy     #   "fixed"        — every newborn starts with `offspring_energy`     #                    (legacy; ignores parent condition)     #   "proportional" — newborn starts with `offspring_energy_fraction     #                    * cost_paid` per Smith-Fretwell quality-quantity     #                    (default in 0.4.0)     off_energy_actual = if off_energy_mode == "proportional"         off_energy_frac * cost_paid     else         off_energy_base     end

code: function _find_mate(ag::Agent, env::Environment)::Union{Agent, Nothing}     specs = env.specs     if specs["ploidy"] == 1         return nothing     # haploid: asexual by genome     end     radius = Int(get(specs, "mate_search_radius", 1))     radius = max(radius, 1)     # ... collect live neighbours within Moore radius ...     if Int(get(specs, "signal_dims", 0)) == 0         return candidates[rand(env.rng, 1:length(candidates))]     end     # signal_dims > 0: pick best signal-preference match (Zahavi 1975)     # ... end

title: sense.jl — what an agent perceives
text: says. Every agent gets exactly input format — 11 numbers (default), [0, 1]. brain wired expect vector length. Optional modules append extra inputs, brain’s input layer sized accordingly construction time. Biology. perceptual world agent — ethologists call Umwelt (von Uexküll 1934). Every species ; clade gives agents one evolutionary ecology rarely models perception detail. 11-input default represents: 2 internal signals (energy, age — agent knows state) 8 external signals (4 grass intensities + 4 conspecific presences, one per cardinal direction) 1 constant bias (lets network learn unconditional preferences) vector small ML standards (typical RL networks consume hundreds inputs). smallness intentional: small input vector interpretable selection acts tractable space.
code: Index  Content -----  ------- 1      own_energy / energy_max          (normalised [0, 1]) 2      own_age / max_age                (normalised [0, 1]) 3–6    grass N/E/S/W at distance 1 7–10   agent presence N/E/S/W at distance 1 11     constant bias term = 1.0  Optional extensions (appended in order): + predators (n_predators_init > 0):  +4 predator-distance inputs + parental_care = TRUE:               +2 care-load inputs + signal_dims > 0:                    +signal_dims inputs  Default total: 11 inputs. With predators: 15. With predators + signals (3 dim) + care: 20.

title: sense.jl — what an agent perceives

Real animals hunger, thirst, fatigue. collapse two numbers: Energy proxy hunger / condition. Low energy → urgent need find food. High energy → slack, can afford explore reproduce. Age proxy ontogenetic stage. Young = unsophisticated; old = experienced (near-death, depending senescence). normalised [0, 1], means agent doesn’t know absolute energy reserves, proportion-full. Biologically reasonable: animal “feels” hungry relative state. Tier 2 fix (max_age scales metabolic_rate) give age signal biological meaning.

title: sense.jl — what an agent perceives
text: says. distance 1 input_radius (default 1), look grass value four cardinal cells (north, east, south, west agent), normalise [0, 1], optionally amplify brain-size multiplier, add input vector. Biology. directional resource perception. agent gets four values telling way food , distance within sensing radius. Two important biological choices: Cardinal — N, E, S, W. diagonals. Real animals usually sense directions, cardinal-choice computational shortcut doesn’t matter much sensing radius small. Per-distance, summed — radius 2, get separate inputs “grass distance 1 north” “grass distance 2 north.” contrasts MATLAB/alifeR ancestors, collapsed distances weighted sum (closer cells weighted ). Per-distance inputs give network strictly information; weighted-sum learnable function . sense_mult factor scales perception brain_size ^ 0.3. default brain_size = 1.0 1.0 (effect). brain_size_evolution = TRUE, larger-brained agents perceive grass gradients clearly — cognitive-foraging benefit makes big brains worthwhile (Aiello & Wheeler 1995). Audit findings. Input normalisation [0, 1] (vs MATLAB/alifeR’s raw values) clade improvement — prevents high-magnitude energy input dominating network’s first layer. biological issue. Variants worth considering. -direction sensing (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW) add four directional channels small computational cost. Worth considering foraging precision matters scenario. Smell vs vision modes: smell integrate across distances (perceive total food sector); vision prioritise nearest signal. Currently implicit per-distance format — network can learn either pattern. Limited field view: real animals blind spots. Currently blind spots modelled. Adding heading-dependent sensing kernel addition.
code: for d in 1:r     xN = wrap_or_clamp(x - d, rows, toroidal);  xS = wrap_or_clamp(x + d, rows, toroidal)     yE = wrap_or_clamp(y + d, cols, toroidal);  yW = wrap_or_clamp(y - d, cols, toroidal)     inp[pos] = clamp(env.grass[xN, y]  / gmax * sense_mult, 0.0f0, 1.0f0);  pos += 1     inp[pos] = clamp(env.grass[x,  yE] / gmax * sense_mult, 0.0f0, 1.0f0);  pos += 1     inp[pos] = clamp(env.grass[xS, y]  / gmax * sense_mult, 0.0f0, 1.0f0);  pos += 1     inp[pos] = clamp(env.grass[x,  yW] / gmax * sense_mult, 0.0f0, 1.0f0);  pos += 1 end

what: 1. Function header — what gets passed in
title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: says. function tick_agents! takes whole environment argument. Inside, pulls user-set parameters (specs), grid dimensions, whether grid toroidal (.e. world wrap around edges, like old Asteroids game, walls). Biology. grid world. Toroidal means edges — useful modelling habitats continuous agent’s perspective (patch forest large enough individual rarely encounters boundary). Bounded (non-toroidal) grids matter invasion-front dynamics like Shine’s spatial sorting (s-dispersal-ifd) front real ecological feature. Variants worth considering. Real habitats rarely either perfectly toroidal perfectly bounded — edges permeability gradients (forest fading pasture). clade currently supports two extremes. “soft edge” mode (probability re-entry decreasing distance grid centre) realistically model habitat fragmentation.
code: function tick_agents!(env::Environment)     specs    = env.specs     rows     = Int(specs["grid_rows"])     cols     = Int(specs["grid_cols"])     toroidal = Bool(get(specs, "toroidal", true))

what: 2. Per-tick costs and gains — the energy economy parameters
title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: says. Read user-set energetic constants top function rather looking every iteration agent loop: move_cost = 1.0 — energy spent moving one cell. idle_cost = 0.5 — energy spent staying place. (Half movement cost, zero — even resting animal burns energy.) eat_gain = 5.0 — energy gained per unit grass consumed. max_bite = 2.0 — maximum grass agent can eat one tick one cell. Added 0.4.0, see audit findings . three brain-energy parameters control expensive thinking . Biology. four parameters carve model basal metabolic rate resource intake. numbers matter less ratios — matters : Moving costs resting (, real animals). Eating yields energy moving costs (otherwise foraging losing strategy). maximum per-tick intake bounded (real animals handling time gut capacity — can’t eat infinite food one bush one moment). max_bite parameter handling time constraint, well-known Holling’s disc equation (Holling 1959, Can. Entomol. 91:385-398). Without , forager finds rich patch can extract entire content instantly — manifest unrealism. Audit findings. max_bite parameter missing clade 0.3.x — agents stripped cell entirely tick. fidelity audit flagged one ten kernel rules diverged ancestor implementations (MATLAB Bulitko 2023; alifeR R port). Restored 0.4.0. See dev/docs/kernel-0.4.0.md full reasoning. Variants worth considering. Real animals’ bite size scales body mass (larger animals take bigger bites). future kernel change make max_bite proportional agent’s body_size trait, coupling two existing modules. 0.4.0 backlog Tier 2 improvement.
code: move_cost  = Float32(get(specs, "move_cost",  1.0)) idle_cost  = Float32(get(specs, "idle_cost",  0.5)) eat_gain   = Float32(get(specs, "eat_gain",   5.0)) max_bite   = Float32(get(specs, "max_bite",   2.0))   # 0.4.0: handling time e_mode     = get(specs, "brain_energy_mode", "activity") e_base     = Float32(get(specs, "brain_energy_base",     0.001)) e_act      = Float32(get(specs, "brain_energy_activity", 0.5))

Every agent ’s alive ages exactly one tick. title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: says. Pass input vector agent’s brain (evolved neural network), getting back vector five “logits” — one possible action (north, east, south, west, idle). Pick action highest logit. Increment two counters tracking many total decisions many greedy decisions agent made. Biology. behavioural decision-making, simplified core: gather information, run brain, choose action. brain heritable structure (genome encodes network’s weights) foraging strategy evolves across generations. “greedy” choice (always pick highest-scoring action) one two exploration schemes: ANN, CTRNN, GRN brains: greedy = deterministic policy, behaviour fully determined current input. BNN brain (default): greedy randomised BNN samples weights posterior distribution forward pass. Two presentations input can yield two different actions sigma > 0. Thompson sampling, mathematically equivalent soft exploration reinforcement-learning theory. Audit findings. BNN’s per-tick weight resampling (s-baldwin, s-rl, s-social-learning audits) dilutes within-lifetime learning — copied RL-trained weights get drowned next tick’s random sample. Tier 5 backlog item: make BNN sample per significant decision interval instead every tick. Variants worth considering. animals (especially insects) behave ways closer fixed action patterns flexible decision-making. “-brain random walk” mode (brain_type = "random", already implemented) provides control. ecological realism, animals intermediate flexibility — habitual behaviour interspersed situational responses. clade’s BNN captures sigma tuned.
code: logits = forward(ag.brain, inp) action = argmax(logits)           # greedy; BNN exploration via sigma ag.num_choices        += Int32(1) ag.num_greedy_choices += Int32(1)

what: 6. Move — the energy cost of action
title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: says. Record agent’s energy tick’s actions (energy_last_tick, used RL module active). Update position based chosen action. North decreases row index; south increases ; east/west change column. Idle leaves position alone. action deducts energy cost, scaled agent’s metabolic rate (heritable trait — agents higher metabolic_rate pay action). Movement costs resting. Biology. mechanical-cost--living rule. Energy universal currency: Movement metabolically expensive (locomotion, oxygen demand, muscular work). Resting still costs something (basal metabolic rate — heart, brain, organs consume energy even rest). cost scales agent’s intrinsic metabolic rate (Kleiber 1932, Hilgardia 6:315-353; Brown et al. 2004, Ecology 85:1771- 1789). Action 5 (idle) costs less movement zero — biologically correct (basal vs active metabolism differ nonzero). MATLAB ancestor charged zero idle; alifeR port charged full movement cost (documented bug). clade’s split move_cost idle_cost realistic either ancestor. Audit findings. specific issues — rule sound matches biology. metabolic_rate heritable trait wired correctly; ’s broken downstream (always-age cap overrides pace--life signals — see audit s-pace--life). Variants worth considering. Real movement costs scale body mass (larger animals pay per metre per metre per gram less — Kleiber’s law). future change make move_cost scale body_size. Different terrain types carry different movement costs (mud slower open ground). Currently cost uniform across grid. fine clade’s grass-grid abstraction matter grid grew complex. current N/E/S/W movement rook-style (diagonal). Real animals typically use king-style (8 directions) movement. Adding diagonals small kernel change biological payoff.
code: ag.energy_last_tick = ag.energy x, y = Int(ag.x), Int(ag.y) if action == 1      # N     x = wrap_or_clamp(x - 1, rows, toroidal)     ag.energy -= move_cost * ag.metabolic_rate elseif action == 2  # E     y = wrap_or_clamp(y + 1, cols, toroidal)     ag.energy -= move_cost * ag.metabolic_rate elseif action == 3  # S     x = wrap_or_clamp(x + 1, rows, toroidal)     ag.energy -= move_cost * ag.metabolic_rate elseif action == 4  # W     y = wrap_or_clamp(y - 1, cols, toroidal)     ag.energy -= move_cost * ag.metabolic_rate else                # idle     ag.energy -= idle_cost * ag.metabolic_rate end ag.x = Int32(x) ag.y = Int32(y)
