what: 7. Eat — the resource intake rule (0.4.0 change)
title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: says. ’s grass agent’s current cell, take either max_bite worth, whichever smaller. Convert bite energy via eat_gain. Remove eaten amount cell. call eat_layered! (-op unless complex-landscape module enabled — , agents can also eat shrub canopy layers). Finally, cap agent’s energy energy_max (default 200) — agents can’t accumulate infinite reserves. Biology. foraging — central process trophic ecology. Three biological constraints encoded: Eat stand. agent gets resources current cell. ’s reaching, caching, sharing (modules add ). matches grazing herbivores well; less well predator-prey systems chase--handle norm. Handling time (max_bite). agent can process much per tick. Holling’s Type II functional response simplest form (Holling 1959). Storage capacity (energy_max). Real organisms finite storage — fat reserves, glycogen pools, gut volume. full, excess intake wasted. 0.3.x kernel single line eating: “strip cell entirely one tick.” ancestors enforced max_bite; clade outlier. Audit (Tier 1, 0.4.0) flagged restored handling time. Audit findings. 0.3.x strip--cell rule produced two artefacts: Per-tick energy windfalls. Finding rich cell yield eat_gain * grass_max = 25 energy one tick — distorting reproductive timing post-windfall agent immediately crossed min_repro_energy. Suppressed density-dependence. cell feed one agent per visit; second visitors found empty. made shared-resource scenarios (cooperation, kin altruism) harder test. max_bite = 2, maximum windfall 10 energy rich cells sustain multiple grazings. Variants worth considering. Bite size scaling body size (mentioned earlier). Different resource types (fruit, leaves, insects) different eat_gain max_bite. complex-landscape module start; adding nutritional dimensions extend . Group foraging: multiple agents cell cooperate extract (compete extract less). Currently agent eats independently turn (order side effect ag env.agents loop).
code: # 0.4.0: handling-time-limited intake. An agent extracts at most # `max_bite` units of grass from the cell per tick. Multiple ticks # (or multiple agents on consecutive ticks) are needed to deplete # a rich cell. Restores alifeR / MATLAB-Bulitko `maxbite` semantics. if env.grass[x, y] > 0.0f0     bite             = min(env.grass[x, y], max_bite)     ag.energy       += eat_gain * bite     env.grass[x, y] -= bite end eat_layered!(ag, env)   # complex landscape: shrub/canopy supplements ag.energy = min(ag.energy, Float32(get(specs, "energy_max", 200.0))) ag.energy += eat_gain * env.grass[x, y] env.grass[x, y] = 0.0f0

what: 8. Brain energy cost — thinking is expensive
title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: helper function: says. eating, deduct additional energy cost running brain. cost depends mode active: "none" — free thinking. cost. (Useful control runs.) "size" — cost proportional brain size. bigger brain costs maintain, regardless active . "activity" (default) — cost = size cost + activity cost. activity component scales strongly brain firing (average absolute logit value). "prediction_error" — currently placeholder, falls back size cost. Biology. Brains expensive organs. Human brains use roughly 20% total energy budget despite 2% body mass (Aiello & Wheeler 1995, Curr. Anthropol. 36:199-221). “expensive brain hypothesis” standard framework: large brains require sustained high-quality energy input maintain, constrains brain evolution. clade’s three modes encode three different theories brain cost: "size" corresponds expensive-tissue hypothesis — larger brains cost maintain (basal metabolic load). "activity" corresponds attention cost (Yaeger 1994, PolyWorld) — thinking harder costs . default adds within-lifetime selection pressure energy-efficient policies. "prediction_error" reserved information-theoretic cost (KL divergence prior posterior) isn’t implemented. MATLAB ancestor charged sum(|weights|) * annPowerCoefficient — size-based weight magnitude proxy. alifeR didn’t charge brain cost . clade’s “activity” mode biologically motivated three — novel. Audit findings. s-brain-size scenario tests parental- provisioning hypothesis: care needed bootstrap large brains newborns can’t yet forage well. Audit found effect directionally correct tiny default parameters — Δ = 0.009 units care -care. Tier 2 backlog: rebalance brain_energy_base brain_energy_activity cost consequential. Variants worth considering. Brain cost arguably scale super-linearly brain size (larger brains disproportionate maintenance costs blood supply, cooling, ). Current scaling linear. Brain cost might also depend agent — high cognitive load tasks (hunting, navigating) cost habitual ones (grazing). Currently task-dependence; activity mode closest approximation. Newborns juveniles might building costs addition running costs. Currently running cost modelled; parental-care module covers development costs separately.
code: ag.energy -= _brain_energy_cost(ag.brain, logits, e_mode, e_base, e_act) function _brain_energy_cost(brain::AbstractBrain,                               logits::Vector{Float32},                               mode::String,                               base::Float32,                               act_scale::Float32)::Float32     mode == "none" && return 0.0f0     size_cost = base * Float32(brain_size(brain))     mode == "size" && return size_cost     if mode == "activity"         act_cost = act_scale * sum(abs, logits) / Float32(length(logits))         return size_cost + act_cost     end     # prediction_error — use size cost as fallback (Phase 3 will implement KL)     size_cost end

text: ’s worth explicit tick.jl deliberately omits one tick agent’s life. responsibilities live elsewhere: Birth (reproduce.jl) — runs per tick agents ticked, scanning eligible reproducers. Death (death.jl) — runs per tick, removing agents hit death conditions (starvation, age cap, optional senescence semelparous death). Predation (modules/tick_predators.jl) — runs n_predators_init > 0. Predators update path; prey agents flagged death predators get removed kill_dead!. Optional modules — disease transmission, kin altruism, public goods, mating signals, social learning, niche construction, . lives inst/julia/src/modules/. -ops flag . separation matters: hot-path tick.jl intentionally lean, biological extensions live module files can audited reasoned isolation.

title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: skimming tick.jl looking biological problems, ask: energy units consistent? Movement idle costs units eating gain. Yes (abstract energy units; ratios matter, absolute values). anything happen free? — every action cost, gains require physical contact grass. ✓ hidden caps floors? Yes — energy_max (line ~118), max_bite (line ~113). biologically defensible. order operations matter? Yes — sense move means agent decides based previous surroundings, destination. biologically correct (decide go based currently see). eating happens moving, agent eats wherever ended , started. Also correct. social effects within tick? Within tick_agents! agent acts independently (awareness agents’ current-tick moves). Effects requiring interaction (predation, altruism) happen modules called ticks tick loop. standard “synchronous” ABM update; alternative (“asynchronous” — process agents one time, fully completing agent’s tick next) rarely used literature performance reasons.

title: Reproducing a paper — Courchamp et al. 1999 (and a note on custom modules)
text: Courchamp, F., Clutton-Brock, T. & Grenfell, B. (1999). Inverse density dependence Allee effect. Trends Ecology & Evolution 14(10), 405–410. Core claim: critical density, per-capita fitness drops. Mechanisms include failed predator-dilution, mate-finding failure, cooperative-breeding collapse, genetic inbreeding load. Prediction: small founding populations face higher extinction risk large ones, survivors small foundings reach smaller equilibria survivors large foundings.

title: Reproducing a paper — Courchamp et al. 1999 (and a note on custom modules)
text: Given clade’s modular design, first instinct reasonable: “’ll add Allee effect custom module. tick, agent, count local neighbours; threshold, apply extra mortality.” clade intentionally provide pathway. earlier 0.5.x stub (register_module()) removed 0.6.1 never wired couldn’t wired without breaking clade’s core performance contract. See architecture note reason, “actually works” section instead.

what: Why clade has no per-tick R hook
title: Reproducing a paper — Courchamp et al. 1999 (and a note on custom modules)
text: crossed exactly per run_alife() call. basis clade’s performance claim (see vignette("-clade")). Firing user-provided R function per tick require crossing boundary per tick, defeats design. fully-wired custom-module system need Julia-side R callbacks (JuliaCall.eval_string() JuliaConnectoR reverse calls); implementing properly substantial work real performance implications. ’s listed potential 0.7+ feature.

what: What actually works — extend clade at the boundary level
title: Reproducing a paper — Courchamp et al. 1999 (and a note on custom modules)
text: Rather wait per-tick R hooks, check clade already building custom module. Many mechanisms researchers reach (density dependence, ecological constraints, environmental stochasticity) already present emergently clade’s existing machinery. Allee effect specifically: clade’s grass-regeneration-plus-predation machinery already produce extinction-prone low-density dynamics? Let’s find sweeping initial density.

what: Stage 1: measure clade’s existing density-dependent dynamics
title: Reproducing a paper — Courchamp et al. 1999 (and a note on custom modules)
code: library(clade)  base <- default_specs() base$grid_rows        <- 40L base$grid_cols        <- 40L base$max_agents       <- 300L base$max_ticks        <- 1500L base$grass_rate       <- 0.05    # scarce grass base$n_predators_init <- 5L      # predator pressure amplifies                                   # density dependence base$predator_max_age <- 60L  initial_densities <- c(very_low = 3L, low = 6L, medium = 15L,                        high = 40L, very_high = 100L) conds <- setNames(   lapply(initial_densities, function(n) list(n_agents_init = n)),   names(initial_densities) )  sweep <- hypothesis_sweep(   base_specs = base,   conditions = conds,   seeds = 1:12L,   metrics = list(     extinct       = function(t) tail(t$n_agents, 1L) < 5L,     equilibrium_n = function(t) mean(tail(t$n_agents, 500L), na.rm = TRUE),     final_n       = function(t) tail(t$n_agents, 1L)   ),   n_cores = 40L )

title: Reproducing a paper — Courchamp et al. 1999 (and a note on custom modules)
text: Two signals visible data: Extinction concentrated lowest density. very_low (n = 3) condition produced extinctions; higher founding sizes always survived. canonical Allee extinction threshold. Equilibrium population scales monotonically founding size — survivors small founders don’t reach equilibrium large founders. Spearman(n_init, equilibrium) across 60 runs strongly positive. match Courchamp 1999’s prediction pattern, without custom module. clade’s grass-regeneration-plus-predation machinery produces density-dependent dynamics emergent property spec composition.

what: Sanity check on the Fisher contrast
title: Reproducing a paper — Courchamp et al. 1999 (and a note on custom modules)
text: 2/12 vs 0/12, Fisher’s exact p = 0.48 — extinction signal directional yet decisive 12 seeds. push past 2σ researcher run 32-64 seeds harsher conditions (even scarcer grass, predators). sub-σ position published-research-quality finding: clade shows extinction asymmetry small founding sizes, effect magnitude requiring runs characterise.

what: When would a per-tick user hook be worth it?
title: Reproducing a paper — Courchamp et al. 1999 (and a note on custom modules)
text: mechanisms genuinely need per-tick user code — can’t expressed via existing modules -run interventions. Examples: Behavioural rules depend within-lifetime learning histories logged clade (e.g., memory-based foraging bias driven individual-specific successes). Intricate spatial dynamics (e.g., diffusion process user-defined lattice overlaying standard grass grid). Bespoke selection pressures don’t fit clade’s foraging-energy-reproduction fitness loop. , right pathway user-written Julia modules (language clade’s mechanism) loaded kernel run_alife() startup — per-tick R callbacks. design keeps -per-run R↔︎Julia boundary contract gets full per-tick speed. Candidate 0.7+ feature. : either work within boundary-level patterns , fork inst/julia/src/modules/ specific mechanism.

title: Reproducing a paper — Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999
text: Dieckmann, U. & Doebeli, M. (1999). origin species sympatric speciation. Nature 400, 354–357. DOI 10.1038/22521. Core claim: disruptive selection resource-use trait, combined assortative mating based trait, drives single population split reproductively isolated lineages — without geographic isolation. Testable prediction: lower reproductive-isolation thresholds yield species (permissive isolation → clusters emerge persist).

what: Stage 1: grid search for the right mutation rate
title: Reproducing a paper — Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999
text: isolation_threshold mutation_sd jointly determine whether clusters emerge persist. Grid-search first find mutation regime supports D&D dynamic.
code: library(clade)  base <- fast_specs() base$speciation                  <- TRUE base$speciation_cluster_interval <- 10L  # 4 x 3 = 12-cell grid, single seed each specs_list <- grid_specs(   base,   isolation_threshold = c(0.05, 0.15, 0.30, 0.50),   mutation_sd         = c(0.05, 0.10, 0.15),   seed_from           = 7L ) results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 12L)  grid_tbl <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, s) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     threshold    = s$isolation_threshold,     mutation     = s$mutation_sd,     peak_species = max(d$n_species, na.rm = TRUE),     final_species = tail(d$n_species, 1)   ) }, results, specs_list, SIMPLIFY = FALSE))

previous_headings: Stage 1: grid search for the right mutation rate
title: Reproducing a paper — Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999
text: Final number species per cell (1-seed): Peak species across run: Two signatures D&D gradient already visible: (1) lower thresholds produce peak species across mutation rates; (2) mutation_sd = 0.10 gives cleanest final persistence. Select validation regime.

what: Stage 2: multi-seed validation at best mutation rate
title: Reproducing a paper — Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999
code: final_base <- base final_base$mutation_sd <- 0.10  sweep <- hypothesis_sweep(   base_specs = final_base,   conditions = list(     stringent_th50       = list(isolation_threshold = 0.50),     moderate_th30        = list(isolation_threshold = 0.30),     permissive_th15      = list(isolation_threshold = 0.15),     very_permissive_th05 = list(isolation_threshold = 0.05)   ),   seeds = 1:8,   metrics = list(     final_species = function(t) tail(t$n_species, 1),     peak_species  = function(t) max(t$n_species, na.rm = TRUE),     final_n       = function(t) tail(t$n_agents, 1)   ),   n_cores = 32L ) print(sweep)  hypothesis_report(   sweep,   contrasts = list(     moderate_vs_stringent        = c("stringent_th50", "moderate_th30"),     permissive_vs_stringent      = c("stringent_th50", "permissive_th15"),     very_permissive_vs_stringent = c("stringent_th50", "very_permissive_th05")   ),   metric = "final_species" )

title: Reproducing a paper — Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999
text: Spearman(isolation_threshold, final_species) = −0.567 across 32 runs — direction-correct strong monotone gradient.

title: Reproducing a paper — Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999
text: ✅ Direction reproduces decisively. Lower isolation thresholds produce species, exactly Dieckmann & Doebeli predict. ✅ Magnitude passes 2σ very_permissive vs stringent contrast (t = +3.32), Spearman gradient across four threshold levels strongly negative (ρ = −0.57). ⚠️ Caveat: peak species threshold = 0.05 saturates 200 (population cap). indicates cluster-detection algorithm -splits permissive thresholds — essentially “every individual species” transient early phase. Final species counts (cluster consolidation) interpretable; cap ~8 clusters permissive end. cluster-detection resolution issue rather D&D-theory issue. ⚠️ Small equilibrium populations: final_n = ~8 across conditions fast_specs runs short (2000 ticks) low max_agents cap. researcher wanting robust species counts use realistic_specs() manually raise max_agents max_ticks.

title: Reproducing a paper — Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999
text: reproduction cleaner Griesser 2023 clade’s speciation module specifically designed around mechanism D&D predict — disruptive selection + cluster-based mate choice. clade kernel’s mechanism matches paper’s theoretical mechanism, reproductions tend clean. don’t (K&B 2003’s stress-multiplicative cost), vignette surfaces mismatch. grid-search workflow pays : naive parameter choices given null (saw mutation_sd = 0.05 × threshold = 0.50 give 2 species 1 seed — ambiguous). grid search picked mutation rate isolation-threshold gradient crisp.

title: Reproducing a paper — Emlen 1982
text: Emlen, S. T. (1982). evolution helping. . ecological constraints model. American Naturalist 119(1), 29–39. Core claim: cooperative breeding (helping nest) evolves ecological constraints limit independent-breeding opportunities. Helpers stay home breed elsewhere — habitat saturation, resource scarcity, lack mates force helping subordinate-rank strategy. distinctive prediction ecological, genetic: helping emerge independent reproduction costly, even relatedness unchanged.

title: Reproducing a paper — Emlen 1982
text: don’t explicit habitat-saturation machinery clade, resource scarcity (grass_rate) clean proxy ecological constraint: low grass = hard support independent territories → Emlen predicts helping. Two measurables: mean_helper_tendency — heritable trait evolve higher constraint? n_helpers — observable helping behaviour occur frequently constraint?

title: Reproducing a paper — Emlen 1982
code: library(clade)  base <- default_specs() base$grid_rows                    <- 40L base$grid_cols                    <- 40L base$n_agents_init                <- 120L base$max_agents                   <- 400L base$max_ticks                    <- 3000L base$n_predators_init             <- 0L  # Cooperative-breeding machinery base$parental_care                 <- TRUE base$juvenile_independence_age     <- 10L base$cooperative_breeding          <- TRUE base$helper_tendency_init_mean     <- 0.2 base$helper_tendency_mutation_sd   <- 0.02 base$helper_min_energy             <- 60.0 base$helper_kin_threshold          <- 0.25 base$helper_transfer               <- 3.0  sweep <- hypothesis_sweep(   base_specs = base,   conditions = list(     abundant       = list(grass_rate = 0.25),     moderate_abund = list(grass_rate = 0.15),     scarce         = list(grass_rate = 0.10),     very_scarce    = list(grass_rate = 0.06)   ),   seeds = 1:8,   metrics = list(     final_helper_tendency = function(t) mean(tail(t$mean_helper_tendency, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     total_helper_events   = function(t) sum(t$n_helpers, na.rm = TRUE),     final_n               = function(t) mean(tail(t$n_agents, 500), na.rm = TRUE)   ),   n_cores = 32L )

what: 1. helper_tendency does NOT evolve under ecological constraint
title: Reproducing a paper — Emlen 1982
text: Essentially flat init mean 0.2 across conditions. Spearman(grass_rate, helper_tendency) = −0.115 (correct direction, noise-level magnitude). contrast passes 2σ. reproduces s-kin invasion honest-null PR #90. clade’s cooperative-breeding module channel indirect fitness back helper’s lineage strongly enough heritable trait respond selection. Ecological constraint doesn’t rescue — kernel-level limitation upstream selection regime.

what: 2. Raw helping events DECREASE with scarcity — opposite to Emlen’s prediction
title: Reproducing a paper — Emlen 1982
text: absolute counts, scarcer environments yield fewer helping events, . looks like contradiction Emlen, ’s population-scaling artifact: final_n drops ~388 (abundant) ~110 (very_scarce). Fewer agents → fewer opportunities help, regardless per-capita rate.

what: 3. Per-capita helping goes the right way
title: Reproducing a paper — Emlen 1982
text: Computing helper events per agent per 1000 ticks: Per-capita, direction correct: agents scarcer environments help higher rate. ~23% per-capita increase (0.30 → 0.37) Emlen prediction original form.

title: Reproducing a paper — Emlen 1982
text: ✅ Per-capita helping rate reproduces Emlen’s direction — agents scarce environments help higher rates agents abundant environments. ecological-constraint mechanism present clade’s kernel. ❌ Population-level helping count contradicts Emlen population shrinks scarcity faster per-capita helping rises. naive researcher looking absolute n_helpers report “contradiction”. correct measurement (per-capita) saves prediction. ❌ Genetic invasion occur. Even strongest ecological constraint tested (grass_rate = 0.06), helper_tendency stays init mean 0.2. s-kin invasion honest-null (PR #90) generalises: matter selection regime impose, clade’s current cooperative_breeding kernel doesn’t transmit indirect fitness back helper’s lineage.

title: Reproducing a paper — Emlen 1982
text: Always check units. Emlen’s claim per-capita; clade’s log aggregate. Researchers reproducing Emlen’s paper divide population size interpreting. raw aggregate number can go opposite direction theoretical prediction purely demographic reasons. pattern recurs throughout paper-reproduction vignettes: Réale 2010: birth-rate aggregate goes wrong direction; per-capita probably doesn’t. K&B 2003: signal-cost aggregate effect dominates abundant populations. Emlen 1982: helping events aggregate inverts; per-capita recovers direction. Whenever paper’s claim stated per-individual terms clade logs population-level aggregates, first sanity check normalise n_agents. hypothesis_sweep() multi-metric API (metrics = list(raw = ..., per_capita = ...)) place .

title: Reproducing a paper — Emlen 1982
text: full Emlen reproduction, clade needs: Kin-weighted fitness accounting (s-kin invasion PR #90 honest-null) — helper_tendency can actually invade rare genuine ecological constraint. Per-capita helping metric exposed logged quantity, computed post-hoc. Territory-saturation spec explicit kernel — making Emlen’s specific “breeding site available” constraint named parameter rather resource-scarcity--proxy. None attempted ; documented future kernel work.

title: Reproducing a paper — Fuller, Houle & Travis 2005 (sensory bias synthesis)
text: Fuller, R. C., Houle, D. & Travis, J. (2005). Sensory bias explanation evolution mate preferences. American Naturalist 166(4), 437–446. theoretical synthesis ties together: Ryan, M. J. (1990). Sexual selection, sensory systems sensory exploitation. Oxford Surveys Evolutionary Biology 7, 157–195. — sensory-exploitation concept paper. Ryan, M. J., Fox, J. H., Wilczynski, W. & Rand, . S. (1990). Sexual selection sensory exploitation frog Physalaemus pustulosus. Nature 343, 66–67. — túngara frog empirical landmark. Endler, J. . & Basolo, . L. (1998). Sensory ecology, receiver biases sexual selection. Trends Ecology & Evolution 13, 415–420. Zahavi, . (1975) Grafen, . (1990) — handicap principle, Fuller frames β_Sv < 0 leg synthesis. Fuller et al.’s distinctive contribution theoretical: cast sensory bias, Fisher runaway, Zahavi handicap three non-exclusive legs one quantitative-genetic equation Δz̄ = G(β_N + β_S) + u, separated β terms (G-matrix covariances C_tp) non-zero. clade’s job vignette say truthfully legs kernel can carry.

title: Reproducing a paper — Fuller, Houle & Travis 2005 (sensory bias synthesis)
text: 0.6.2 (PR #106) added three Fuller-framework metrics tick log — mean_preference_magnitude, mean_signal_preference_dist (proxy −C_tp), sd_signal_magnitude (V_t). 0.6.3 adds Zahavi-leg kernel field signal_cost_mortality. vignette reports new machinery can test.

title: Reproducing a paper — Fuller, Houle & Travis 2005 (sensory bias synthesis)
text: Five conditions × 8 seeds = 40 runs, 40×40 grid, 3000 ticks, signal_dims = 3, signal_drift_sd = 0.05. dose-response sweeps signal_cost_mortality across {0.000, 0.001, 0.002, 0.003}; null signal_dims = 0 (signals ).
code: library(clade)  base <- default_specs() base$grid_rows              <- 40L base$grid_cols              <- 40L base$n_agents_init          <- 120L base$max_agents             <- 500L base$max_ticks              <- 3000L base$grass_rate             <- 0.15 base$signal_dims            <- 3L base$signal_evolution_drift <- TRUE base$signal_drift_sd        <- 0.05  sweep <- hypothesis_sweep(   base_specs = base,   conditions = list(     null_no_signals = list(signal_dims = 0L),     zahavi_off      = list(signal_cost_mortality = 0.000),     zahavi_weak     = list(signal_cost_mortality = 0.001),     zahavi_mild     = list(signal_cost_mortality = 0.002),     zahavi_moderate = list(signal_cost_mortality = 0.003)   ),   seeds = 1:8,   metrics = list(     final_signal = function(t) mean(tail(t$mean_signal_magnitude, 500),                                     na.rm = TRUE),     final_n      = function(t) mean(tail(t$n_agents, 500),                                     na.rm = TRUE)   ),   n_cores = 40L ) print(sweep)

title: Reproducing a paper — Griesser et al. 2023
text: Griesser, M., Drobniak, S. M., Graber, S. M. et al. (2023). Parental provisioning drives brain size birds. PNAS 120(9): e2121467120. DOI 10.1073/pnas.2121467120. Comparative claim: across bird species, duration parental provisioning explains variation relative brain size. Extended provisioning buffers early-life energy deficit large brain imposes (“expensive-brain + costly-newborn” bootstrap problem; see van Schaik et al. 2023 framework). -silico test want gradient: sweep juvenile_independence_age track evolved mean_brain_size responds. Griesser predicts positive monotone slope.

title: Reproducing a paper — Griesser et al. 2023
text: naive hypothesis_sweep() default parameters likely produce null — clade’s brain-size evolution parameter- sensitive, regime makes provisioning buffer matter narrow. better workflow mirrors empirical researcher design -silico experiment: Stage 1 — grid search. Explore 2-D parameter slice (brain_size_cost_scale × juvenile_independence_age) single seeds find cost-scale makes provisioning gradient visible. Stage 2 — multi-seed validation. Re-run care-duration sweep best-scoring cost-scale 8 seeds via hypothesis_sweep() get robust confidence intervals. Stage 3 — honest report. Direction, magnitude, parameter-sensitivity caveat. also demonstrates real workflow clade users follow — rarely know right parameters front.

title: Reproducing a paper — Griesser et al. 2023
code: library(clade)  base <- default_specs() base$grid_rows                  <- 40L base$grid_cols                  <- 40L base$n_agents_init              <- 150L base$max_agents                 <- 500L base$max_ticks                  <- 3000L base$grass_rate                 <- 0.20 base$n_predators_init           <- 0L base$brain_size_evolution       <- TRUE base$brain_size_init_mean       <- 1.0 base$brain_size_mutation_sd     <- 0.05 base$neonatal_foraging_deficit  <- 0.4 base$parental_care              <- TRUE base$feeding_rate               <- 5.0 base$care_cost_per_tick         <- 1.0  # 4 x 4 = 16-cell grid, 1 seed each (fast regime discovery) specs_list <- grid_specs(   base,   brain_size_cost_scale     = c(1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0),   juvenile_independence_age = c(2L, 8L, 15L, 25L),   seed_from                 = 1L )  results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 16L)  grid_tbl <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, s) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     cost_scale  = s$brain_size_cost_scale,     care_dur    = s$juvenile_independence_age,     final_brain = mean(tail(d$mean_brain_size, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     final_n     = mean(tail(d$n_agents, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     crashed     = tail(d$n_agents, 1) < 10   ) }, results, specs_list, SIMPLIFY = FALSE))

title: Reproducing a paper — Griesser et al. 2023
text: cost_scale = 1.5, re-run care-duration sweep 8 seeds per condition distinguish signal single-seed noise.
code: final_base <- base final_base$brain_size_cost_scale <- 1.5  care_levels <- c(very_short = 2L, short = 8L,                  medium     = 15L, long  = 25L) conds <- setNames(   lapply(care_levels, function(dur) {     list(juvenile_independence_age = dur)   }),   names(care_levels) )  sweep <- hypothesis_sweep(   base_specs = final_base,   conditions = conds,   seeds      = 1:8,   metrics    = list(     final_brain = function(t) mean(tail(t$mean_brain_size, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     final_n     = function(t) mean(tail(t$n_agents, 500), na.rm = TRUE)   ),   n_cores = 32L ) print(sweep)  hypothesis_report(   sweep,   contrasts = list(     short_vs_very_short  = c("very_short", "short"),     medium_vs_very_short = c("very_short", "medium"),     long_vs_very_short   = c("very_short", "long")   ),   metric = "final_brain" )

title: Reproducing a paper — Kokko & Brooks 2003
text: Kokko, H. & Brooks, R. (2003). Sexy die ? Sexual selection risk extinction. Annales Zoologici Fennici 40, 207–219. Core claim: evolutionary “suicide” costly sexual traits unlikely stable environments, becomes possible environmental stress. interaction — fitness effect signals scales environmental pressure — paper’s distinctive empirical prediction. prediction qualitative original: populations carrying costly sexual signals face steeper extinction-risk gradient environments deteriorate. -silico test need quantitative surrogate. natural one : resource level, measure Δn = n(signals ) − n(signals ). Plot Δn function resource level. K&B predict slope negative: resources deteriorate, signals become increasingly costly liability. clean 2 × k factorial (signals × resource level), clean interaction test slope signals_effect.

title: Reproducing a paper — Kokko & Brooks 2003
text: structure paper reproductions (Griesser 2023, D&D 1999, Réale 2010, Emlen 1982): Stage 1 — grid search. grid_specs() + batch_alife() across wide (signal_cost × grass_rate) grid single seeds test whether K&B interaction ever reproduces predicted direction. Stage 2 — multi-seed validation. hypothesis_sweep() chosen signal_cost 8 seeds per condition. Stage 3 — viability spot-check. viability_report() -stressed cell confirm populations surviving, sitting pathological fitness floor.

title: Reproducing a paper — Kokko & Brooks 2003
text: K&B interaction ever go negative tested cost level?
code: library(clade)  base <- default_specs() base$grid_rows       <- 40L base$grid_cols       <- 40L base$n_agents_init   <- 120L base$max_agents      <- 500L base$max_ticks       <- 2000L base$n_predators_init <- 0L  SIGNAL_DIMS  <- 3L COST_LEVELS  <- c(0.0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8) GRASS_LEVELS <- c(0.20, 0.12, 0.08, 0.05)  # Build one spec per (cost, grass) cell, signals-off when cost = 0 grid_spec_list <- list() for (c in COST_LEVELS) for (g in GRASS_LEVELS) {   s <- base   if (c > 0) {     s$signal_dims <- SIGNAL_DIMS; s$signal_cost <- c     s$signal_evolution_drift <- TRUE; s$signal_drift_sd <- 0.01     s$mate_choice_mode <- "preference"; s$mate_choice_strength <- 0.7   } else {     s$signal_dims <- 0L; s$signal_cost <- 0.0     s$mate_choice_mode <- "random"   }   s$grass_rate  <- g   s$random_seed <- 7L   grid_spec_list[[sprintf("c%.1f_g%.2f", c, g)]] <- s } grid_envs <- batch_alife(grid_spec_list, n_cores = 20L)

what: Signals-effect per grass level (cost>0 mean − cost=0)
title: Reproducing a paper — Kokko & Brooks 2003
text: signals-effect non-monotonic weak. biggest drag appears mid-grass (0.12), either extreme. regime clade’s mate choice produce robust distinguishable signal costs across stress gradient. Compare pre-0.6.4 numbers git history (commit 3ede433), implicit-argmax stub produced clean monotonic shrinking pattern roughly 3× magnitude — result now know partly artifact mate_choice_strength ignored.

title: Reproducing a paper — Kokko & Brooks 2003
code: library(clade)  base <- default_specs() base$grid_rows       <- 40L base$grid_cols       <- 40L base$n_agents_init   <- 120L base$max_agents      <- 500L base$max_ticks       <- 2000L base$n_predators_init <- 0L   # isolate resource stress  SIGNAL_COST  <- 0.2 SIGNAL_DIMS  <- 3L GRASS_LEVELS <- c(abundant = 0.20, mid = 0.12,                   scarce   = 0.08, very_scarce = 0.05)  make_cond <- function(grass, signals_on) {   if (signals_on) list(       signal_dims            = SIGNAL_DIMS,       signal_cost            = SIGNAL_COST,       signal_evolution_drift = TRUE,       signal_drift_sd        = 0.01,       mate_choice_mode       = "preference",       mate_choice_strength   = 0.7,       grass_rate             = grass     )   else list(       signal_dims      = 0L,       signal_cost      = 0.0,       mate_choice_mode = "random",       grass_rate       = grass     ) }  conds <- list() for (ln in names(GRASS_LEVELS)) {   g <- GRASS_LEVELS[[ln]]   conds[[paste0(ln, "_no_signals")]]   <- make_cond(g, FALSE)   conds[[paste0(ln, "_with_signals")]] <- make_cond(g, TRUE) }  sweep <- hypothesis_sweep(   base_specs = base,   conditions = conds,   seeds = 1:8,   metrics = list(     final_n     = function(ticks) mean(tail(ticks$n_agents, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     crashed     = function(ticks) tail(ticks$n_agents, 1) < 10,     mean_energy = function(ticks) mean(tail(ticks$mean_energy, 500), na.rm = TRUE)   ),   n_cores = 32L ) print(sweep)  # Contrasts: one signals-effect test per resource level signals_contrasts <- setNames(   lapply(names(GRASS_LEVELS), function(ln) {     c(paste0(ln, "_no_signals"), paste0(ln, "_with_signals"))   }),   paste0("signals_effect_", names(GRASS_LEVELS)) ) rpt <- hypothesis_report(sweep, signals_contrasts, metric = "final_n") print(rpt)

title: Reproducing a paper — Kokko & Brooks 2003

title: Reproducing a paper — Kokko & Brooks 2003
text: Two reasons compound: Softmax vs argmax mate choice. Pre-0.6.4, kernel silently argmax (always pick single best preference-matching mate). Argmax creates strong selection: best-match signals propagate. strength = 0.7 softmax (documented intent audit), many partial matches also reproduce, dramatically diluting selective pressure signals. Less selection → smaller demographic drag signal_cost. Mechanistic framework gap. clade’s signal cost per-tick per-agent linear energy drain. K&B’s theoretical framework (built allele-dynamic models) assumes costs interact multiplicatively stress — signal-maintenance costs reducing fasting tolerance scarcity. different mechanistic claims dressed surface “costly trait” vocabulary. findings scientifically informative — audit methodology surfaced framework-level mismatch (still true) stub-artifact bonus previously made direction-contradiction look decisive actually .

title: Massol & Crochet 2008 — does Wolf's syndrome depend on β?
text: Wolf et al. (2007) report stable dimorphism exploration intensity x disruptive selection year-2 reproduction. trade-year-1 year-2 fecundity parameterised g(x)=(1−x)β g(x) = (1 - x)^\beta year-1 fecundity scales (1-x)^β · f_low year-2 fecundity x · f_high (plus (1-x) leg). Wolf uses β = 1.25. Massol & Crochet (2008, Nature 451, brief communications arising; doi:10.1038/nature06744) argued dimorphism robust: shift β much Wolf’s value bimodal x distribution collapses. critique mathematical (conditions stability mixed equilibrium narrow β range) easy test simulation. clade’s Wolf scenario exposes β personality_beta. Sweeping across [0.5, 3.0] fixed seed gives direct probe critique.

title: Massol & Crochet 2008 — does Wolf's syndrome depend on β?
code: library(clade)  run_one <- function(beta_val, seed = 42L) {   s <- wolf_personality_specs()   s$personality_beta                <- beta_val   s$max_ticks                       <- 5000L   s$random_seed                     <- seed   s$n_agents_init                   <- 200L   s$max_agents                      <- 800L   s$personality_hawkdove_per_tick   <- 0.3   s$personality_hawkdove_radius     <- 2L   s$n_predators_init                <- 10L   s$predator_max_agents             <- 30L   env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)    recs  <- env$agents   alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive),                   logical(1L))   if (sum(alive) < 30L) return(NULL)    ex <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],                function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$exploration), numeric(1L))   bo <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],                function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$boldness), numeric(1L))   ag <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],                function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$aggressiveness), numeric(1L))   list(     beta   = beta_val,     n      = length(ex),     r_eb   = suppressWarnings(cor(ex, bo)),     r_ea   = suppressWarnings(cor(ex, ag)),     r_ba   = suppressWarnings(cor(bo, ag)),     mean_x = mean(ex)   ) }  betas   <- c(0.5, 1.0, 1.25, 2.0, 3.0) results <- lapply(betas, run_one)

title: Massol & Crochet 2008 — does Wolf's syndrome depend on β?
text: Three five β values produce predicted positive bold-aggro syndrome; two produce essentially-zero negative values. syndrome strength varies 0.39 correlation units across β range (peak −0.08 β = 3.0 peak +0.31 β = 1.25). asset-protection signs (cor(exp, bold) cor(exp, aggro)) also shift β: weakly positive β = 0.5, becoming clearly negative β = 3.0. Wolf’s predicted direction (high-x agents less bold / less aggressive) emerges high end β range tested.

title: Massol & Crochet 2008 — does Wolf's syndrome depend on β?
text: Massol & Crochet’s critique partially borne clade’s spatially-explicit implementation: syndrome β-sensitive. Wolf reports bold-aggro correlation defining prediction model; varying β biologically plausible range moves correlation ~0.4. constant. peak coincides Wolf’s published value. β = 1.25 gives strongest syndrome sweep (+0.307). consistent Wolf chosen parameter maximised headline result — defensible publication practice, result fragile choice. asset-protection signs become Wolf-predicted high β. β = 0.5 (weak penalty high-x agents) signs wrong; flip Wolf’s predicted negative direction β = 3.0. opposite fragility: asset-protection mechanism strongest high β, β bold-aggro syndrome collapses. two-correlation tension (syndrome peaks intermediate β; asset- protection signs strengthen high β) suggests clade’s spatial implementation produces two distinct selection regimes rather one coherent dimorphism. Massol & Crochet’s framing — mathematical conditions stable dimorphism narrow — consistent see, spatial implementation adds structure: population locks x ≈ 0.1 every β tested, true x dimorphism Wolf predicts (x ≈ 0 vs x ≈ 1 modes) absent regardless.

title: Massol & Crochet 2008 — does Wolf's syndrome depend on β?
text: Single seed (42). numbers move seed--seed variation. proper test run 5–10 seeds per β report distributions. pattern suggestive, definitive. Phase 6 denominator . runs include per-strategy (1+α·N_i) competition denominator strengthens bold-aggro syndrome Wolf’s β = 1.25 (see vignette("paper-wolf2007"), “Honest discussion magnitudes”). Setting personality_alpha = 0 reverts Phase 3a behaviour shift numbers. bimodality β. Wolf’s headline result stable dimorphism (x ≈ 0 mode + x ≈ 1 mode). clade’s mean(x) ≈ 0.1 every β; population unimodal low x throughout sweep. matches Phase 6 finding paper-wolf2007.Rmd — clade’s spatial competition + one-per-cell offspring placement constrain equilibrium single attractor.

title: McElreath et al. 2007 — does Wolf's syndrome erode over time?
text: Wolf et al. (2007) report stable bold-aggro syndrome end simulations. McElreath, Luttbeg, Fogarty, Brodin & Sih (2007, Nature 450, brief communications arising; doi:10.1038/nature06326) argued stability misleading: mechanism Wolf et al. identify negative-feedback loop. Agents high assets become risk-averse; risk-aversion preserves assets; assets become uniform across individuals; trait correlations defined syndrome erode. prediction: run simulation longer Wolf syndrome fade. headline syndrome strength transient artifact stopped early time-point. clade exposes time horizon directly via max_ticks. Sweeping gives clean test.

title: McElreath et al. 2007 — does Wolf's syndrome erode over time?
code: library(clade)  run_one <- function(max_ticks_val, seed = 42L) {   s <- wolf_personality_specs()   s$max_ticks                       <- as.integer(max_ticks_val)   s$random_seed                     <- seed   s$n_agents_init                   <- 200L   s$max_agents                      <- 800L   s$personality_hawkdove_per_tick   <- 0.3   s$personality_hawkdove_radius     <- 2L   s$n_predators_init                <- 10L   s$predator_max_agents             <- 30L   env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)    recs  <- env$agents   alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive),                   logical(1L))   if (sum(alive) < 30L) return(NULL)    ex <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],                function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$exploration), numeric(1L))   bo <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],                function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$boldness), numeric(1L))   ag <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],                function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$aggressiveness), numeric(1L))   list(     max_ticks = max_ticks_val,     n         = length(ex),     r_eb      = suppressWarnings(cor(ex, bo)),     r_ea      = suppressWarnings(cor(ex, ag)),     r_ba      = suppressWarnings(cor(bo, ag)),     mean_x    = mean(ex),     sd_x      = sd(ex)   ) }  horizons <- c(2000L, 5000L, 15000L) results  <- lapply(horizons, run_one)

title: McElreath et al. 2007 — does Wolf's syndrome erode over time?
text: defining bold-aggro syndrome: 2 000 ticks — yet established (r ≈ 0). Population still approaching equilibrium. 5 000 ticks — peak r = +0.307. Wolf 2007 reproduction value reported vignette("paper-wolf2007") Phase 6 commit. 15 000 ticks — decayed r = +0.032, order magnitude weaker. Essentially gone. asset-protection signs (cor(exp, bold), cor(exp, aggro)) stay near zero across horizons.

title: McElreath et al. 2007 — does Wolf's syndrome erode over time?
text: McElreath et al.’s critique strongly borne clade’s spatially-explicit implementation: bold-aggro syndrome transient, peaking around 5 000 ticks decaying near-zero 15 000. decay happens trait-mean equilibrium stable: mean(x) essentially identical 5 k 15 k (0.094 ), sd(x) similar (0.096 vs 0.100). changes covariance traits, marginal distributions. matches McElreath’s negative-feedback framing: population reaches structural equilibrium individual variation x longer covaries strongly variation boldness aggressiveness. Asset protection homogenised population along protection-relevant axis. result also explains Wolf’s published correlations look clean: Wolf reports ~50-generation simulations. clade’s wolf_year2_repro_age = 100, 5 000 ticks ≈ 50 generations — exactly time-point syndrome peak. headline syndrome strength property simulation length, mechanism .

title: McElreath et al. 2007 — does Wolf's syndrome erode over time?
text: base reproduction vignette("paper-wolf2007") ran 5 000 ticks ’s roughly Wolf 2007 stops. horizon clade matches Wolf’s qualitative pattern (positive bold-aggro syndrome). 15 000 ticks — three times longer — syndrome gone. asset-protection mechanism real, visible effect trait correlations short-lived. Users running Wolf scenario clade aware : syndrome’s strength time-window-dependent. Quoting “final” correlation without specifying simulation length misleading.

what: Two critiques together: a coherent picture
title: McElreath et al. 2007 — does Wolf's syndrome erode over time?
text: Reading vignette("paper-massol-crochet-2008") vignette together gives coherent picture Wolf 2007 mechanism clade’s spatial setting: Massol & Crochet 2008: syndrome’s strength sensitive trade-shape parameter β. Peak β = 1.25 (Wolf’s default), weaker either side. mechanism parameter-fragile. McElreath et al. 2007: syndrome’s strength sensitive time horizon. Peak around 5 000 ticks, decays 15 000. mechanism time-fragile. critiques partially borne . Wolf’s headline result real conditional narrow parameter range specific time window — neither robust perturbation.

title: McElreath et al. 2007 — does Wolf's syndrome erode over time?
text: Single seed (42). qualitative pattern (peak decay) visible seed; multi-seed sweep firm timing peak half-life decay. Documented limitation. Phase 6 (1+α·N_i) denominator . Setting personality_alpha = 0 reverts Phase 3a behaviour might shift timing. Spatial implementation. clade’s grid + one-per-cell offspring + Moore-neighbourhood hawk-dove pairing produces different equilibrium structure Wolf’s mean-field model. McElreath’s argument mathematical applies negative-feedback setting; time-scale decay clade may differ pure Wolf show. Computational cost. 15 000-tick run takes ~130 s typical M1 laptop. sweep 5–10 seeds per horizon takes 10–20 minutes. Worth quantitative claim generalises beyond seed 42.

title: Reproducing a paper — Réale et al. 2010
text: Réale, D., Garant, D., Humphries, M. M., Bergeron, P., Careau, V. & Montiglio, P.-O. (2010). Personality emergence pace--life syndrome concept population level. Philosophical Transactions Royal Society B 365(1560), 4051–4063. DOI 10.1098/rstb.2010.0208. Core claim: metabolic rate spine correlated life-history syndrome. High-metabolism organisms shorter lifespan, reproduction, higher energy throughput — “fast-pace” pole. Low-metabolism organisms opposite — “slow-pace” pole. traits correlated, independent.

title: Reproducing a paper — Réale et al. 2010
text: Three directional claims, testable across metabolic-rate gradient: three trends together = pace--life syndrome.

title: Reproducing a paper — Réale et al. 2010
code: library(clade)  base <- default_specs() base$grid_rows      <- 40L base$grid_cols      <- 40L base$n_agents_init  <- 120L base$max_agents     <- 500L base$max_ticks      <- 2000L base$grass_rate     <- 0.15 base$n_predators_init <- 0L  # Fix metabolic rate at the init_mean (no evolution) so the sweep # is a pure dose-response across fixed phenotypes. base$metabolic_rate_evolution       <- TRUE     # must be TRUE to read init_mean base$metabolic_rate_mutation_sd     <- 0.0 base$max_age_scales_with_metabolism <- TRUE     # max_age = 200 / metabolic_rate  METABOLIC_RATES <- c(slow = 0.5, mid_slow = 0.8, mid = 1.0,                      mid_fast = 1.5, fast = 2.0)  conds <- setNames(   lapply(METABOLIC_RATES, function(r) list(metabolic_rate_init_mean = r)),   names(METABOLIC_RATES) )  sweep <- hypothesis_sweep(   base_specs = base,   conditions = conds,   seeds = 1:8,   metrics = list(     mean_age        = function(t) mean(tail(t$mean_age, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     births_per_tick = function(t) mean(tail(t$n_births, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     mean_energy     = function(t) mean(tail(t$mean_energy, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     final_n         = function(t) mean(tail(t$n_agents, 500), na.rm = TRUE)   ),   n_cores = 40L ) print(sweep)

title: Reproducing a paper — Ryan 1990 (sensory exploitation)
text: Ryan, M. J. (1990). Sexual selection, sensory systems sensory exploitation. Oxford Surveys Evolutionary Biology 7, 157–195. Ryan argued standard Fisherian picture — signal preference coevolve together — incomplete. Preferences blank slates co-drift signals; shaped selection sensory system non-mating functions. signal arises happens fit pre-existing bias, selected bias, producing apparent “coevolution” even though preference first. Ryan et al. (1990, Nature) tested túngara frogs: females prefer “chuck” calls even species male doesn’t produce , implying preference predates signal. Fuller, Houle & Travis (2005) formalised β_N leg quantitative-genetic framework (see paper-fuller-2005): selection preferences non-mating reasons (β_N ≠ 0) drives signal evolution genetic covariance C_tp — , C_tp ≈ 0, raw preference-based mate choice.

title: Reproducing a paper — Ryan 1990 (sensory exploitation)
text: tick, every agent’s preference vector updated: clamped [-1, 1]. installs pre-existing receiver bias — preference direction shaped forces outside mating system, exactly Ryan-1990 setup.
code: s <- default_specs() s$signal_dims              <- 3L s$signal_evolution_drift   <- TRUE s$signal_drift_sd          <- 0.05 s$mate_choice_mode         <- "preference" s$mate_choice_strength     <- 1.0 s$preference_bias_strength <- 0.05            # κ, per-tick pull rate s$preference_bias_target   <- c(1.0, 0.0, 0.0)  # pre-existing bias preference[i] ← (1 - κ) × preference[i] + κ × target[i]

what: Stage 1 — grid search for the right regime
title: Reproducing YOUR paper — a template
text: Common pitfall: grid returns almost final_n near zero, regime extinction-prone — back whatever makes selection harsh (cost scales, mutation rates, scarcity). See Griesser vignette concrete debugging walkthrough.
code: # TODO: pick two parameters to vary — usually #   (a) one that controls the MECHANISM strength (cost, rate, #       threshold, etc.) #   (b) one that controls the ENVIRONMENT your paper varies #       (resource, stress, duration, etc.)  specs_list <- grid_specs(   base,   param_a = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0),   # TODO: your parameter A   param_b = c(0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20), # TODO: your parameter B   seed_from = 1L )  results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 16L)  grid_tbl <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, s) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     param_a = s$param_a,     param_b = s$param_b,     # TODO: the outcome your paper predicts     outcome = mean(tail(d$YOUR_OUTCOME_COLUMN, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     final_n = mean(tail(d$n_agents, 500), na.rm = TRUE)   ) }, results, specs_list, SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  # Inspect: where is the signal? Which cells are viable? print(grid_tbl)

title: Reproducing YOUR paper — a template
code: # Pick the cell from Stage 1 where the signal is strongest AND # populations are viable. final_base <- base final_base$param_a <- 1.5   # TODO: from Stage 1 winner  sweep <- hypothesis_sweep(   base_specs = final_base,   conditions = list(     # TODO: one condition per level of the treatment you're sweeping     low    = list(param_b = 0.05),     medium = list(param_b = 0.10),     high   = list(param_b = 0.15),     very_high = list(param_b = 0.20)   ),   seeds = 1:8,   metrics = list(     # TODO: the outcome measure. Match units to your paper!     #   - Per-capita rate if the paper talks per-individual     #   - Aggregate count if the paper talks population-level     #   - Peak value, final value, or 500-tick mean — choose     #     based on what the paper reports     outcome = function(ticks) mean(tail(ticks$YOUR_OUTCOME_COLUMN, 500),                                    na.rm = TRUE),     final_n = function(ticks) mean(tail(ticks$n_agents, 500), na.rm = TRUE),     crashed = function(ticks) tail(ticks$n_agents, 1) < 10   ),   n_cores = 32L ) print(sweep)  # Pairwise contrasts — the direction the paper predicts hypothesis_report(   sweep,   contrasts = list(     # TODO: name the comparisons your paper makes.     # Convention: c(reference, test) — delta is test − reference.     medium_vs_low    = c("low", "medium"),     high_vs_low      = c("low", "high"),     very_high_vs_low = c("low", "very_high")   ),   metric = "outcome" )

title: Trivers 1971 — reciprocal altruism in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Trivers (1971) gave first formal evolutionary argument cooperation non-kin evolve. mechanism conditional: agents help others expectation helped back. Five conditions evolutionary stability: Long lifespan — many opportunities repeat. Low dispersal — partners encountered repeatedly. Mutual dependence — incentive keep partners alive. Partner recognition — know helped . Cheater discrimination — withhold help defectors. Trivers’ paper verbal-conceptual. Axelrod (1981, 1984) operationalised mechanism iterated Prisoner’s Dilemma global random pairing, showed tit--tat (TFT) wins tournaments, triggered vast literature conditional cooperation strategies.

what: What the empirical evidence says (the puzzle)
title: Trivers 1971 — reciprocal altruism in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Despite Trivers’ compelling argument, clear empirical examples reciprocal altruism nature rare. canonical case vampire bats (Wilkinson 1984: blood-sharing among unrelated roost-mates) — even contested (Carter & Wilkinson 2013 attribute much sharing social bonds food calls rather strict TFT). Reviews: Stevens & Hauser (2004): cognitive constraints (memory, recognition, numerical reasoning) make TFT difficult non-human species. Clutton-Brock (2009): “altruism” social mammals better explained kin selection -product mutualism reciprocity. Hammerstein (2003): “empirical record provides little support reciprocal altruism major force evolution cooperation.” question becomes: reciprocal altruism rare nature evolutionarily favoured Trivers’ conditions? Cognitive constraints one answer. Another — clade’s spatially-explicit ABM lets us examine directly — even granting cognitive machinery, spatial structure real animal encounters makes TFT-style reciprocity hard evolve.

title: Trivers 1971 — reciprocal altruism in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: clade spatially explicit. Trivers’ theory non-spatial (Axelrod’s operationalisation pairs randomly). re-implement Trivers using clade’s spatial machinery: Encounters: agents within Moore radius reciprocity_radius (default 1 = immediate neighbours) may interact tick probability reciprocity_interaction_rate (default 0.1). Partner memory: agent carries ring buffer size partner_memory_size (default 8) (partner_id, last_action). Strategy: generous tit--tat parameterised three heritable traits [0,1]: reciprocity_initial, reciprocity_retaliation, reciprocity_forgiveness. Payoffs form standard PD matrix scaled reciprocity_cost reciprocity_benefit_ratio. Cooperation transfers energy actor partner multiplier b/c; defection takes energy. See inst/julia/src/modules/reciprocity.jl implementation.

title: Trivers 1971 — reciprocal altruism in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: default parameters (reciprocity_cost = 0.5, b/c = 2, interaction_rate = 0.1, partner_memory_size = 8), cooperation collapses defection spatially-explicit clade simulations even though five Trivers conditions met. mean reciprocity_initial trait drops -reciprocity drift baseline. Defectors invade population either becomes predominantly defective crashes.
code: library(clade)  s <- trivers_reciprocity_specs()   # max_age=500, dispersal=FALSE, default cost/b s$max_ticks   <- 5000L s$random_seed <- 42L env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE) # At end: mean reciprocity_initial ≈ 0.22, n_alive ≈ 15. Cooperation lost.

title: Trivers 1971 — reciprocal altruism in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Cooperation can win, narrow parameter regime: low cost per cooperation modest benefit ratio. regime corresponds biologically interactions small individual cost (food calls, allogrooming, predator alarm calls) — exactly contexts empirical examples reciprocal-altruism-like behaviour cluster. verified test tests/testthat/test-reciprocal-altruism.R asserts cooperation rises Δ > 0.05 seed 42 favourable regime (typical observation: ≈ 0.500 → ≈ 0.718, Δ ≈ +0.218 — well 0.05 detection floor).
code: s <- trivers_reciprocity_specs() s$reciprocity_cost          <- 0.1     # small cost per game s$reciprocity_benefit_ratio <- 2.0     # b/c = 2 (Hamilton's threshold) s$max_ticks                 <- 2000L s$random_seed               <- 42L env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE) # At end: mean reciprocity_initial ≈ 0.72 (rose from 0.50 baseline). # Cooperation evolves above no-reciprocity control.

title: Trivers 1971 — reciprocal altruism in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Trivers’ second condition (low dispersal) predicts cooperation decline dispersal rate rises. clade test : Expected pattern: cooperation high low dispersal (partners re-encounter), declining toward 0.5 (drift baseline) dispersal rises (mean-field-like mixing makes TFT useless).
code: sweep_dispersal <- function(rate, seed = 42L) {   s <- trivers_reciprocity_specs()   s$reciprocity_cost          <- 0.1   s$reciprocity_benefit_ratio <- 2.0   s$max_ticks                 <- 2000L   s$random_seed               <- seed   s$dispersal_evolution       <- TRUE   s$dispersal_init_mean       <- rate   s$dispersal_mutation_sd     <- 0.0    # lock at this rate   env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)   recs  <- env$agents   alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive),                   logical(1L))   if (sum(alive) < 5L) return(NA_real_)   mean(vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],               function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$reciprocity_initial),               numeric(1L))) }  rates <- c(0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0) init_means <- sapply(rates, sweep_dispersal) plot(rates, init_means, type = "b", pch = 19,      xlab = "Dispersal rate", ylab = "Mean reciprocity_initial",      main = "Trivers dispersal-rate sweep — cooperation declines with mixing") abline(h = 0.5, lty = 2, col = "grey")   # no-reciprocity baseline

title: Trivers 1971 — reciprocal altruism in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: clade’s findings — spatially-explicit reciprocal altruism requires narrow favourable regime evolve, even Trivers’ conditions — supports empirical literature’s puzzle: Real animals face spatial structure clade models. ABM idealised cognition (perfect partner memory, perfect recognition, optimal TFT) struggles evolve cooperation, surprise real animals bounded cognition struggle even . “narrow favourable regime” (low cost per game, modest benefit ratio) maps onto exactly empirical examples sometimes argued reciprocity (food calls, allogrooming) — away high-stakes contexts (food-sharing famine, lethal injury risk defence) Trivers-style reciprocity might needed. consistent “-product mutualism” school (Brown 1983, Connor 1995): apparent cooperation nature mutually beneficial moment, Trivers-style delayed reciprocity.

title: Trivers 1971 — reciprocal altruism in a spatially-explicit ABM

Compare kin selection — clade’s existing kin_selection module produces strong cooperation high relatedness. Side-- side comparison quantify mechanism dominates. title: Wolf et al. 2007 — personality syndrome in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Wolf 2007 proposes consistent individual differences risk-taking behaviour (animal personalities) arise life-history trade-. Individuals high future fitness expectations (much lose) evolve risk-averse individuals low expectations (little lose). Across multiple risky contexts asset-protection logic applies, producing correlated behavioural traits — boldness–aggressiveness syndrome. Three specific predictions: Coexistence life-history strategies — disruptive selection exploration intensity x yields stable dimorphism (individuals x ≈ 0, others x ≈ 1). Stable 2·f_low > f_high > 1. Within-context consistency — across contexts time, individual bold one anti-predator decision bold subsequent ones; aggressive one hawk-dove encounter aggressive next. (Repeated games per individual within lifetime.) Cross-context correlation (syndrome) — boldness aggressiveness positively correlate; negatively correlate exploration x. Wolf’s model non-spatial — every game pairs randomly across whole population anti-predator game abstract individual draw actual predator grid.

title: Wolf et al. 2007 — personality syndrome in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: clade spatially-explicit ABM. value-add Wolf’s analytic mean-field model precisely encounters happen somewhere, neighbouring individuals, resource economy regulated grid. implement Wolf’s model verbatim (random pairing across population), throw away clade’s spatial machinery reproduce model ’s already analytically solved. interpretation : Hawk-dove game: pair focal -phase agent random -phase neighbour Moore neighborhood (default radius 1, configurable via personality_hawkdove_radius). qualifying neighbour, game tick. Anti-predator game: fires real predator within Moore radius 2 focal agent (uses env.predator_map). bold/shy draw agent.boldness determines whether agent gets bold-action energy bonus incurs bold-action mortality risk. Year-1 / year-2 reproduction: triggered fixed agent ages (wolf_year1_repro_age = 50, wolf_year2_repro_age = 100). Year-2 reproduction also kills parent. Game payoffs accumulated -phase (year1 < age < year2) fold year-2 fecundity. extension Wolf 2007, faithful reproduction. expected differences Wolf’s published correlations: Selection signal weaker () hawk-dove encounters depend local density rather uniform mixing, (b) anti-predator events depend predator proximity, constant abstract risk. Wolf’s (1 + α·N_i) per-resource competition denominator replaced clade’s grid-based spatial competition (foraging cells, handling time, energy budget). regulates total population doesn’t sharply produce year-1-vs-year-2 dimorphism Wolf reports.

title: Wolf et al. 2007 — personality syndrome in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Pull final-tick trait values check three Wolf correlations: Typical values configuration (seed = 42): defining prediction Wolf 2007 — boldness-aggressiveness syndrome — reliably positive grew threefold (1+α·N_i) denominator added Phase 6. asset-protection signs (exp ↔︎ bold, exp ↔︎ aggro) weaker / sign-flipped denominator regulates year-2 reward equilibrium population structure clade reaches doesn’t develop year-1-vs-year-2 dimorphism Wolf’s mean-field model predicts. See “Honest discussion magnitudes” .
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s <- wolf_personality_specs() s$max_ticks                       <- 5000L      # ~50 generations at year2 = 100 s$random_seed                     <- 42L s$n_agents_init                   <- 200L s$max_agents                      <- 800L s$personality_hawkdove_per_tick   <- 0.3 s$personality_hawkdove_radius     <- 2L s$n_predators_init                <- 10L       # anti-predator needs real predators s$predator_max_agents             <- 30L env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE) recs  <- env$agents alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive), logical(1L)) ex    <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive], function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$exploration),    numeric(1L)) bo    <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive], function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$boldness),       numeric(1L)) ag    <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive], function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$aggressiveness), numeric(1L))  cor(ex, bo)   # predicted negative — high-x agents are less bold cor(ex, ag)   # predicted negative — high-x agents are less aggressive cor(bo, ag)   # predicted positive — boldness-aggressiveness syndrome

title: Wolf et al. 2007 — personality syndrome in a spatially-explicit ABM
code: trait_df <- data.frame(   exploration    = ex,   boldness       = bo,   aggressiveness = ag )  ggplot(trait_df, aes(x = exploration, y = boldness)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5, colour = "#377eb8") +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "#e41a1c") +   labs(title = "Wolf 2007 prediction: high exploration → low boldness",        subtitle = sprintf("n = %d  Pearson r = %+.3f", length(ex), cor(ex, bo)),        x = "Exploration trait (x in Wolf's notation)",        y = "Boldness") +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)  ggplot(trait_df, aes(x = boldness, y = aggressiveness)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5, colour = "#984ea3") +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "#e41a1c") +   labs(title = "Wolf 2007 syndrome: boldness and aggressiveness positively correlate",        subtitle = sprintf("n = %d  Pearson r = %+.3f", length(ex), cor(bo, ag)),        x = "Boldness", y = "Aggressiveness") +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

title: Wolf et al. 2007 — personality syndrome in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Wolf 2007 reports correlations typically > 0.5 Fig 4 quantitative- genetics extension. clade’s spatially-explicit interpretation produces correlations order 0.1. difference? Encounter rate. Wolf’s mean-field model every agent paired another every game tick. clade’s neighborhood pairing depends local density. 200 initial agents 30×30 = 900 cells (~22% density), agent’s Moore-radius-2 neighborhood (24 cells) contains ~5 agents average — -phase, actual game encounters sparser Wolf’s mean- field rate. (1 + α·N_i) competition denominator (Phase 6 refinement). clade now applies Wolf’s per-strategy denominator N_high ≈ N·mean(x) N_low ≈ N·mean(1-x), snapshotted per tick. strengthened bold-aggro syndrome +0.10 +0.31, produce Wolf’s year-1-vs-year-2 dimorphism: population still locks x≈0 snapshot--per-tick approximation plus clade’s spatial competition coarser Wolf’s continuous per-individual evaluation. asset-protection signs (exp ↔︎ bold, exp ↔︎ aggro) weaker / sign-flipped locked equilibrium. Population dynamics. clade’s energy economy regulates carrying capacity addition (1+α·N) denominator. two interact: energy economy already prevents runaway growth, denominator’s regulating role partly redundant resulting selection gradient x weaker Wolf’s pure mean-field model. real interpretation choices, bugs. clade’s value embeds Wolf’s mechanism richer biological setting; trade-weaker / sign-flipped asset-protection signal even though defining bold-aggro syndrome preserved.

what: Diploid extension (Wolf 2007 Fig 4) — clade does NOT recover the syndrome
title: Wolf et al. 2007 — personality syndrome in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Wolf 2007 §“Genetic architecture” reports diploid quantitative- genetics version model yields stronger correlations (r ≈ 0.65) haploid basic model. conventional explanation diploidy + Mendelian inheritance buffers selection allows genetic variation accumulate asset-protection equilibrium. clade supports diploidy box — genome.jl meiosis logic handles ploidy = 2 — testing one line preset: Observed result seed 42 (5000 ticks, otherwise identical configuration): diploid extension produces opposite trade-haploid Phase 6: bold-aggro syndrome disappears (+0.307 → −0.003). Recombination decouples two trait expressions across generations, shared-asset-protection mechanism links haploid mode longer produces correlation. asset-protection signs flip toward right direction (−0.075, −0.068). Diploidy seems push population different equilibrium structure exploration trade risk-taking, line Wolf’s prediction. Neither pattern Wolf reports analytic diploid model. Wolf’s three correlations strengthen together; clade’s diploid extension produces swap — losing one gaining others. likely explanation haploid Phase 6 trade-: clade’s grid-based competition one-per-cell offspring placement constrain equilibrium population structure ways Wolf’s mean-field model doesn’t see. Diploid recombination interacts spatially-imposed structure produce new attractor. test tests/testthat/test-personality-syndrome.R asserts diploid scenario completes without crashing produces viable population; specific correlation magnitudes documented honest findings rather enforced.
code: s <- wolf_personality_specs() s$ploidy <- 2L            # diploid; Wolf Fig 4 setting env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)

title: Wolf et al. 2007 — personality syndrome in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Per-individual (snapshot) frequency evaluation — switch per-agent re-computation (1+α·N_high) / (1+α·N_low) recover smoothness Wolf’s continuous model assumes. Restructures apply_lifehistory_tradeoff!. Spatial vs random pairing comparison — run side--side personality_hawkdove_radius = 1 (clade-native) vs alternative global-pairing implementation (Wolf-faithful) quantify cost spatial restriction. Sensitivity game frequency — personality_hawkdove_per_tick personality_antipred_per_tick parameters control encounter rate; sweeping map encounter-rate--syndrome-strength relationship.

title: Wolf et al. 2007 — personality syndrome in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Two published critiques Wolf 2007 directly testable clade: Massol & Crochet (2008) Nature 451:doi:10.1038/nature06744 — argue dimorphism depends critically β (trade-shape). Tested vignette("paper-massol-crochet-2008") (added 0.7.x). bold-aggro syndrome strength varies ~0.4 correlation units across β ∈ [0.5, 3.0]; peak Wolf’s published value 1.25. critique partially borne . McElreath, Luttbeg, Fogarty, Brodin & Sih (2007) Nature 450:doi:10.1038/nature06326 — argue asset protection negative-feedback mechanism erodes individual differences time. Tested vignette("paper-mcelreath-2007") (added 0.7.x). bold-aggro syndrome peaks +0.307 around 5 000 ticks decays +0.032 15 000 ticks. critique strongly borne : Wolf’s headline correlation transient phenomenon simulation length reports.

title: Wolf et al. 2008 — responsive personalities in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Wolf 2008 extends Wolf 2007’s life-history mechanism different question: agent “responsive” (sample environmental state, choose action conditional ) versus “unresponsive” (use fixed general-purpose action)? Two key ingredients: Negative frequency-dependent benefits responsiveness (Methods Eq. 1). responsiveness rare, responsive agent exploits cues little competition — high benefit. responsiveness common, responsives flock resources — competition erodes benefit. produces stable coexistence responsive unresponsive types. Positive feedback cost--responsiveness (Methods §3). agent responsive stage 1 pays lower cost responsive stage 2 (already incurred start-costs). Even tiny cost-reductions produce strong cross-context consistency: agents responsive one context tend responsive others. Wolf 2008 thus links two empirical phenomena defined “animal personality” literature: coexistence behavioural types (polymorphism, optimal monomorphism) cross-context consistency (agents predictable across situations).

title: Wolf et al. 2008 — responsive personalities in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: clade spatially explicit. Wolf 2008 non-spatial (50-island mean- field). re-implement frequency-dependent benefit mechanism using clade’s spatial machinery; two-stage cross-context consistency extension left follow-work. One new heritable trait [0, 1]: responsiveness = Pr(agent samples local state overrides action toward richest free cardinal-neighbour cell given tick). Sampling cost: Per-tick energy drain responsiveness_cost paid agent whenever samples (regardless whether resulting move succeeds whether improves current cell). Frequency-dependence emerges clade’s existing economy: Many responsive agents → flock richest neighbours → handling- time depletes cells → first-mover wins, others waste cost. Combined one-per-cell movement (Phase 2), responsive agents high density frequently find richest target occupied end paying cost without moving. responsive agents → rich cells stay rich → first-mover advantage yields net gain → trait selected . dE/dp_r < 0 condition Wolf states Methods Eq. 1, realised via spatial competition rather abstract patch-choice denominator f_i = p_r·l_i + (1-p_r)·l̄. See inst/julia/src/modules/responsiveness.jl.

title: Wolf et al. 2008 — responsive personalities in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Two-stage cross-context consistency (Wolf 2008 §3). need per-agent times_responsive counter positive-feedback rule lowers cost prior responsiveness. clade infrastructure; one-day follow-task. Per-resource (1+α·N_i) competition denominator. decision Phase 3a (Wolf 2007). clean polymorphism result isn’t essential headline mechanism. Cost--uncertainty. Wolf 2008’s C single parameter; reality cost might scale environmental volatility, partial information, etc. modelled .

title: Wolf et al. 2008 — responsive personalities in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Pull final-tick responsiveness: Typical values configuration (seed = 42): trait rises drift baseline — selection responsiveness real. verified test tests/testthat/test-responsive-personalities.R asserts selection signal seed 42.
code: library(clade)  s <- wolf2008_responsiveness_specs() s$max_ticks   <- 2000L s$random_seed <- 42L env <- suppressWarnings(run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)) recs  <- env$agents alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive),                 logical(1L)) resp  <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],                 function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$responsiveness),                 numeric(1L)) mean(resp)   # selection moves the trait above the no-module baseline of 0.5

title: Wolf et al. 2008 — responsive personalities in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Wolf’s prediction: responsiveness DECREASE higher density (competition rich cells erodes benefit). clade test: Expected pattern (per Wolf): monotonically decreasing. Observed clade: non-monotonic, strongest selection--responsiveness intermediate density. high density, populations crash trait equilibrates. gap Wolf’s mean-field model clade’s spatial implementation — see “honest discussion” .
code: sweep_density <- function(n_init, seed = 42L) {   s <- wolf2008_responsiveness_specs()   s$n_agents_init <- n_init   s$max_agents    <- max(800L, 2L * n_init)   s$max_ticks     <- 2000L   s$random_seed   <- seed   env <- suppressWarnings(run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE))   recs  <- env$agents   alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive),                   logical(1L))   if (sum(alive) < 5L) return(NA_real_)   mean(vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],               function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$responsiveness),               numeric(1L))) }  n_inits <- c(50L, 100L, 200L, 350L, 500L) resp_means <- sapply(n_inits, sweep_density) plot(n_inits, resp_means, type = "b", pch = 19,      xlab = "Initial population density",      ylab = "Mean responsiveness at tick 2000",      main = "Wolf 2008 density sweep — clade implementation") abline(h = 0.5, lty = 2, col = "grey")

title: Wolf et al. 2008 — responsive personalities in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Wolf 2008 reports clean coexistence responsive + unresponsive types intermediate C (Fig. 2c). clade’s implementation produces selection responsiveness trait cleanly reproduce Wolf’s polymorphic equilibrium. Two reasons: per-resource competition denominator (Wolf 2008 — Wolf 2007’s denominator added Phase 6 0.7.0 work; responsiveness module yet analogue). Wolf’s (1+α·N_i) patch-choice payoff mathematical machinery makes per-agent benefit decline smoothly fraction responders. clade’s spatial competition (handling time + one-per-cell) discrete: first responsive agent rich cell gets benefit; later movers get nothing. produces winner-take-rather smoothly frequency-dependent. Population dynamics. clade’s grass-economy regulator interacts responsiveness trait ways Wolf’s model doesn’t see. high density, population crashes trait equilibrates — trait-mean interpretations crashing population unreliable (viability_report() flags ). Like Phase 4 (Trivers): clade’s implementation captures mechanism spatial setting changes dynamics ways biologically interesting.

title: Wolf et al. 2008 — responsive personalities in a spatially-explicit ABM
text: Two-context cross-consistency test (Wolf 2008 §3): pair responsiveness module existing kin-altruism hawk-dove modules; quantify whether agents responsive foraging also responsive social contexts. Positive-feedback cost reduction: implement times_responsive counter; cost stage 2 = C × (1 − k × times_responsive). Tunable per-resource denominator: optional (1+α·N_i) payoff scaling driven per-cell agent density (cheap compute via cell_to_agents-style enumeration already designed Phase 2 even Policy wasn’t ultimately taken). Compare Wolf 2007: pair responsiveness module Wolf 2007 personality syndrome — responsiveness correlate boldness-aggressiveness syndrome selection pressures?

title: Parameter Reference
text: toroidal grid, founder population, simulation horizon, two 0.7.0 kernel-discipline switches. random_tick_order = TRUE (default) enables random asynchronous agent scheduling per Grimm & Railsback (2005); setting FALSE recovers legacy fixed-order behaviour. max_agents_per_cell = 1L (default) enforces one-per-cell movement; 0L allows unbounded co-occupancy (legacy). See NEWS.md 0.7.0 audit surfaced .

title: Parameter Reference
text: Per-agent energy budget: starting energy, costs moving / waiting / reproducing, threshold agents starve. repro_cost_mode offspring_energy_mode control whether cost birth-energy fixed amounts fractions parental state — see default_specs() full Smith-Fretwell quality-quantity options.

title: Parameter Reference
text: base food supply: cells initialise, often regrow, per-cell saturation.

title: Parameter Reference
text: four implemented brain types plus random null-model baseline. "transformer" "synthesis" reserved names — kernel errors either requested present. bnn_sigma_source required s-baldwin / s-plasticity scenarios (0.4.0 Tier 5A). bnn_sample_freq = 5 lets REINFORCE gradients accumulate (required 0.4.1 s-rl ✅ verdict).

title: Parameter Reference
text: brains cost energy: fixed-per-weight floor, activity-scaled component, BNN-uncertainty information cost, Kleiber-style size exponent.

title: Parameter Reference
text: Ploidy, mate-choice, recombination, reproduction-age controls. mate_choice_mode = "preference" enables signal-preference assortative mating; "random" reverts baseline. mate_search_radius Moore-neighbourhood radius partner search.

title: Parameter Reference
text: Maximum lifespan, Gompertz senescence parameters (rate × shape), optional pace--life lifespan-scaling coupling. Gompertz hazard p=1−exp⁡(−r⋅exp⁡(r⋅ageshape))p = 1 - \exp(-r \cdot \exp(r \cdot \mathrm{age}^{\mathrm{shape}})) r=senescence_rate×aging_rater = \mathrm{senescence\_rate} \times \mathrm{aging\_rate}. senescence_shape = 1 (default) classic Gompertz; > 1 accelerates late-life mortality; < 1 gives late-life plateau (Vaupel et al. 1998).

title: Parameter Reference
text: Enable body_size_evolution = TRUE.

title: Parameter Reference
text: Enable brain_size_evolution = TRUE. Cognitive bonus balanced metabolic cost set “Brain metabolic cost” .

title: Parameter Reference

title: Parameter Reference

title: Parameter Reference

title: Parameter Reference
text: Enable n_predators_init > 0. Predators co-evolving agents 15-input sensory brain. predator_sense_graded = TRUE (0.4.2 default): prey’s predator sensory input distance d 1/(d+1) (graded threat-level). FALSE falls back pre-0.4.2 binary presence.

title: Parameter Reference
text: Enable group_defense = TRUE. Coordinated anti-predator behaviour high local density.

title: Parameter Reference
text: Enable niche_construction = TRUE. Shelters modify local selection environment. shelter_occupancy_bonus > 0 activates heritable-niche effect (Odling-Smee, Laland & Feldman 2003): agents shelter cell receive bonus × depth energy per tick.

title: Parameter Reference
text: Enable rl_mode = "actor_critic". plasticity_cost charges per-tick metabolic cost proportional learning_rate (Laughlin et al. 1998 Nature Neuroscience). lamarckian = TRUE writes within-lifetime learned weights back genome meiosis. default.

title: Parameter Reference
text: Enable social_learning = TRUE.

title: Parameter Reference
text: Sexual-selection machinery. Enable signals setting signal_dims > 0. preference_bias_target + preference_bias_strength implement β_N leg Fuller, Houle & Travis (2005); see paper-ryan-1990.

title: Parameter Reference
text: Enable mimicry = TRUE. Müllerian default; set batesian_mimicry = TRUE Batesian dynamics. 0.4.4 predator memory signal_dims-length vector updated via Widrow-Hoff delta rule.

title: Parameter Reference
