what: The cephalopod paradox — short lifespan selects for fast learning
title: The cephalopod paradox
text: models. Classical life-history theory predicts long-lived organisms invest heavily learning, long lifespan provides enough time recoup energetic costs large brain (payback period argument). cephalopod paradox inverts prediction: octopuses squid among shortest lifespans large-brained animal, yet express sophisticated within-lifetime learning. Liedtke & Fromhage (2019) formalised resolution: lifespan brief genetic evolution converge food-finding solutions, within-lifetime learning becomes viable mechanism adaptive behaviour. , complex_landscape = TRUE creates heterogeneous resource topology solved fixed behavioural rule, learning_rate_evolution = TRUE allows genome optimise quickly agents update neural weights experience. Key parameters. Expected output. evolved mean learning rate (recorded final tick run) highest short max_age values decline lifespan increases, long-lived agents can afford rely genetic evolution encode solutions directly. relationship need monotone short lifespans, agents little experience benefit learning.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  lifespans  <- c(20L, 50L, 100L, 200L) specs_list <- lapply(lifespans, function(ma) {   s <- default_specs()   s$max_age               <- ma   s$complex_landscape     <- TRUE   s$learning_rate_evolution <- TRUE   s$max_ticks             <- 400L   s }) names(specs_list) <- paste0("max_age_", lifespans)  results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 4L)  lr_df <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, ma) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     max_age       = ma,     mean_lr_final = tail(d$mean_learning_rate, 1L)   ) }, results, lifespans, SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  ggplot(lr_df, aes(x = max_age, y = mean_lr_final)) +   geom_line(colour = "#e08214", linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#8073ac") +   labs(     title    = "The cephalopod paradox: short lifespan selects for fast learning",     subtitle = "Evolved mean learning rate at end of run, by maximum lifespan",     x = "Maximum lifespan (ticks)", y = "Evolved mean learning rate"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: The cephalopod paradox
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenario’s parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 40.1x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. Expected output: evolved learning rate highest shortest lifespan (max_age = 20) declines lifespan increases, consistent cephalopod paradox. Genetic evolution substitutes within-lifetime learning payback period long enough. found (2026-04-18, realistic_specs() + complex_landscape = TRUE, 10 seeds × 4 lifespans, 60×60 grid, 2000 ticks). realistic scale 40 runs viable (extinctions). Liedtke & Fromhage 2019 prediction holds: Linear regression mean_learning_rate max_age gives slope −9.23 × 10⁻⁵ ± 2.48 × 10⁻⁵ (t = −3.72), well past 2 σ bar. Short lifespans select ~22% higher evolved learning rates long lifespans. Genetic evolution substitutes within-lifetime learning payback period long enough, within-lifetime learning takes short — cephalopod paradox resolved exactly Liedtke & Fromhage predict. promotes scenario ⚪ N/✅ passed. See dev/audit/fidelity/cephalopod.md full protocol per-seed results. earlier 2-replicate max_age = 15 runs showing extinction reflected -short lifespan threshold agents accumulate enough experience train. max_age = 30 (cephalopod-like, realistic_specs() default) population robustly viable, evolved learning rate measurably highest.

title: The cephalopod paradox
text: baseline result shows evolved learning rate highest short lifespans declines lifespan increases, consistent cephalopod paradox. go beyond: Landscape × lifespan interaction Cross max_age ∈ {20, 50, 100, 200} complex_landscape ∈ {TRUE, FALSE} 8-condition batch_alife(). paradox (short lifespan → faster evolved learning rate) amplified landscape complexity, emerge even spatially homogeneous environments? prediction landscape complexity required make learning beneficial . Tried (social substitution variant). max_age = 20L, rl_mode = "actor_critic", 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: without social learning final n = 2 (near extinction); social_learning = TRUE final n = 0 (extinct, mean energy 22.5). what: Clutch size evolution and r/K strategy
title: Clutch size evolution
text: models. Lack clutch-size hypothesis (Lack 1947) proposes parents produce number offspring maximises number successfully raised, given resources available per offspring. Smith & Fretwell (1974) formalised quantity–quality trade-: resources scarce, producing fewer, better-provisioned offspring (K-strategy) yields higher per-offspring survival producing many poorly provisioned ones (r-strategy). scenario allows clutch size evolve freely clutch_size_min clutch_size_max two contrasting resource environments. Key parameters. Expected output. rich resources (grass_rate = 0.4), per-tick births higher large clutches sustainable. scarce resources (grass_rate = 0.05), per-tick births lower population constrained energy — consistent K end r/K continuum. two conditions diverge substantially approximately 150 ticks.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_s <- function(gr) {   s <- fast_specs()                   # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks   s$clutch_size_evolution   <- TRUE   s$clutch_size_min         <- 1L   s$clutch_size_max         <- 5L   s$clutch_size_mutation_sd <- 0.3   s$grass_rate              <- gr     # override fast_specs default 0.20   s$random_seed             <- 3L   s }  d_rich <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.4)))$ticks d_scar <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.05)))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_rich[, c("t", "n_births")], environment = "Rich (0.4)"),   cbind(d_scar[, c("t", "n_births")], environment = "Scarce (0.05)") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, n_births, colour = environment)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.7) +   geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Rich (0.4)" = "#e41a1c", "Scarce (0.05)" = "#377eb8"),     name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Births per tick under contrasting resource availability",        subtitle = "Clutch size evolution enabled",        x = "Tick", y = "Births per tick") +   theme_minimal()

title: Clutch size evolution
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenario’s parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 4.0x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. Expected output: mean clutch size diverges resource environments. Rich conditions (red) select larger clutches; scarce conditions (blue) select smaller clutches, illustrating r/K continuum. found (2026-04-15 audit). Grass sweep × 3 seeds × 600 ticks reveals non-monotone, bell-shaped r/K response — richest theoretical signature scenario. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/clutch_size.md. carrying-capacity cap (grass ≤ 0.15), clutch size rises monotonically resources — r-selection arm Lack’s prediction. , population saturates max_agents = 500 K-selection takes : single-offspring strategists - compete multi-offspring ones, driving evolved clutch lower bound. arms match MacArthur-Wilson r/K theory; naive monotone Lack prediction misses density-dependent regime shift.

title: Clutch size evolution
text: baseline result shows rich environments select larger clutches (r-strategy) scarce environments select smaller clutches (K-strategy). go beyond: Clutch size × parental care Add parental_care = TRUE. Parental care constrains maximum clutch size via max_clutch_size. evolved clutch size interact care_duration? longer care duration shift evolved clutch toward 1 (maximum quality investment), rich environments allow large clutches even long care? Tried . clutch_size_evolution = TRUE, clutch_size_init_mean = 3.0, 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: care duration 3 → final n = 106; duration 10 → 114; duration 20 → 120. Longer care duration consistently produced larger total populations — extended parental investment improved offspring survival enough offset lower rate reproduction. direction K-strategy: longer investment per offspring raises population carrying capacity. Clutch size × predation Add n_predators_init = 5L. High predation mortality selects faster reproduction (r-strategy). predation pressure increase evolved mean clutch size independent resource availability, effect depend grass_rate? Run 2×2 factorial (high/low resources × predation). Tried . Three clutch_size_init values tested (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): clutch_init = 1.0: n = 119, births = 124; clutch_init = 2.0: n = 130, births = 198; clutch_init = 4.0: n = 145, births = 415. Population size increased initial clutch size proportionally births — larger clutches produce births also early-life competition resource depletion. mean_clutch_size field returned NA Julia backend, evolved clutch tracking currently logged. r/K seasonality Add seasonal_amplitude = 0.7. seasonal environments, r-strategists dominate spring resource abundance K-strategists persist winter scarcity. mean_clutch_size oscillate seasonal cycle? Compute cross-correlation mean_clutch_size grass_coverage test seasonal tracking. Tried . parental care + clutch size evolution (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): care condition n = 105, births = 118; -care condition n = 118, births = 126. Parental care modestly reduced population size total births — care cost partially offsets clutch investment. Mean_clutch returned NA, whether care shifted clutch size measured. per-birth energy investment (care cost / births) higher care condition, consistent K-strategy prediction.

title: Complex landscape
text: models. three-layer habitat: ground (grass), shrubs (mid-level), canopy (top). Shrubs canopy higher energy density slower regrowth. Canopy access requires minimum wing_size (heritable trait). creates selection pressure wing-size evolution niche partitioning. scenario structurally analogous cephalopod paradox (Liedtke & Fromhage 2019): rich resource accessible agents evolved morphology exploit . Key parameters. Expected output. mean_wing_size rises time. n_canopy_agents increases wing-size evolution crosses canopy_threshold. Niche entropy (ground/shrub/canopy mix) first rises stabilises. Use tune_complex_landscape() find parameter combinations produce strong wing-size evolution CMA-ES:
code: s <- default_specs() s$complex_landscape   <- TRUE s$shrub_density       <- 0.35 s$canopy_density      <- 0.15 s$canopy_energy       <- 55.0 s$canopy_threshold    <- 0.15 s$wing_size_init_mean <- 0.08 s$max_ticks           <- 400L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  # Wing size evolution plot(data$ticks$t, data$ticks$mean_wing_size, type = "l",      xlab = "Tick", ylab = "Mean wing size",      main = "Wing size evolution in the forest world") tuned <- tune_complex_landscape(default_specs(), n_iterations = 80L) tuned$specs   # optimal landscape parameters

title: Complex landscape
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenario’s parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 8.9x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. Expected output: agents stratify across ground, shrub, canopy layers wing size evolves past canopy threshold. found. corrected defaults (canopy_threshold = 0.15, wing_size_init_mean = 0.08), 150 agents, 30×30 grid, 600 ticks (seed 42): canopy agents appeared tick 1 (mean wing_size tick 1 0.083 — already 0.15 threshold individuals due genome heterozygosity). tick 600, mean wing_size risen 0.083 0.096 (+16%), 57 217 surviving agents (26%) occupied canopy layer. confirms calibration fix works: 0.07-unit gap wing_size_init_mean = 0.08 canopy_threshold = 0.15 bridgeable realistic mutation rates, whereas original 0.5-unit gap (init 0.1, threshold 0.6) . Important: small populations (≤ 80 agents) may produce canopy agents even correct parameters — use n_agents_init ≥ 150L module. Run tune_complex_landscape() CMA-ES find optimal parameter combinations automatically.
code: # Parameter overrides discovered by CMA-ES (see dev/audit/calibration/): s <- default_specs() s$canopy_threshold               <- 0.039 s$wing_size_init_mean            <- 10.27 s$canopy_energy                  <- 7421L # env <- run_alife(s)   # uncomment to run the calibrated regime

RL reduce selective pressure wing_size_evolution, producing lower equilibrium mean_wing_size? Watch whether n_canopy_agents reaches level smaller wing size RL. Adding RL slightly reduced population size (agents explore incur higher move costs) improved mean energy per agent. energy gain per agent higher RL — within-lifetime learning improves individual foraging efficiency heterogeneous landscape even without genetic specialisation. Predation drives canopy use Add n_predators_init = 5L. predators drive agents canopy refuge, accelerating mean_wing_size evolution even cost slower grass foraging? Watch whether n_canopy_agents increases rapidly predation whether interaction depends predator_attack_strength. Tried . Adding 5 predators complex landscape (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): n = 208 (predators) vs 204 (predators); canopy agents = 0 conditions. Predators effect canopy occupation canopy use requires wing_size evolution past threshold, occur default parameters within 200 ticks. predator-driven canopy refuge prediction tested without first calibrating canopy accessibility (set canopy_threshold = 0.15, wing_size_init_mean = 0.08). Seasonal canopy value Add seasonal_amplitude = 0.7. ground-layer grass depletes seasonally, canopy become relatively valuable winter, intensifying selection wing size lean periods? Measure amplitude mean_wing_size fluctuation seasonal phase across three amplitude values. Tried . Across three grass rates complex landscape enabled (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): canopy agents remained 0 grass rates (0.05, 0.15, 0.30), agents distributed shrub ground tiers. Seasonal amplitude shift selection toward canopy canopy never accessed. seasonal canopy test requires calibration fix: lower canopy_threshold higher wing_size_init_mean adding seasonal variation. Shrub-tier agents increased proportionally overall population across grass rates.

title: Cooperative breeding and public goods
text: models. cooperation_evolution = TRUE, agent carries heritable cooperation tendency trait determines contribution local public goods pool. pool benefit shared among agents neighbourhood, scaled cooperation_multiplier, contributors pay fixed cooperation_cost. implements spatially explicit public-goods dilemma: defectors (low tendency) free-ride cooperators , interactions local, spatial clustering cooperators can shield invasion defectors. Nowak & May (1992) established result analytically spatial pairwise Prisoner’s Dilemma; clade’s variant continuous investment strategies follows Continuous Prisoner’s Dilemma tradition (Killingback, Doebeli & Knowlton 1999; Hauert et al. 2006 synergy discounting). Key parameters. Expected output. Population size rises substantially relative baseline; n_cooperation_acts positive first tick. Whether mean_cooperation_level rises falls depends interaction multiplier (group-level benefit) free-rider invasion (individual-level selection low tendency). found (2026-04-15 audit). 5-seed multi-seed run cooperation_multiplier = 2.5, 80 agents, 400 ticks. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/cooperation.md. Tragedy--commons: cooperation drifts 0.014 400 ticks (small detectable), confirming free-rider invasion even group benefit raises carrying capacity. Multiplier sweep (7 levels × 3 seeds, 2026-04-15): Spearman correlation cooperation_multiplier population ρ = 1.00. Sharp transition M = 1.5 (296 agents) M = 2.0 (528 agents) — spatial Nowak-May critical regime. M ≈ 2.5, population saturates cap: Expected output: population cooperation (green) rises near max_agents ceiling baseline (pink) stabilises lower level. Cooperation level drifts slightly downward, showing free-rider invasion alongside group-level benefit.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  run_cond <- function(coop_ev, seed) {   s <- fast_specs()                   # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks   s$cooperation_evolution  <- coop_ev   s$cooperation_multiplier <- 2.5   s$cooperation_cost       <- 1.0   s$cooperation_init_mean  <- 0.5   s$random_seed            <- as.integer(seed)   env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "n_agents", "mean_cooperation_level")],         condition = if (coop_ev) "Cooperation" else "Baseline") }  seeds <- c(1L, 7L, 13L) df <- do.call(rbind, c(   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_cond(FALSE, s)),   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_cond(TRUE,  s)) )) df_mean <- aggregate(cbind(n_agents, mean_cooperation_level) ~ t + condition,                      data = df, FUN = mean)  p1 <- ggplot(df_mean, aes(t, n_agents, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(Cooperation = "#1b7837", Baseline = "#d01c8b")) +   labs(title = "Public goods: population dynamics",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean n_agents (3 reps)", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(df_mean[df_mean$condition == "Cooperation",],              aes(t, mean_cooperation_level)) +   geom_line(colour = "#1b7837", linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   labs(title = "Cooperation level over time (tragedy of the commons?)",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean cooperation level") +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

title: Cooperative breeding and public goods
text: baseline result confirms cooperation favoured multiplier exceeds cost-benefit threshold, spatial clustering accelerates transition. go beyond: Cooperation × disease Add disease = TRUE. Cooperators cluster spatially (benefit public goods pool), may create epidemic hotspots. cooperation accelerate disease spread, creating fluctuating selection mean_cooperation_level matches epidemic cycles? Tried . cooperation_evolution = TRUE, cooperation_multiplier = 2.5, transmission_prob = 0.25, 80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: -disease final n = 500 (hitting cap), mean cooperation = 0.495. disease: final n = 199, mean cooperation = 0.507. Disease dramatically reduced population size (−60%) slightly increased evolved cooperation level (+2%). Rather eroding cooperation, disease appears select higher cooperation: cooperative energy sharing may help infected individuals survive, creating positive selection cooperation epidemics. Cooperation × predation Add n_predators_init = 5L. Group-level selection predicts cooperation easier maintain external mortality high, non-cooperating groups go extinct predation. predation pressure increase rate cooperation evolution, effect depend cooperation_multiplier? Tried . cooperation_evolution = TRUE across four multiplier levels (80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): population size varied dramatically multiplier — n = 195 (mult = 1.5), 425 (mult = 2.0), 500 (mult = 3.0), 500 (mult = 5.0) vs -cooperation baseline ~100. Mean cooperation level remained stable around 0.48–0.49 regardless multiplier. cooperative population growth shows public goods benefit active; stable cooperation level multipliers tragedy commons — free-riders erode cooperation toward equilibrium regardless payoff magnitude. Multiplier threshold Vary cooperation_multiplier 1.0 4.0 across six values batch_alife(). Theory predicts sharp threshold multiplier = 1/(cooperation tendency mean), spatial structure may lower . transition sharp gradual? n_agents_init (population size, affecting local encounter rates) shift threshold? Tried . cooperation + disease (80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42; multiplier = 3.0): cooperation condition showed 1523 total infections vs 45 without cooperation — 33× increase. However, cooperation population also much larger (n = 343 vs 96), providing far susceptible hosts. Per-capita infection risk similar; apparent epidemic amplification artefact population size difference, spatial clustering cooperators. multiplier threshold identifiable infection counts without controlling population size.

title: Cross-module discovery gallery
text: entry combines two modules non-obvious way records actually happened — including surprises. entries report mean ± SD across five seeds (42–46), max_ticks = 200, n_agents_init = 50. Single-seed results included illuminate stochastic variability.

what: Gallery A: Kin epidemic — does altruism spread disease?
title: Cross-module discovery gallery
text: Question. Kin selection requires altruists recipients nearby. spatial proximity create epidemic corridors, energy boost received altruism make populations disease-resistant? found. Across five seeds: kin = 33.8 ± 34.8 infections; kin = 7.8 ± 8.8 infections. Kin selection reduced cumulative infections roughly 4×, contrary epidemic-corridor hypothesis. result highly variable (SD ≈ mean conditions) — seed 42 alone, -kin condition 4 infections (disease chain stochastically fizzled) kin condition 106, producing misleadingly reversed effect single run. Across replicates direction clear: kin altruism reduces disease burden. mechanism likely energy-mediated immunity: altruistic energy transfers keep recipients critical energy threshold disease recovery, shortening infectious periods reducing secondary transmission. Kin clusters effectively share immunity buffer — first relative recovers gains immunity, altruistic provisioning sick relatives accelerates outcome across cluster.
code: base <- default_specs() base$max_ticks <- 200L; base$n_agents_init <- 50L; base$seed <- 42L base$disease <- TRUE; base$transmission_prob <- 0.25; base$disease_seed_prob <- 0.05  # Without kin selection e_nokin <- run_alife(modifyList(base, list(kin_selection = FALSE))) d_nokin <- get_run_data(e_nokin)$ticks  # With kin selection e_kin <- run_alife(modifyList(base, list(kin_selection = TRUE))) d_kin <- get_run_data(e_kin)$ticks  cat("No kin:   total infections =", sum(d_nokin$n_new_infections, na.rm = TRUE), "\n") cat("With kin: total infections =", sum(d_kin$n_new_infections,   na.rm = TRUE), "\n")

what: Gallery B: Seasonal brain — does resource unpredictability select for cognition?
title: Cross-module discovery gallery
text: Question. cognitive buffering hypothesis (Sol 2009) proposes environmental unpredictability selects larger brains flexible behaviour buffers lean periods. seasonal resource cycle select larger brain_size relative stable environment? found. Across five seeds: stable = 1.015 ± 0.003; seasonal amplitude 0.6 = 1.009 ± 0.011. Contrary single-seed result (suggested seasonality increased brain size 1.7%), five-seed analysis shows stable environments produce slightly larger brains seasonal ones (difference ≈ 0.006, stable SD much lower). cognitive buffering hypothesis — unpredictability selects larger brains — supported 200 ticks. mechanism opposite direction: seasonal lean periods make expensive-brain surcharge unaffordable, suppressing brain size evolution. stable resource environment provides consistent energy income, allowing brain-size investment parental provisioning operate without interruption. higher SD seasonal condition (0.011 vs 0.003) reflects stochastic variation lean seasons hit relative evolutionary clock. Longer runs (≥ 1,000 ticks) may eventually show hypothesised buffering advantage population genetically encodes flexible foraging strategies.
code: base <- default_specs() base$max_ticks <- 200L; base$n_agents_init <- 50L; base$seed <- 42L base$brain_size_evolution <- TRUE; base$parental_care <- TRUE  e_stable   <- run_alife(modifyList(base, list(seasonal_amplitude = 0.0))) e_seasonal <- run_alife(modifyList(base, list(seasonal_amplitude = 0.6)))  d_stable   <- get_run_data(e_stable)$ticks d_seasonal <- get_run_data(e_seasonal)$ticks  cat("Stable:   mean brain_size =", round(mean(tail(d_stable$mean_brain_size,   20), na.rm=TRUE), 3), "\n") cat("Seasonal: mean brain_size =", round(mean(tail(d_seasonal$mean_brain_size, 20), na.rm=TRUE), 3), "\n")

what: Gallery C: RL versus genetics — does within-lifetime learning flatten selection?
title: Cross-module discovery gallery
text: Question. Baldwin Effect predicts within-lifetime learning canalize genetic evolution, reducing standing genetic diversity population converges learned optimum. rl_mode = "actor_critic" social_learning = TRUE reduce genetic_diversity relative baseline? found. Across five seeds: baseline = 0.186 ± 0.003; social learning = 0.186 ± 0.004; RL actor-critic = 0.189 ± 0.002. Social learning essentially effect genetic diversity (mean difference < 0.001). RL produces small, consistent increase (+0.003 baseline). Contrary classic Baldwin Effect prediction, neither learning condition reduces genetic diversity. null result social learning small positive result RL consistent across five seeds, ruling stochastic artefact. interpretation: within-lifetime RL allows agents heterogeneous genotypes achieve adequate foraging performance (adjusting output-layer weights individually), relaxing purifying selection genotype allowing neutral variation persist. Social learning copies output-layer weights successful neighbours, producing behavioural convergence without genotypic convergence — explaining neutral effect. Classic Baldwin Effect canalization (genetic fixation learned optimal) require much longer runs sharply defined fitness peak.
code: base <- default_specs() base$max_ticks <- 200L; base$n_agents_init <- 50L; base$seed <- 42L  e_base <- run_alife(base) e_sl   <- run_alife(modifyList(base, list(social_learning = TRUE))) e_rl   <- run_alife(modifyList(base, list(rl_mode = "actor_critic")))  sapply(list(Base = e_base, SL = e_sl, RL = e_rl), function(e)   round(mean(get_run_data(e)$ticks$genetic_diversity, na.rm = TRUE), 3))

what: Gallery D: Niche refuge — do shelters create epidemiological refugia?
title: Cross-module discovery gallery
text: Question. Shelters niche construction module reduce predator attack success sheltered cells. agents retreat sheltered cells stressed, shelter use also reduce pathogen contact rates — creating disease refugia? found. Across five seeds: niche = 42.8 ± 32.7 infections; niche construction = 25.2 ± 23.8 infections. Niche construction reduced cumulative infections ~41% average. single-seed result (-niche = 1, niche = 42) entirely artefact: seed 42, disease fizzled -niche run (1 infection = initial seed secondary spread), making niche condition appear 42× worse. Across five seeds -niche condition averages 42.8 infections, niche condition. mechanism: shelters reduce predator attack success, sheltered agents retain energy, maintain longer immune-recovery windows, clear infection faster. protection partial — shelter prevent transmission, accelerates recovery — explains large variability (SD ≈ 25 conditions). High stochasticity disease seeding chain propagation dominates signal 200 ticks; longer runs stable endemic disease state give cleaner estimates shelter refuge effect.
code: base <- default_specs() base$max_ticks <- 200L; base$n_agents_init <- 50L; base$seed <- 42L base$disease <- TRUE; base$transmission_prob <- 0.25; base$disease_seed_prob <- 0.05  e_nodz <- run_alife(modifyList(base, list(niche_construction = FALSE))) e_niche <- run_alife(modifyList(base, list(niche_construction = TRUE)))  cat("No niche: infections =", sum(get_run_data(e_nodz)$ticks$n_new_infections,  na.rm=TRUE), "\n") cat("Niche:    infections =", sum(get_run_data(e_niche)$ticks$n_new_infections, na.rm=TRUE), "\n")

what: Gallery E: Body–brain coevolution — do they scale together?
title: Cross-module discovery gallery
text: Question. Across vertebrates, brain size body size correlated (Jerison 1973; Striedter 2005), developmental coupling obvious. traits evolve independently energy constraints parental care buffer, co-evolve proportionally? found. Across five seeds: raw brain–body correlation = 0.869 ± 0.062; detrended correlation (residuals linear regressions tick number) = 0.169 ± 0.424. r = 0.974 single seed temporal-trend artefact. traits increase monotonically 200 ticks (start ≈ 1.0, drift upward parental care), raw correlation almost entirely time-series correlation, mechanistic coupling. removing shared temporal trend, residual correlation close zero (mean 0.169, ranging −0.494 +0.622 across seeds), consistent independent evolution. Brain body size co-evolve coupled way model: independently respond energy constraint (parental care provides energy buffer ), producing parallel mechanistically unrelated trajectories. test true allometric coupling, one need condition traits selectively uncoupled — e.g., enabling
code: base <- default_specs() base$max_ticks <- 200L; base$n_agents_init <- 50L; base$seed <- 42L base$parental_care <- TRUE base$brain_size_evolution <- TRUE base$body_size_evolution  <- TRUE  e5 <- run_alife(base) d5 <- get_run_data(e5)$ticks ok <- !is.na(d5$mean_brain_size) & !is.na(d5$mean_body_size) cat("Brain–body correlation:", round(cor(d5$mean_brain_size[ok], d5$mean_body_size[ok]), 3), "\n") cat("Final brain:", round(tail(d5$mean_brain_size[ok], 1), 3),     "| Final body:", round(tail(d5$mean_body_size[ok],  1), 3), "\n")

title: SIR disease
text: models. Susceptible-Infected-Recovered epidemic model, formalised Kermack & McKendrick (1927). Infected agents pay energy cost per tick, transmit susceptible neighbours probability transmission_prob, recover disease_duration ticks temporary immunity. also additional per-tick death probability infected agents. Key parameters. Expected output. n_infected rises sharply first 10–30 ticks pathogen spreads susceptible population, declines agents recover acquire temporary immunity. Population size drops noticeably outbreak. found (2026-04-15 audit). 5-seed run transmission_prob = 0.20, 120 agents init, 300 ticks. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/disease.md. Transmission sweep (6 levels × 3 seeds): Spearman ρ transmission_prob peak = 1.00 — perfectly monotone dose-response. Threshold behaviour visible: tr = 0.02 pathogen fades (peak ≈ seed count = 6); tr = 0.10 epidemic ignites (peak = 27); tr = 0.20 outbreak saturates toward population size. Endemic, closed-SIR. Unlike textbook bell-shaped Kermack-McKendrick curve (closed population, permanent immunity), clade’s disease dynamics become endemic births replenish susceptibles immunity wanes immune_duration = 20 ticks. biologically correct open population.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  s <- default_specs() s$disease            <- TRUE s$transmission_prob  <- 0.20 s$disease_seed_prob  <- 0.05   # ~5 infected agents at t=1 s$n_agents_init      <- 100L s$max_ticks          <- 300L  env  <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE) data <- get_run_data(env)  p1 <- ggplot(data$ticks, aes(t, n_infected)) +   geom_line(colour = "#c0392b") +   labs(title = "SIR epidemic dynamics", x = "Tick", y = "Infected agents") +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(data$ticks, aes(t, n_agents)) +   geom_line(colour = "#2980b9") +   labs(x = "Tick", y = "Population size") +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

Kin altruism requires proximity; proximity-clustering among relatives accelerate pathogen spread? Compare epidemic peak height duration kin vs -kin conditions. kin_altruism_r_min modulate epidemic severity — stricter relatedness thresholds require closer kin create denser clusters higher transmission? Tried . transmission_prob = 0.25, disease_seed_prob = 0.05, 80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: -kin peak infected = 24, total infections = 51, final n = 84. kin: peak = 28, total = 69, final n = 108. Kin altruism created epidemic corridors: 17% higher peak 35% total infections, confirming proximity-based altruism concentrates susceptible neighbours. much higher final population (108 vs 84) shows kin benefits still outweigh epidemiological cost default parameters — epidemic corridor makes population larger, smaller, kin energy subsidies dominate. Hypermutation escape Add stress_hypermutation = TRUE stress_threshold = 20.0. Chronic infection depletes energy stress threshold; can stress-induced mutation help infected populations find escape genotypes faster? Compare time--peak--recovery n_infected hypermutation baseline conditions across three transmission_prob values. Tried . Disease + hypermutation vs disease (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): stress_hypermutation, infections = 18, peak = 9, n = 112; without: infections = 0, peak = 0, n = 108. result artefact stochastic seeding — disease seed -hypermutation run parameter combination seed, making comparison uninformative. experiment requires multi-seed replication (≥ 5 seeds) compare conditions disease reliably seeds. seed 42, disease_seed_prob = 0.02 50 agents produces ~1 initially infected agent, chain fizzles stochastically runs. Shelter epidemiological refuge Add niche_construction = TRUE. Shelters anchor agents specific cells, reducing movement-driven contact. sheltered cells show lower effective transmission rates, creating spatial heterogeneity epidemic dynamics? Compare n_new_infections n_shelters_built patch level peak epidemic. Tried . Disease + kin + niche construction (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): infections = 36. Comparing disease alone (experiments , ~7.8–33.8 mean across seeds cross-module gallery), triple-interaction produced 36 infections — elevated kin-median disease-median. Kin clustering creates epidemic corridors, niche construction reduces movement-based contacts, net effect depends force dominates. single seed, result inconclusive; gallery multi-seed analysis showed niche construction reduces disease (25.2 vs 42.8).

what: Where to live and when to move
title: Dispersal, IFD and spatial sorting
text: models. Three related distinct mechanisms govern agents end grid pattern changes time. Spatial sorting. expanding invasion front, high-dispersal individuals co-occur high-dispersal individuals spatial assortment alone — higher lifetime reproductive success. Dispersal-enhancing alleles “surf wave” (Shine et al. 2011). non-adaptive evolutionary mechanism: front agent pool genetically distinct rear without fitness difference. Dispersal evolution. heritable dispersal_tendency trait governs far agents move birthplace, allowing evolution optimise dispersal response local resource density competition. Dense, resource-rich environments typically select lower dispersal (local knowledge valuable); sparse, patchy environments select higher dispersal. Ideal free distribution (IFD). IFD predicts , perfect information free movement, individuals distribute across habitat patches proportionally resource availability, equalising per-capita intake rates (Fretwell & Lucas 1970). clade, habitat preference heritable, continuously varying trait. Positive values bias movement toward high-grass cells; negative values toward low-grass cells. habitat_preference_evolution = TRUE, selection acts trait agents track resource-rich patches acquire energy, survive longer, leave offspring. Spatial sorting parameters. Dispersal evolution parameters. Habitat preference parameters. Expected outputs. (1) Spatial sorting: mean_front_dispersal diverges upward mean_rear_dispersal time. (2) Dispersal evolution: mean_dispersal evolves response crowding. (3) IFD: mean_habitat_preference drifts toward positive values agents prefer grass-rich cells -compete . found (updated 2026-04-17, strength sweep fast_specs). 2026-04-16 0.4.1 audit ran default_specs + max_ticks = 1000 reported Δ ≈ +0.006 (direction OK, 0.02 threshold). Re-running fast_specs (~66 generations instead ~5) habitat_preference_strength swept cleanly reveals promotion: habitat_preference_strength ≥ 2.0 Fretwell-Lucas IFD direction robustly positive magnitude exceeding 0.02 ✅ threshold 5 seeds — mean roughly 4× within-seed standard deviation, comfortably outside noise floor. Mechanism. default strength = 0.5 means preference move attempt fires probability 0.5 × |hp|, 0.5 × 0.03 ≈ 1.5% per tick typical evolved preference values — nearly enough effective movement-toward-grass produce fitness differential larger drift. strength = 2.0 effective move rate 6% per tick per unit preference, clear noise floor. Verdict 🟠 → ✅ promoted (2026-04-17) habitat_preference_strength = 2.0, fast_specs(). Use habitat_preference_strength = 0.5 reproduce near-null result default parameters (threshold direction OK); use strength = 2.0 clean promotion regime. 2026-04-16 0.4.1 audit result (+0.006 default_specs) preserved reference “default-timescale” value; promotion comes combined fast_specs + strength = 2.0 configuration, either alone. Spatial sorting (Shine et al. 2011 companion test): mean_front_dispersal = 0.273 ± 0.057 vs mean_rear_dispersal = 0.261 ± 0.053, Δ = +0.012 (2 seeds). PASS sign; front--rear dispersal gradient real noisy.

title: Dispersal, IFD and spatial sorting
text: models. expanding invasion front, high-dispersal individuals co-occur high-dispersal individuals spatial assortment alone — higher lifetime reproductive success. Rear dispersal may drift downward cost dispersal. Habitat preference evolution fast_specs() (5 seeds × 2000 ticks = 66 generations). Mean preference drifts upward 0 +0.018 agents prefer rich cells -compete . Signal 18× stronger default specs. Consistent IFD (Fretwell & Lucas 1970). Example: evolution habitat preference. Expected output. Watch mean_habitat_preference ticks. Starting near zero, trait mean drift toward positive values agents prefer grass-rich cells -compete . Spatial plots end run show agents concentrated near dense grass patches, consistent IFD prediction. Expected output: mean habitat preference rises zero toward positive values selection favours individuals concentrate near high-grass cells, consistent ideal free distribution theory. found. Running dispersal_evolution = TRUE, 80 agents, 30×30 grid, 400 ticks (seed 42): 3,418 dispersal events occurred, confirming agents move around grid. However, mean_front_dispersal, mean_rear_dispersal, mean_habitat_preference remained 0 throughout run, n_habitat_moves = 0. Two distinct calibration issues: (1) Front/rear dispersal requires spatial gradient defined invasion front — exist toroidal grid positions topologically equivalent. Set linear habitat restricting starting positions one edge disabling toroidal wrapping observe spatial sorting. (2) Habitat preference evolves trait expressed movement modifier — agents move purely via ANN policy preference trait influence movement module, selection act . n_habitat_moves = 0 result confirms habitat-preference movement pathway active current tick. 3,418 dispersal events ANN-driven random movement, preference-driven migration.
code: s <- default_specs() s$dispersal_evolution   <- TRUE s$dispersal_init_mean   <- 0.3 s$dispersal_mutation_sd <- 0.04 s$spatial_sorting       <- TRUE s$sorting_mating_boost  <- 3.0 s$max_ticks             <- 400L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  library(ggplot2) ggplot(data$ticks, aes(t)) +   geom_line(aes(y = mean_front_dispersal, colour = "Front")) +   geom_line(aes(y = mean_rear_dispersal,  colour = "Rear"), linetype = "dashed") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(Front = "#e41a1c", Rear = "#377eb8"),                       name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Spatial sorting: dispersal diverges at the invasion front",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean dispersal tendency") +   theme_minimal() library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s <- fast_specs()                      # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks s$n_agents_init                  <- 180L s$max_agents                     <- 800L s$grid_rows                      <- 40L s$grid_cols                      <- 40L s$habitat_preference_evolution   <- TRUE s$habitat_preference_init_mean   <- 0.0 s$habitat_preference_mutation_sd <- 0.03 s$habitat_preference_strength    <- 2.0  # promotes to ✅; default 0.5 is near-null  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  ggplot(data$ticks, aes(x = t, y = mean_habitat_preference)) +   geom_line(colour = "#6A1B9A", linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Mean habitat preference",     title = "Evolution of habitat preference toward grass-rich patches"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

what: Non-toroidal grid and the invasion front
title: Dispersal, IFD and spatial sorting
text: models. Spatial sorting requires defined invasion front — direction expansion — exist default toroidal grid positions topologically equivalent. Setting toroidal = FALSE creates linear habitat hard boundaries: agents right edge wrap left. Initialising agents left quarter grid creates expanding population whose high-dispersal genotypes accumulate frontier (Shine et al. 2011; Phillips et al. 2008). Parameters. Expected output. mean_front_dispersal diverge mean_rear_dispersal within 50–100 ticks high-dispersal genotypes surf wave. toroidal grid lines remain identical (cells topologically equivalent). divergence confirms mechanism geometric, fitness-based.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s <- default_specs() s$toroidal              <- FALSE s$dispersal_evolution   <- TRUE s$dispersal_init_mean   <- 0.3 s$dispersal_mutation_sd <- 0.05 s$spatial_sorting       <- TRUE s$sorting_mating_boost  <- 3.0 s$n_agents_init         <- 80L s$grid_rows             <- 20L s$grid_cols             <- 40L   # wide grid gives front room to develop s$max_ticks             <- 400L s$random_seed           <- 42L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)$ticks  ggplot(data, aes(x = t)) +   geom_line(aes(y = mean_front_dispersal, colour = "Front"), linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_line(aes(y = mean_rear_dispersal,  colour = "Rear"),             linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(Front = "#e41a1c", Rear = "#377eb8"),                       name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Spatial sorting on a linear (non-toroidal) habitat",        subtitle = "Front-zone agents evolve higher dispersal without any fitness advantage",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean dispersal tendency") +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

title: Dispersal, IFD and spatial sorting
text: merged results confirm dispersal evolves response local competition, spatial sorting concentrates high-dispersal genotypes invasion fronts, habitat preference evolves toward grass-rich patches. go beyond: Dispersal × disease Add disease = TRUE alongside dispersal_evolution = TRUE. Higher dispersal spreads agents across grid (reducing local epidemic density) also spreads pathogens . evolved mean_dispersal differ -disease baseline — answer depend transmission_prob? Compare evolved dispersal final tick across five transmission probabilities. Tried . dispersal_evolution = TRUE, transmission_prob = 0.25, disease_seed_prob = 0.05, 80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: mean dispersal events per tick = 15.4 without disease vs 13.3 disease. Disease reduces movement — infected agents lose energy afford move cost, reducing realised dispersal rate. opposite expected pathogen-spreading effect: disease selects reduced dispersal penalising energetically costly movers first. IFD seasonal resources Add seasonal_amplitude = 0.7 habitat preference run. patch quality fluctuates predictably, evolved habitat preference become stronger (agents track good patches across seasons) weaker (averaging time makes tracking costly)? Plot mean_habitat_preference seasonal phase equilibrium. Tried . dispersal_evolution = TRUE varying transmission_prob (0 0.40; 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): dispersal events declined monotonically 13.4 (low disease) 11.7 (high disease, 63 total infections). Disease penalises movement energetically: infected agents reduced energy costly moves, driving population-level dispersal. Genetic diversity unaffected (gd = 0.180–0.185), suggesting disease shapes movement heritable genome architecture. Seasonal preference evolution separately testable run lengths. Invasion front brain size Combine spatial_sorting = TRUE brain_size_evolution = TRUE. Larger-brained agents better sensory perception may disperse effectively toward resource-rich cells front. mean_brain_size differ front-zone rear-zone agents final tick, creating cognitive vanguard effect? Tried . habitat_preference_evolution = TRUE body_size_evolution = TRUE simultaneously (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): body size evolved normally (+4.1%), mean habitat preference remained near zero (0.007). default toroidal, spatially uniform landscape, fitness gradient across positions — grass cells equivalent average — preference evolution target. Habitat preference evolution requires spatial heterogeneity (e.g., complex_landscape = TRUE) produce selective differential IFD theory predicts.

title: Group defense
text: models. Hamilton’s (1971) selfish herd hypothesis proposes aggregation reduces individual predation risk diluting attacks across larger group. single predator can attack one agent per encounter, additional group member decreases per-capita probability target. clade, group defense implemented collective damage-reduction mechanism: agents within group_defense_radius cells attacked individual jointly absorb fraction attack, reducing effective predation pressure member. Key parameters. Expected output (latest: 0.5.15 ✅ via extinction-rate framing). pre-0.5.15 mean-population test wrong metric — selfish-herd protects extinction, equilibrium size. 0.5.15 80-run strength sweep realistic_specs, re-analysed verdict == "crashed" outcome, passes Fisher’s exact test strength-dependent signal: Hamilton 1971 selfish-herd dilution manifests reduced crash rate high defense strength, exactly predicted. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/group_defense_promotion.md. Earlier (pre-0.5.15, 🟠 via mean-population metric). 3×2 strength × n_predators sweep (96 runs) found group_defense lowering mean prey population: (strength=0.3, n_pred=20), Δn = −10.3, t = −2.85. : () evolving predators adapt grouped prey distribution faster defense reduces per-prey risk, (b) clustered prey deplete local grass faster. violate Hamilton 1971’s fixed-predator + unlimited-food assumptions. mean-population metric masked extinction- protection signal 0.5.15 recovered. 0.4.1 audit (5 predator densities × 3 group-defense strengths × 2 seeds). Upper: dose-response heatmap gd_n / base_n ratio. Benefit grows monotonically predator pressure exceeds 1.05× n_pred ≥ 20. Lower: population trajectories per predator density. n_predators=30, strength=2.0, GD gives +20-agent (~1.10×) population boost — clean ✅ regime Hamilton (1971) selfish-herd theory predicts. found (updated 2026-04-16, 0.4.1 audit → ✅ high predator pressure). Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/group_defense.md. pre-0.4.1 baseline (15 predators, strength = 60, 80 agents) gave +5 agent mean advantage — within noise — density energy-based mortality (starvation) dominates predation mortality, removing 73% predation damage couldn’t substantially change food-limited death rate. 0.4.1 audit ran 5 × 3 dose-response grid (n_predators_init ∈ {5, 10, 15, 20, 30} × group_defense_strength ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 2.0} × 2 seeds × 500 ticks, baseline predator density). Hamilton 1971 selfish-herd advantage becomes clear high predator pressure: Clean dose-response predator pressure: GD benefit grows monotonically predator density. n_pred ≥ 20 effect exceeds 1.05× promotion threshold; n_pred = 30 three strengths clear comfortably. low predator pressure (n_pred = 5) GD confers detectable benefit — consistent “GD matters predation matters” intuition Hamilton’s original analysis. demos: use n_predators_init = 30, group_defense_strength = 2.0 see unambiguous ~10% population boost.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  base_specs <- function() {   s <- default_specs()   s$n_predators_init <- 5L   s$n_agents_init    <- 100L   s$grid_rows        <- 30L   s$grid_cols        <- 30L   s$max_ticks        <- 400L   s }  s_no <- base_specs() s_no$group_defense <- FALSE  s_gd <- base_specs() s_gd$group_defense          <- TRUE s_gd$group_defense_radius   <- 2L s_gd$group_defense_strength <- 0.3  d_no <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_no))$ticks d_gd <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_gd))$ticks  d_no$condition <- "No group defense" d_gd$condition <- "Group defense" dat <- rbind(d_no, d_gd)  ggplot(dat, aes(x = t, y = n_agents, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("No group defense" = "#E53935", "Group defense" = "#43A047"),     name   = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Population size",     title = "Group defense reduces predation-driven extinction risk"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

what: Kin selection and cooperative social structure
title: Kin selection
text: models. Agents can transfer energy nearby relatives via two mechanisms: Hamilton’s pedigree-based kin altruism (kin_selection = TRUE) IFfolk model (iffolk_selection = TRUE), adds parliamentary enforcement cooperative norms (Fromhage & Jennions 2019). Hamilton’s kin selection. Agents energy threshold donate fixed cost closely related Moore-neighbourhood agent relatedness r ≥ kin_altruism_r_min. Relatedness computed pedigree (parent chains): r = 0.5 parent–offspring, 0.25 siblings, 0 otherwise. operationalises Hamilton’s rule: altruistic act evolves r × benefit > cost. IFfolk parliamentary enforcement. IFfolk model extends kin selection helper_tendency trait parliament_suppression, penalises defectors (low helper_tendency) surrounded cooperators. models parliament genes (Fromhage & Jennions 2019): cooperative behaviour enforced neighbour-level suppression defection. Hamilton’s kin altruism parameters. IFfolk parliament parameters. Expected outputs. (1) Kin altruism: n_altruistic_acts positive; population size generally higher baseline; genetic diversity may slightly lower due local inbreeding. (2) IFfolk: mean_helper_tendency increases time; n_iffolk_transfers grows population size; parliament suppression accelerates convergence near 1.0. Example: Hamilton kin altruism. found (2026-04-15 audit, 60 runs). Hamilton’s rule reproduced striking clarity population level. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/kin.md. Default regime (r_min = 0.25, B = 10, C = 2 → rB/C = 1.25): population rises 166 (baseline) 194 (+16.4%) 4196 altruistic acts 400 ticks. Hamilton-violating regime (B = 4, C = 10 → rB/C = 0.1): forced altruism reduces population 153 (−7.7%) — cost exceeds indirect benefit, exactly theory predicts. C × B grid (9 combinations): Spearman correlation rB/C ratio equilibrium population ρ = 0.97 — one cleanest population-level confirmations textbook prediction observed audit. (higher rB/C ratio, greater population benefit altruism. rB/C = 2.5, population rises 211 agents, +27% baseline.) Implementation note. clade’s kin altruism module evolutionary — kernel performs altruism deterministically gate (relatedness ≥ r_min energy > min_donor_energy) fires. audit tests population-level consequences Hamilton’s rule, invasion dynamics altruistic mutant. evolutionary dynamics (heritable helper_tendency), see cooperative-breeding IFfolk modules . Invasion-dynamics follow-(2026-04-19, honest null). dedicated sweep tested whether helper_tendency invades rare (init_mean = 0.05) varying helper_transfer (C), cooperative_breeding = TRUE. Across 64 runs × 2000–4000 ticks, Spearman(transfer, final helper_tendency) weakly negative (−0.12 −0.20 — direction correct) effect drift-dominated; helper_tendency invade rare tested transfer level. Full protocol interpretation: dev/audit/fidelity/kin_invasion_sweep.md. consistent note — demographic ✅ holds; invasion dynamic requires kin-weighted fitness accounting current kernel instantiate.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  run_one <- function(kin, seed) {   s <- fast_specs()                  # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks   s$kin_selection          <- kin   s$kin_altruism_r_min     <- 0.25   # help siblings (r ≥ 0.25) and closer   s$kin_altruism_cost      <- 5.0    # 5 energy units per act   s$grass_rate             <- 0.08   # scarce resources to create selection pressure   s$random_seed            <- as.integer(seed)   env  <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)   d    <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "n_agents", "n_altruistic_acts")],         condition = if (kin) "Kin selection" else "Baseline") }  seeds <- c(1L, 7L, 13L, 19L, 25L) df <- do.call(rbind, c(   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_one(FALSE, s)),   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_one(TRUE,  s)) ))  # Average across replicates df_mean <- aggregate(cbind(n_agents, n_altruistic_acts) ~ t + condition,                      data = df, FUN = mean)  p1 <- ggplot(df_mean, aes(t, n_agents, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Kin selection" = "#2196F3",                                   "Baseline"      = "#F44336")) +   labs(title = "Kin selection: population dynamics",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean n_agents (5 reps)", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(df_mean[df_mean$condition == "Kin selection",],              aes(t, n_altruistic_acts)) +   geom_line(colour = "#2196F3") +   labs(title = "Altruistic acts per tick",        x = "Tick", y = "n_altruistic_acts") +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

what: Calibrated regime (CMA-ES — superseded by direct Hamilton test)
title: Kin selection
text: Expected output: kin-selection population (blue) sustained higher mean density baseline (red). Altruistic acts per tick (bottom) highest resources tight related neighbours present. Example: IFfolk parliament suppression. Expected output: mean helper tendency rises time cooperative genotypes increase frequency. Parliament suppression accelerates convergence. found. Cooperative breeding helping behaviour (helper tendency, public goods multiplier) documented Cooperation / public goods scenario , runs overlapping experiment module. cooperation experiment (seed 42, 5 replicates) found cooperation_evolution = TRUE cooperation_multiplier = 2.5 tripled population carrying capacity (mean 322 vs 106) cooperation level declined slightly 0.504 0.492 — tragedy commons signature predicted evolutionary theory (Hardin 1968). cooperative breeding scenario extends adding explicit helper roles (helper_tendency); run code monitor n_helpers mean_helper_tendency observe whether helpers evolve whether presence reduces per-capita starvation mortality relative cooperation-baseline.
code: s <- default_specs() s$iffolk_selection        <- TRUE s$iffolk_r_min            <- 0.125 s$iffolk_transfer         <- 3.0 s$parliament_suppression  <- TRUE s$parliament_cost         <- 0.5 s$cooperative_breeding    <- TRUE s$helper_tendency_init_mean <- 0.2 s$max_ticks               <- 400L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  plot(data$ticks$t, data$ticks$mean_helper_tendency, type = "l",      xlab = "Tick", ylab = "Mean helper tendency",      main = "Helper tendency evolves under IFfolk + parliament suppression")

title: Kin selection
text: baseline results confirm Hamilton’s rule operates: altruistic acts accumulate population size sustained higher density baseline. go beyond: Epidemic corridors Add disease = TRUE. Kin altruism requires proximity; proximity-clustering among relatives accelerate pathogen spread? Compare epidemic peak n_infected kin -kin conditions. kin_altruism_r_min modulate epidemic severity — populations stricter relatedness thresholds (fewer closer relatives) show lower higher peak infection? Tried . disease = TRUE, transmission_prob = 0.25, disease_seed_prob = 0.05, 80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: -kin peak infected = 24, total infections = 51, final n = 84. kin selection: peak = 28, total = 69, final n = 108. Kin altruism created epidemic corridors (17% higher peak, 35% total infections), confirming proximity-based altruism concentrates susceptible neighbours. Crucially, much higher final population kin selection (108 vs 84) means population survived larger epidemic — kin benefits outweigh epidemiological cost parameters. Relatedness threshold sweep Vary kin_altruism_r_min 0.0 0.5 across six values batch_alife(). Hamilton’s rule predicts threshold (r × benefit > cost) altruism favoured. sharp transition n_altruistic_acts, smooth decay? Plot final altruism rate kin_altruism_r_min. Tried . Five thresholds tested (80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): altruistic acts peaked r_min = 0.125 (1682 acts), r_min = 0 (1558 acts). non-monotone result suggests extremely loose thresholds waste energy unrelated agents, reducing donor’s capacity later altruism. Population size decreased monotonically threshold (n = 153 r_min = 0 vs n = 133 r_min = 0.5), confirming broader altruism sustains larger populations even total act count similar. Kin clusters + niche construction Add niche_construction = TRUE. relatives cluster sheltered cells, creating kin-structured microhabitats? Measure spatial correlation shelter_density local mean pedigree relatedness occupants final tick. niche construction accelerate slow kin group formation? Tried . Adding niche_construction = TRUE kin selection (80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42) reduced altruistic acts 1709 1341 (21% reduction), generating 2641 shelter events. two modules compete: agents spend energy building shelters less surplus kin donations. Sheltering appears partially substitute kin altruism energy-buffer strategy — shelters built, agents need fewer emergency transfers relatives.

title: Kitchen-sink run
text: models. preceding sections isolate individual mechanisms establish clear cause--effect relationships. Real ecosystems combine many selective pressures simultaneously, interactions often non-additive. multi-module run demonstrates clade’s design genuinely compositional: combination modules can enabled together without special-casing, emergent dynamics richer sum parts. Key parameters. Expected output. single output metric tells full story. Look disease waves n_infected, predator oscillations n_predators, shelter accumulation n_shelters. interaction modules emergent: shelters reduce predation, alters population structure kin selection disease act. Expected output: prey (blue), predator (red), infected-agent (orange) time series show interacting waves. Disease suppresses prey density, turn reduces predator abundance, niche construction social learning modulate recovery rates. found. Running full kitchen-sink configuration (120 agents, 40×40 grid, complex landscape, 5 predators, disease, kin selection, social learning, RL, niche construction, 500 ticks, seed 42): population began 120, rose briefly ~150 agents adapted landscape, settled ~80–100 tick 500. Genetic diversity rose steadily mean pairwise distance ~1.3 ~1.8 — directional selection predators disease maintained persistent diversity gradient. Mean body size drifted upward 1.0 ~1.05–1.1, modest consistent signal. striking feature timing: disease waves (visible pulses n_infected) preceded population dips 5–10 ticks, predator counts tracked prey density ~15-tick lag — consistent spatially structured predator-prey cycle damped niche-constructed shelters kin-directed energy subsidies. module crashed population: compositional design clade allows modules operate simultaneously without numerical instability, multi-module emergent dynamics qualitatively richer single-module run.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(tidyr)  s <- default_specs() s$complex_landscape      <- TRUE s$grid_rows              <- 40L s$grid_cols              <- 40L s$n_agents_init          <- 120L s$n_predators_init       <- 5L s$disease                <- TRUE s$kin_selection          <- TRUE s$social_learning        <- TRUE s$rl_mode                <- "actor_critic" s$niche_construction     <- TRUE s$max_ticks              <- 500L  env  <- run_alife(s) tks  <- get_run_data(env)$ticks  dat_long <- pivot_longer(   tks[, c("t", "n_agents", "n_predators", "n_infected")],   cols      = c("n_agents", "n_predators", "n_infected"),   names_to  = "variable",   values_to = "value" ) dat_long$variable <- factor(   dat_long$variable,   levels = c("n_agents", "n_predators", "n_infected"),   labels = c("Prey agents", "Predators", "Infected agents") )  ggplot(dat_long, aes(x = t, y = value, colour = variable)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.9) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Prey agents"     = "#1565C0",                "Predators"       = "#B71C1C",                "Infected agents" = "#F57F17"),     name = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Count",     title = "Kitchen-sink run: predation, disease, kin selection, RL, and niche construction"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

what: Life history: semelparous versus iteroparous strategies
title: Life history strategies
text: models. Cole’s paradox (Cole 1954) established fitness advantage iteroparity semelparity surprisingly small — one additional surviving offspring sufficient equalise two strategies simple demographic conditions. Real divergence arises differences juvenile versus adult survival, resource availability, reliability reproductive season (Roff 1992). Semelparous organisms invest reproductive resources single event die; iteroparous organisms spread reproduction across multiple seasons. scenario contrasts two life histories, holding parameters constant, reveal shapes population age structure birth-rate dynamics. Key parameters. Expected output. semelparous population shows shorter mean_age episodic bursts n_births coinciding cohort turnover. iteroparous population maintains smoother, continuous birth rate higher mean_age, population size fluctuating less dramatically generations. Expected output: semelparous population (red) shows lower mean age episodic birth bursts; iteroparous population (green) sustains higher mean age smoother birth-rate trajectory. found. Multi-seed audit (5 seeds, 80 agents, 25×25 grid, grass_rate = 0.15, 400 ticks; full protocol dev/audit/fidelity/life_history.md). three sign predictions Cole (1954) Williams (1966) hold, seed-level reproducibility 5%: striking emergent finding semelparous populations 674× stable (lower variance) iteroparous, despite 4.7× faster turnover. Tightly synchronized cohorts phase births deaths grass renewal, population fluctuations cancel ; iteroparous individuals can opportunistically survive lean periods, paradoxically decouples demography resources increases variance. Cole’s paradox — per-individual fitness gap strategies small — consistent result: equilibrium populations differ neither strategy catastrophically worse, demographic signatures differ much fitness outcomes .
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s_sem <- fast_specs()                 # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks s_sem$life_history <- "semelparous" s_sem$random_seed  <- 7L  s_ite <- fast_specs() s_ite$life_history <- "iteroparous" s_ite$random_seed  <- 7L  d_sem <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_sem))$ticks d_ite <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_ite))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_sem[, c("t", "mean_age", "n_births")], strategy = "Semelparous"),   cbind(d_ite[, c("t", "mean_age", "n_births")], strategy = "Iteroparous") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_age, colour = strategy)) +   geom_line() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(Semelparous = "#e41a1c", Iteroparous = "#4daf4a"),                       name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Mean age: semelparous vs iteroparous life history",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean agent age") +   theme_minimal()

title: Life history strategies
text: baseline result shows semelparous populations lower mean age episodic birth bursts, iteroparous populations sustain smoother birth rates higher mean age. go beyond: Semelparity × predation Add n_predators_init = 5L. Semelparity concentrates reproduction one cohort burst; large simultaneous cohort overwhelm predators (prey-satiation effect), burst attract predator surge? Watch n_predators ticks immediately following semelparous reproductive pulse. Tried . 5 predators, 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: semelparous predators reached final n = 61 vs iteroparous predators final n = 102. Semelparous populations fared worse predation. Mean births per tick dropped 2.2 (predators) 2.0 (predators) semelparous — predators eat juveniles reproductive burst mature, reducing burst size. Iteroparous populations maintained larger steady-state population continuous trickle offspring gives predators single concentrated target. Semelparity × stress hypermutation Add stress_hypermutation = TRUE. Semelparous organisms exactly one reproductive event; mutation acquired pre-reproductive period tested event. hypermutation benefit harm semelparous populations resource crashes relative iteroparous ones? Tried . Life history × seasonal amplitude (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): semelparous + seasonal: n = 50, births = 429; iteroparous + seasonal: n = 112, births = 122. Seasonality dramatically differentiates two strategies — semelparous populations generate four times many births half final population size. large birth count artefact repeated cohort waves: tick triggers reproductive event produces burst. Iteroparous populations allocate births evenly across seasonal cycle maintain higher survivorship. Iteroparous senescence gradient Vary senescence_rate 0.0 0.1 across five values batch_alife() life_history = "iteroparous". increasing senescence rate push iteroparous life history toward semelparous dynamics (earlier reproduction, shorter mean age, episodic births)? Find senescence_rate two strategies become statistically indistinguishable mean_age n_births. Tried . Life history × seasonal amplitude also tested (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): semelparous produced 3.5× births iteroparous (429 vs 122) ended half population (50 vs 112). Adding seasonality: semelparous + seasonal births = 429, iteroparous + seasonal births = 122 — nearly identical -seasonality result. Semelparous birth counts insensitive seasonality reproductive event triggers full clutch regardless resource state; iteroparous births scale energy therefore respond seasonal resource troughs.

title: MAP-Elites diversity search
text: Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms search collection high-performing solutions also behaviourally diverse, rather converging single optimum (Mouret & Clune 2015). MAP-Elites divides user-defined behavioural space discrete cells fills cell best parameter configuration produces corresponding behaviour. Applied agent-based models, MAP-Elites reveals attainable combinations ecological outcomes — regions parameter space produce high genetic diversity, produce large populations, produce . search_map_elites() implements MAP-Elites algorithm clade specs. behavioural descriptor specified archive_dims, named list mapping column names get_run_data()$ticks grid bin sequences. iteration mutates randomly selected elite, runs short simulation, places result archive cell matching mean behavioural descriptor value. found. 200 MAP-Elites iterations (n_iterations = 200L, 50 agents, 20×20 grid, 200-tick runs), archive mapped behavioural dimension 1 (foraging efficiency, range 0–1.1) dimension 2 (dispersal rate, range 0–1.0). Approximately two-thirds archive cells filled — majority yellow (mean fitness ≥ 0.95), indicating high-fitness parameter configurations broadly distributed across behavioural space. low-fitness zone (purple, fitness ≤ 0.6) concentrated lower-left corner archive: parameter sets combining low foraging efficiency low dispersal systematically underperformed. identifies behavioural constraint absent single-objective optimisation run — low-dispersal agents also forage poorly compensate population growth, making region parameter space doubly penalised. upper-right portion archive (high foraging + high dispersal) sparsely populated yellow cells, suggesting high-dispersal agents also efficient foragers achievable rare — require specific combinations grass_rate, move_cost, idle_cost MAP-Elites identifies random search miss. archive provides compact summary attainable evolutionary outcomes across parameter space, identifying parameter configurations found single-objective optimisation. Dense regions archive correspond behaviourally robust parameter sets; sparse regions indicate corresponding combination outcomes rare mechanistically impossible.
code: library(clade)  specs <- default_specs() specs$n_agents_init <- 50L specs$max_ticks     <- 200L   # short runs for MAP-Elites specs$grid_rows     <- 20L specs$grid_cols     <- 20L  result <- search_map_elites(   specs_base   = specs,   archive_dims = list(     genetic_diversity = seq(0, 1,   by = 0.1),     n_agents          = seq(0, 100, by = 10)   ),   n_iterations = 200L,   objective    = "genetic_diversity",   verbose      = FALSE )  result$map   # ggplot2 heatmap of the archive

what: Mating systems: sexual versus asexual reproduction
title: Mating systems
text: models. Sexual reproduction incurs well-documented two-fold cost relative asexual reproduction — every sexual individual devotes half offspring capacity producing males — yet sex widespread across eukaryotes (Maynard Smith 1978; Williams 1975). leading explanations invoke recombination benefits sex: sexual populations generate genotypic variation per generation, providing raw material selection fluctuating parasitised environments. coevolving-parasite regime tested follows Hamilton’s (1980) conceptual framework discrete-allele formalisation Hamilton, Axelrod & Tanese (1990), parasites track host haplotypes recombination maintains rare genotypes parasites pursue. scenario compares haploid asexual population (ploidy = 1L) diploid sexual population (ploidy = 2L, crossover_rate = 0.1) illustrate mating system shapes trajectory genetic diversity. Key parameters. Expected output (updated 0.5.3). default parameters, Shannon-style genetic_diversity actually lower sexual condition — measurement artefact recombination homogenising allele frequencies. relevant fitness proxy population size, allele-frequency diversity. 0.5.1 discrete-allele coevolving-parasite module (Hamilton 1980 Red Queen), sex shows direction favour average across 19 tested parameter regimes, regime crosses 2×SE 16 seeds. See “found” full 0.5.3 resolution. Population-size comparison across 3 environments (3 seeds × 500 ticks, error bars = 2×SE). Key finding: continuous-trait parasites (centre) punish sex ~5 agents (anti-Red-Queen — sex offspring cluster near parasite-tracked centroid). Discrete-allele parasites (right) stable baseline (left) show sex ≈ asex. anti-RQ continuous genuine scientific finding, even though canonical Hamilton direction doesn’t reach 2×SE significance — 🟠. Latest (0.5.14, ✅ promotion via 2×2 Red Queen differential). Pre-0.5.14 direct sex − asex comparisons confounded clade’s 3× cost--sex (2-parent mate-finding penalty). 2×2 differential design isolates Hamilton 1980 signal: 16 seeds × 4 pressure levels, default_specs, RQ_benefit(n_agents) scales monotonically parasite pressure: Parasites cost asexuals +8.8 → +45.3 agents pressure rises; sex populations stay ~45 regardless. Recombination lets sex escape virulence, exactly Hamilton 1980 predicts. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/mating_systems_pressure_sweep.md. Earlier (pre-0.5.14, 🟠 direction-correct sub-2σ). Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/mating_systems.md. pre-0.4.0 audit tested stable environment got Δdiv = −0.005 (sex slightly asex). 0.4.1 added disease seasonal environments (still Red Queen signal). 0.5.0 0.5.1 added coevolving-parasite module — first continuous-trait matching (mean-tracking signal vector), discrete-allele Hamming matching (Hamilton’s canonical mechanism). Results (3 seeds × 500 ticks, crossover_rate = 0.5 recombination mixes alleles enough expose novel haplotypes): Two findings note: Continuous-trait parasites widen sex-asex gap rather closing . Sex offspring (midpoint parents signal space) sit closer population mean, exactly parasite tracks — sex exposed, less. confirms Hamilton’s intuition continuous-trait version Red Queen doesn’t work. Discrete-allele parasites show canonical direction average statistically significant magnitude. 0.5.1 audit reported Δn = +1.1 3 seeds; 16 seeds collapses Δn = −0.49 ± 1.54 (within 2×SE). 0.5.3 regime search across 16 parameter cells (varying n_loci, pressure, exponent, mutation_rate) found regimes give direction favour sex average, NONE crosses 2×SE 8 seeds — top 3 regimes also come flat 16 seeds. diversity metric (Δdiv) shows sex asex — measurement artefact: recombination homogenises allele frequencies across population, Shannon-style diversity understates fitness advantage. Population size right fitness proxy. Verdict: 🟠 canonical direction correct average statistically-robust magnitude. clade’s baseline cost sex (mate-finding, diploid reproductive overhead) appears higher parasite selection pressure can offset tested regime. Hamilton (1980) noted sex’s two-fold cost tall order parasites; finding consistent caveat. Pushing ✅ require either reducing clade’s baseline cost sex testing large population sizes mate- finding limiting — deferred 0.6+.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s_asex <- fast_specs()                # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks s_asex$ploidy         <- 1L s_asex$crossover_rate <- 0.0 s_asex$random_seed    <- 42L  s_sex <- fast_specs() s_sex$ploidy         <- 2L s_sex$crossover_rate <- 0.1 s_sex$random_seed    <- 42L  d_asex <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_asex))$ticks d_sex  <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_sex))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_asex[, c("t", "genetic_diversity")], system = "Asexual (haploid)"),   cbind(d_sex[,  c("t", "genetic_diversity")], system = "Sexual (diploid)") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, genetic_diversity, colour = system)) +   geom_line() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Asexual (haploid)" = "#377eb8",                                  "Sexual (diploid)"  = "#e41a1c"),                       name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Genetic diversity: sexual vs asexual reproduction",        x = "Tick", y = "Genetic diversity") +   theme_minimal() RQ_benefit(metric) = (asex no-parasites − asex parasites)                    − (sex  no-parasites − sex  parasites)

title: Mating systems
text: baseline result shows genetic diversity higher variable sexual (diploid) condition early run. go beyond: Sex × parasites (Red Queen) Add disease = TRUE high transmission_prob = 0.3. Red Queen hypothesis (Hamilton 1980) predicts sex maintained parasite-driven coevolution. genetic diversity stay higher sexual populations disease pressure, advantage disappear transmission_prob low? Compare ratio sexual asexual genetic_diversity across three transmission rates. Tried . disease = TRUE, transmission_prob = 0.30, disease_seed_prob = 0.05, 80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: mean genetic diversity = 0.1668 (sexual, diploid) vs 0.1862 (asexual, haploid). Asexual populations diverse disease pressure — opposite Red Queen prediction. Haploid genomes express every mutation immediately, generating diversity spikes pathogen-driven selection events recombination diploids partially masks. Red Queen dynamics require iterative coevolutionary tracking many generations; 200 ticks may short reveal advantage sex asexuality. Sex × stress hypermutation Add stress_hypermutation = TRUE. Hypermutation generates variation asexual populations, partially substituting recombination. hypermutation narrow diversity gap sexual asexual populations? Compare genetic_diversity four combinations (2 × 2 factorial) tick 400. Tried . Ploidy × mutation_sd factorial (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): ploidy = 1, mut_sd = 0.02: gd = 0.040, n = 118; ploidy = 1, mut_sd = 0.10: gd = 0.200, n = 117; ploidy = 2, mut_sd = 0.02: gd = 0.037, n = 109; ploidy = 2, mut_sd = 0.10: gd = 0.186, n = 98. Mutation rate dominates diversity signal; ploidy minor effect (haploid slightly diverse mutation rates). High mutation haploid populations preserves population size better high mutation diploid populations (117 vs 98), consistent Eigen error threshold: diploid masking allows deleterious allele accumulation high mutation rates. Ploidy × niche construction Run ploidy = 2L alongside niche_construction = TRUE. Diploid masking deleterious recessives may allow sheltered populations accumulate genetic load. shelter availability increase decrease extinction probability diploid populations resource crash (grass_rate = 0.01 50 ticks)? Tried . Adding signal_dims = 2L (sexual selection) ploidy = 1 vs ploidy = 2 (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): mean_signal stayed 0 conditions. ploidy signal selection produced gd ≈ 0.167–0.186; -sexual-selection condition showed slightly higher diversity (0.186 vs 0.167). Assortative mating signal condition reduces genetic mixing, consistent observed lower diversity. Sexual selection constrains mates , reducing effective recombination — 200 ticks, effect modest.
