title: Mimicry and toxicity
text: models. heritable toxicity trait makes prey costly attack. Predators learn signal–toxicity associations via Rescorla-Wagner rule build avoidance. models evolution warning coloration Batesian/ Müllerian mimicry (Ruxton et al. 2004): toxic prey avoided; non-toxic prey resemble toxic prey also avoided (Batesian mimicry). Key parameters. Expected output (primary claim, post-2026-04-17 reframe). scenario conditionally 🟠 — aposematism evolves clade predation-dominant ecology, grass scarce (grass_rate ≈ 0.08) predation accounts ~89% mortality rather ~20%. default well-fed ecology, aposematism evolve, Zahavi handicap cost exceeds predation benefit (see “ecology-limited” Grafen/Számadó/Getty theoretical background). vignette now leads predation-dominant regime configuration clade reproduces textbook positive Δtoxicity; default-ecology result documented limit condition. 0.4.4 kernel fixes (vector-signal memory, delta-rule RW, aposematic pleiotropy): P1 (mean toxicity rises predation-dominant ecology): +0.006 600 ticks grass_rate = 0.08, vs −0.001 grass_rate = 0.20 (default well-fed). P3 (avoidance mechanism fires): PASS, 12–28 events per 600 ticks. P4 (positive toxin_dose dose-response): PASS, Spearman ρ = +0.40 across dose sweep. See mimicry fidelity report full evidence. Status: ✅ passed (conditional, grass_rate = 0.08) — promoted 2026-04-18 consistency conditional claims classified (s-dispersal-ifd strength = 2.0, s-social-learning freq = 50). canonical Bates/Müller prediction reproduced predation-dominant ecology; default well-fed ecology doesn’t support handicap equilibrium theoretically expected (Grafen 1990; Getty 2006; Számadó 2011), clade bug. Pre-0.4.4 kernel simplification (historical). 0.4.4, clade’s predator memory scalar updated toward prey.toxicity directly, removed signal-specific learning — predators couldn’t recognise warning patterns. 0.4.4 refactor replaced full vector-signal memory using Widrow-Hoff delta rule. See “found” details.
code: # Predation-dominant ecology — the regime where aposematism evolves. # At default grass_rate = 0.20, the Zahavi handicap cost exceeds the # protection benefit and toxicity stays flat. Lowering grass to 0.08 # flips the cost-benefit ratio in toxicity's favour. # # Note: this scenario uses default_specs() rather than fast_specs(). # fast_specs() + grass_rate = 0.08 crashes the prey population to # near-extinction — fast_specs shortens prey lifespan, and stacking # predation-dominant ecology on top of that overwhelms viability. # The default_specs timescale (generation ~190 ticks) gives the # population time to persist under heavy predation. s <- default_specs() s$mimicry            <- TRUE s$n_predators_init   <- 5L s$toxicity_init_mean <- 0.1 s$grass_rate         <- 0.08          # predation-dominant ecology s$max_ticks          <- 1000L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)

what: Calibrated regime (formerly CMA-ES discovered — claim retracted)
title: Mimicry and toxicity
text: Ecology comparison (5 seeds × 1000 ticks, full 0.4.4 machinery). Top: toxicity trajectories standard ecology (grass=0.20, grey) vs predation-dominant ecology (grass=0.08, orange). Predation-dominant preserves toxicity predation becomes main mortality cause. Bottom: cumulative avoidance events — predator learning fires ecologies (~30 events). mechanism works; magnitude depends ecology — 🟠. found (updated 2026-04-16, audit 🟠 0.4.4 kernel fixes). Two audit iterations materially changed picture: Pre-0.4.0 (scalar-predator memory): 15–67 toxic attacks vs ~700 total attacks (2.5–10%), 0–9 avoidance events total across 600 ticks; mean toxicity essentially locked init value. scalar memory wired correctly never crossed threshold averaged prey.toxicity (prey.signal), signal-specific learning couldn’t occur. 0.4.0 Tier 4 + 0.4.4 refactor (vector-signal memory + delta-rule Rescorla-Wagner + aposematic pleiotropy). kernel now: Stores dedicated signal_memory::Vector{Float32} field predator — linear model predicts toxicity signal vector. Updates memory symmetric Widrow-Hoff delta rule: memory += lr × (tox − dot(memory, signal)) × signal. Reinforcement toxic prey, extinction non-toxic prey → Batesian breakdown palatable mimics outnumber models. Supports optional aposematic pleiotropy (signal_toxicity_coupling > 0): signal[1] tracks toxicity predators can learn honest warning signal. Post-fix measurements (5 seeds × 600 ticks, signal_dims = 3, audit measurement bug also fixed — per-tick counters now summed cumulatively, sampled last tick): P3 FAIL → PASS: 12–28 cumulative avoidance events (spurious 0 reported measurement bug). P4 FAIL → PASS: Spearman ρ(toxin_dose, final toxicity) = +0.40 — expected positive dose-response. P5 PASS (new): pleiotropy sweep signal_toxicity_coupling ∈ {0, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0} shows monotone direction (ρ = +1.0). P2 (treatment > control) remains direction-sensitive ±0.002 noise band — toxicity magnitude evolution still small ecological parameters (predator encounter rate vs toxicity cost) don’t give strong selection differential. Verdict: 🟠 substantially richer kernel semantics. machinery now theoretically aligned Bates (1862) / Müller (1879); magnitude upward toxicity evolution limited ecology, predator-learning channel. ecology-limited — Zahavi handicap critique. toxicity trait acts Zahavi (1975) handicap: costly honest signal unpalatability. Grafen (1990, J. Theor. Biol. 144:517) formalised Zahavi’s verbal argument signalling equilibrium marginal fitness cost signalling must cheaper high-quality signallers low-quality ones. series later critiques — Getty (2006), Számadó (2011), TREE review Számadó & Penn (2015) — showed handicap equilibrium requires quite specific cost-benefit structures. particular, cost must scale differentially quality; cost flat across population (toxicity_cost_per_tick), equilibrium condition met honest signalling robustly evolve. means clade’s weak upward toxicity evolution default ecology theoretical limitation handicap model , kernel bug. Zahavi/Grafen problem showing ABM scale. 0.5.4 calibration found predation-dominant ecology (predation accounts ~89% mortality) produces clear positive Δtoxicity — regime, cost-benefit ratio inverts handicap-cost dominated survival gain avoiding predation. matches theoretical critique predicts: handicap honesty emerges environment makes differential benefit large enough exceed flat cost. Batesian mode. Set s$batesian_mimicry <- TRUE let palatable mimics (toxicity = 0) share learned aversion. 0.4.4 delta rule, non-toxic encounters now drive memory extinction, mimic frequency regulates (Batesian breakdown mimics outnumber models).

title: Mimicry and toxicity
text: baseline result shows mean_toxicity evolves upward predator pressure n_avoided_attacks increases predators learn. go beyond: Mimicry × kin selection Add kin_selection = TRUE. Kin clusters may accelerate warning coloration evolution (Hamilton’s genetical theory aposematism). kin structure speed slow evolution mean_toxicity? Compare mean_toxicity trajectories three conditions: kin, kin without predators, kin predators mimicry. Tried . mimicry = TRUE, toxicity_init_mean = 0.3, 5 predators, 80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: -kin mean_toxicity = 0.304, final_n = 61; kin mean_toxicity = 0.300, final_n = 96. Kin selection negligible effect evolved toxicity (0.304 vs 0.300) dramatically boosted population size (+57%). Kin clusters provide energy buffers allow agents survive regardless toxicity — survival advantage kin altruism much larger aposematism benefit parameters. Mimicry × disease Add disease = TRUE. High-toxicity prey produce costly defensive compounds simultaneously paying disease_energy_cost infected. disease suppress evolution costly toxicity pushing agents energy deficit infection? Compare final mean_toxicity disease vs -disease matched predator_attack_strength. Tried . Four toxicity_init_mean levels (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8; 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): final toxicity tracked initial closely (0.108, 0.296, 0.492, 0.792) upward drift — toxicity heritable selection strong enough drive upward 200-tick runs. Avoided attacks remained 0 conditions, confirming predator avoidance learning requires longer runs. Population size declined higher toxicity (99, 95, 75, 68 agents), toxin production cost reduces energy available foraging, imposing demographic cost without compensating survival benefit run lengths. Signal honesty toxicity Add signal_dims = 2L. heritable ornamental signals co-evolve toxicity (honest Müllerian signalling) become decoupled (Batesian dishonest copying non-toxic agents)? Plot mean_toxicity mean_signal_magnitude parametric trajectory test whether correlation strengthens weakens evolutionary time. Tried . mimicry = TRUE kin_selection = TRUE (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): mean_toxicity = 0.018, avoided attacks = 0. Combining kin selection mimicry low toxicity_init_mean (near 0) produced negligible toxicity evolution predator avoidance. kin benefit dominated population dynamics (larger final n mimicry alone), mimicry signal-toxicity coevolution requires higher initial toxicity longer runs honest-signal dynamics emerge.

title: Module comparison
text: models. Biological systems shaped multiple interacting selective pressures, individual contributions difficult disentangle nature. scenario applies one-module---time factorial design: condition activates exactly one module shared baseline, three summary statistics — mean genetic diversity, mean population size, mean energy — recorded across full run. Comparing modules isolation combining allows module’s unique contribution identified; -condition reveals emergent interactions predicted single-module results alone. Key parameters. Expected output. Modules imposing strong directional selection — predators disease — tend increase genetic diversity maintaining selective gradient; modules smooth energy acquisition — niche construction, social learning — may reduce within-population variance. -condition typically reveals non-additive interactions: cooperation kin selection can partially compensate disease mortality, niche construction can buffer energetic cost predation evasion. Expected output: horizontal bar chart mean genetic diversity across 14 conditions. Conditions imposing strong directional selection (predators, disease, mimicry) appear near top; -condition reveals emergent multi-module interactions. found. dashboard shows -condition tick 500 500-tick run (120 agents, 30×30 grid, 5 predators, disease, kin selection, social learning, RL, niche construction, body size evolution active simultaneously): 362 agents survived, 758 total deaths, mean body size evolved 1.0 approximately 1.3. Genetic diversity rose steadily throughout run; lifespan distribution shows majority agents dying age, starvation — indicating population remained energetically healthy despite combined selective load. Across single-module conditions, predators disease produce strongest directional selection (highest mean genetic diversity), social learning niche construction smooth energy gradients reduce within-population variance. -condition simply sum effects: cooperation kin selection partially compensate disease mortality, niche-constructed shelters buffer energetic cost predator evasion, producing population larger diverse predator-disease-conditions.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_specs <- function(...) {   s <- default_specs()   s$max_ticks <- 300L   args <- list(...)   for (nm in names(args)) s[[nm]] <- args[[nm]]   s }  specs_list <- list(   baseline    = make_specs(),   predators   = make_specs(n_predators_init = 5L),   disease     = make_specs(disease = TRUE),   kin         = make_specs(kin_selection = TRUE),   niche       = make_specs(niche_construction = TRUE),   rl          = make_specs(rl_mode = "actor_critic"),   social      = make_specs(social_learning = TRUE),   body_size   = make_specs(body_size_evolution = TRUE),   dispersal   = make_specs(dispersal_evolution = TRUE),   care        = make_specs(parental_care = TRUE),   mimicry     = make_specs(mimicry = TRUE, n_predators_init = 5L),   landscape   = make_specs(complex_landscape = TRUE),   cooperation = make_specs(cooperation_evolution = TRUE),   all_on      = make_specs(     n_predators_init      = 5L,     disease               = TRUE,     kin_selection         = TRUE,     niche_construction    = TRUE,     rl_mode               = "actor_critic",     social_learning       = TRUE,     body_size_evolution   = TRUE,     dispersal_evolution   = TRUE,     cooperation_evolution = TRUE   ) )  results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 4L)  summary_df <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, nm) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     condition      = nm,     mean_diversity = mean(d$genetic_diversity, na.rm = TRUE),     mean_pop       = mean(d$n_agents,          na.rm = TRUE),     mean_energy    = mean(d$mean_energy,       na.rm = TRUE)   ) }, results, names(results), SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  ggplot(summary_df,        aes(x = reorder(condition, mean_diversity), y = mean_diversity)) +   geom_col(fill = "#4dac26") +   coord_flip() +   labs(     title = "Mean genetic diversity by module condition",     x     = NULL,     y     = "Mean genetic diversity"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Niche construction
text: models. Agents build shelter current cell energy exceeds shelter_min_energy. Shelters reduce predator damage slow grass regrowth cell. Shelters decay stochastically tick. models ecosystem engineering: organisms modify selective environment (Odling-Smee et al. 2003). Key parameters. Expected output. n_shelters_built positive. Predator damage reduced sheltered cells. Population size may larger baseline due shelter protection. Heritable-feedback finding (2026-04-19 ✅ shelter_occupancy_bonus > 0). canonical Odling-Smee et al. 2003 claim — niche construction creates heritable environmental feedback rewarding builders’ lineage — tested sweeping shelter_occupancy_bonus across {0, 1, 3, 5} 8 seeds niche_construction = TRUE + 5 predators. Key result 40 runs: Spearman(bonus, final_n) = +0.863. Notably, bonus = 0 gives lower population (179.9) -construction control (188.3) — shelters slow grass regrowth, construction mechanism alone net cost. heritable feedback (bonus > 0) flips niche construction private cost large population-level benefit, exactly Odling-Smee et al. 2003 argue. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/niche_heritable_feedback_sweep.md.
code: s <- default_specs() s$niche_construction <- TRUE s$n_predators_init   <- 5L s$max_ticks          <- 300L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env) cat("Total shelters built:", sum(data$ticks$n_shelters_built), "\n")

title: Pace-of-life syndromes
text: models. Pace--life syndromes (Réale et al. 2010) describe co-variation life-history traits, behaviour, physiology along slow–fast continuum. Fast-living organisms high metabolic rates, reproduce early often, die young; slow-living organisms invest maintenance, defer reproduction, achieve longer lifespans. Stearns (1992) formalised consequence age-specific mortality schedule: high extrinsic mortality selects early reproduction fast metabolism. scenario fixes metabolic rate across three values isolate direct effect metabolic pace age–reproduction–energy trade-. Key parameters. Expected output. Fast-pace agents (metabolic_rate_init_mean = 2.0) exhibit higher n_births per tick, lower mean_age, higher energy throughput, populations volatile. Slow-pace agents (metabolic_rate_init_mean = 0.5) show lower birth rate, higher mean_age, stable mean_energy. Fast agents dominate productive environments; slow agents persist reliably resources lean. Audit history. pre-0.4.0 audit found metrics flat (mean_age ≈ 98 regardless rate) hardcoded max_age = 200 cap dominated age schedule. 0.4.0 Tier 2 fix (max_age_scales_with_metabolism = TRUE) makes eff_max_age = max_age / metabolic_rate, cleanly recovers Réale et al. signal: Spearman(rate, mean_age) = −1.00 across 5 rates. Note: metabolic_rate_evolution must TRUE read init_mean — FALSE, trait hardcoded 1.0. Set mutation_sd = 0 freeze rate init value still reading spec. See dev/audit/fidelity/pace_of_life.md.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  # NOTE: metabolic_rate_evolution must be TRUE to read init_mean # (when FALSE, metabolic_rate is hardcoded to 1.0). Set mutation_sd = 0 # to freeze the rate at its initial value (no evolution, pure isolation). make_run <- function(rate, seed = 99L) {   s <- default_specs()   s$metabolic_rate_evolution      <- TRUE    # needed to read init_mean   s$metabolic_rate_init_mean      <- rate   s$metabolic_rate_mutation_sd    <- 0.0     # freeze: no evolution   s$max_age_scales_with_metabolism <- TRUE   # 0.4.0 Tier 2: max_age ∝ 1/rate   s$n_agents_init <- 100L   s$grass_rate    <- 0.12   s$max_ticks     <- 500L   s$random_seed   <- seed   get_run_data(run_alife(s))$ticks }  d_slow <- make_run(0.5) d_mid  <- make_run(1.0) d_fast <- make_run(2.0)  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_slow[, c("t", "mean_age", "n_agents")], pace = "Slow (0.5)"),   cbind(d_mid[,  c("t", "mean_age", "n_agents")], pace = "Moderate (1.0)"),   cbind(d_fast[, c("t", "mean_age", "n_agents")], pace = "Fast (2.0)") )  library(patchwork) p1 <- ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_age, colour = pace)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Slow (0.5)" = "#1565C0",     "Moderate (1.0)" = "#2E7D32", "Fast (2.0)" = "#E65100"), name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Slow-pace agents live 4× longer", y = "Mean age") +   theme_minimal() p2 <- ggplot(df, aes(t, n_agents, colour = pace)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Slow (0.5)" = "#1565C0",     "Moderate (1.0)" = "#2E7D32", "Fast (2.0)" = "#E65100"), name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Slow-pace sustains 5× more agents", x = "Tick", y = "n_agents") +   theme_minimal() p1 / p2

title: Pace-of-life syndromes
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenario’s parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 155.2x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. Pace--life syndrome across 5 metabolic rates × 3 seeds × 500 ticks (max_age_scales_with_metabolism = TRUE). Top: mean age ranges 205 (slow, rate=0.5) 32 (fast, rate=3.0) — 6× difference. Middle: population ranges 328 22. Bottom: energy reserves similar across rates. fast-slow continuum crystal-clear. found. Running senescence_rate = 0.05 (fast pace, repro_senescence = 0.03) vs senescence_rate = 0.001 (slow pace, reproductive senescence), 80 agents, 25×25 grid, grass_rate = 0.15, 400 ticks (3 replicates): extreme fast pace drove near-extinction (mean population 5 vs 188 slow pace). Fast-pace agents mean age 2.2 ticks (vs 85 ticks slow), mean energy 22 (vs 131 slow), total births 88 (vs 433 slow). Gompertz senescence rate 0.05/tick creates ~5% per-tick hazard rate independent current age, causing rapid population collapse offspring reach reproductive age dying. age-related deaths logged fast condition (agents died young trigger age-death pathway), whereas 245 age-deaths logged slow condition. contrast illustrates senescence_rate mild parameter: values ~0.01 impose near-lethal selection pressure. Calibrate starting senescence_rate = 0.005 increasing steps; watch mean_age confirm viable demography comparing conditions.

title: Pace-of-life syndromes
text: baseline result shows pace--life trade-: fast-pace agents higher birth rates lower mean age; slow-pace agents lower birth rates higher mean age energy stability. go beyond: Pace × disease Add disease = TRUE. High metabolic rate means fast-paced organisms less energy margin; disease_energy_cost push fast-paced agents energy deficit readily. disease selectively eliminate fast-paced phenotypes metabolic_rate_evolution = TRUE, driving evolved mean_metabolic_rate downward? Tried . metabolic_rate_evolution = TRUE, transmission_prob = 0.25, disease_seed_prob = 0.05, 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: -disease final metabolic rate = 1.004; disease = 0.988. Disease reduced evolved metabolic rate ~1.6%, consistent directional selection high-energy-burn phenotypes infection. Population size modestly lower (102 vs 106). effect small correct direction: disease imposes pace--life cost selects toward slower, energy-conservative strategies. Pace × brain size Enable metabolic_rate_evolution = TRUE brain_size_evolution = TRUE. fast-paced, high-energy agents evolve larger brains (metabolic resources available expensive brain tissue), metabolic competition prevent ? Test whether mean_brain_size mean_metabolic_rate positively negatively correlated final tick. Tried . Four starting metabolic rates tested (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): met_rate_init = 0.5: final_met = 0.503, n = 146, energy = 133; met_rate_init = 1.0: final_met = 0.999, n = 105, energy = 118; met_rate_init = 1.5: final_met = 1.471, n = 66, energy = 109; met_rate_init = 2.0: final_met = 1.978, n = 49, energy = 103. Higher metabolic rate consistently reduced population size mean energy, confirming pace--life trade-. Evolved rates deviate initial values, suggesting metabolic rate heritable strong directional selection default parameters alone. Pace × environment quality Run metabolic_rate_evolution = TRUE across grass_rate ∈ {0.02, 0.1, 0.4} batch_alife(). Theory predicts rich environments select fast pace. evolved mean_metabolic_rate increase monotonically grass_rate, non-monotone relationship (high resources remove mortality pressure fast-paced energy burn)? Tried . metabolic_rate_evolution = TRUE body_size_evolution = TRUE simultaneously (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): within-individual metabolic rate × body size correlation r = -0.834 — strongly negative. Agents higher metabolic rates evolved smaller body sizes. fast-slow syndrome expected: fast pace (high metabolism, fast burning) incompatible large somatic investment. negative correlation pace--life syndrome individual level.

title: Parental care
text: models. Offspring carried parent graduation age juvenile_independence_age (earlier reach juvenile_independence_energy). carried, juveniles receive energy parent feeding_rate per tick. parent dies, carried offspring die (obligate altriciality). models clutch-size-versus-offspring- quality trade-(Smith & Fretwell 1974) buffering role care demographic stochasticity (Clutton-Brock 1991). Note Clutton-Brock buffering: default parameters, clade reproduces graduation pathway show predicted variance reduction (see audit results ). buffering prediction holds tighter resource scarcity higher care_cost_per_tick. Key parameters. Expected output. n_juveniles positive. Per-capita offspring count may lower baseline (parents can carry fewer), juvenile survival higher. Population dynamics buffered. Expected output: juvenile count positive throughout run; per-capita offspring count lower baseline juvenile survival higher, resulting buffered population dynamics. found (2026-04-15 audit, 3 seeds × 400 ticks). Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/parental_care.md. Graduation pathway verified (P1 PASS) — juveniles persist ~1.24 care , 0 without. 0.3.0 fix confirmed working: offspring carried, fed, graduate adult population. Population-level buffering (P2: FAIL default, ✅ sweet spot). Care measurably reduce population variance default parameters (4625 vs 4548). 2026-04-19 follow-sweep tested vignette’s hypothesis — tighter resource scarcity higher care_cost_per_tick reveal Clutton-Brock’s 1991 buffering signal — found clean pass grass_rate = 0.08, care_cost_per_tick = 3.0: sweet spot, care reduces absolute population variance 58% (62.8 → 26.6). caveat Clutton-Brock’s framework doesn’t emphasise: care halves equilibrium size (mean_n 34.6 vs 117.9), CV (variance / mean) actually rises care — absolute-variance buffering coexists smaller, relatively-variable populations. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/parental_care_buffering_sweep.md. “conditional ✅” pattern s-mimicry, s-dispersal-ifd, s-mating-systems, s-niche: theory reproduces regime theory , arbitrary default.
code: s <- default_specs() s$parental_care                <- TRUE s$juvenile_independence_age    <- 5L    # was `care_duration` in docs <0.5.6 s$care_cost_per_tick           <- 2.0 s$max_ticks                    <- 300L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env) cat("Total juveniles recorded:", sum(data$ticks$n_juveniles, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")

title: Parental investment
text: models. Trivers (1972) proposed sex invests per offspring one choosy mate selection, reproductive success constrained parental effort mate access. Houston & Davies (1985) extended biparental care games, showing evolutionarily stable investment levels depend shape offspring fitness function. scenario holds parental_care = TRUE varies female_investment — fraction care energetics contributed female — examine allocation investment parents affects offspring provisioning survival. Key parameters. Expected output. High maternal investment (female_investment = 0.9) increases per-offspring energy cost, fewer births occur per tick. Equal investment (female_investment = 0.5) allows births lower per-offspring cost. trade-visible n_births n_juveniles trajectories. Trivers (1972) quality-quantity trade-: female_investment sweep (fi = 0.3 → 0.9, 3 seeds × 500 ticks). Population near-flat (~257) across fi levels parameters. quality-quantity trade-may visible n_juveniles total population; calibration needed dramatic visual. found (2026-04-15 audit, 3 seeds × 4 investment levels). Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/parental_investment.md. Across female_investment ∈ {0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9} population metrics essentially flat: births ≈ 1.5/tick, juveniles ≈ 1.5, n_agents ≈ 262, mean_energy ≈ 125. Spearman ρ(fi, births) = −0.20 (weak, significant). Trivers quality-quantity trade-reproduced current parameter couplings. mechanism wired (offspring carried, fed, graduated), female_investment ratio differentially change per-offspring quality total births enough recover Trivers’ prediction. kernel extension coupling female_investment tightly offspring graduation energy (introducing heritable mate-choice preferences) needed. Flagged 🟠 passed-consistent.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_s <- function(fi) {   # default_specs() — parental_investment at fast_specs crashes   # (n_final < 5 across seeds per crash_audit.R). Parental-care cost +   # short fast_specs lifespan overwhelms viability.   s <- default_specs()   s$parental_care                 <- TRUE   s$parental_investment_evolution <- TRUE   s$female_investment             <- fi   s$male_repro_cost               <- 0.3   s$max_ticks                     <- 400L   s$random_seed                   <- 11L   s }  d_hi <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.9)))$ticks d_eq <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.5)))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_hi[, c("t", "n_births", "n_juveniles")],         condition = "High maternal (0.9)"),   cbind(d_eq[, c("t", "n_births", "n_juveniles")],         condition = "Equal (0.5)") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, n_births, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.7) +   geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("High maternal (0.9)" = "#e41a1c", "Equal (0.5)" = "#377eb8"),     name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Births per tick by parental investment allocation",        subtitle = "High maternal investment → fewer but better-provisioned offspring",        x = "Tick", y = "Births per tick") +   theme_minimal()

title: Parental investment
text: baseline result shows quality-quantity trade-: high maternal investment produces fewer better-provisioned offspring. go beyond: Investment × brain size Add brain_size_evolution = TRUE. High parental investment creates conditions brain size evolution buffering large-brained infants bootstrapping period. female_investment = 0.9 produce higher final mean_brain_size female_investment = 0.5? interact brain_size_cost_scale? Tried . brain_size_evolution = TRUE, brain_size_cost_scale = 2.0, care_duration = 10L, 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: female_investment = 0.5 produced final brain = 1.111 (n = 89); female_investment = 0.9 produced final brain = 1.083 (n = 86). Lower maternal investment per offspring paradoxically produced brain evolution. One interpretation: 50% investment, offspring born (higher fecundity), intensifying competition selection cognitive foraging advantage. 90% investment, offspring heavily provisioned fewer — selection pressure brain size diluted smaller competing cohort. Investment × kin selection Add kin_selection = TRUE. Kin altruism provides additional energy channel offspring — may substitute direct parental investment. kin altruism allow evolution toward lower female_investment values? Compare evolved investment ratio without kin selection across 10 replicates. Tried . Four female_investment levels (0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0; 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): n = 106, 104, 106, 113; births = 115, 109, 108, 116. Population size births nearly flat across investment levels. female_investment field returned NA logged variable — Julia backend currently log evolved investment ratio — whether investment drifts selection undetectable current metrics. absence population-level signal suggests investment parameter affecting per-offspring energy total population output run lengths. Biparental game Vary female_investment 0.1 0.9 across nine values batch_alife(). evolutionarily stable investment ratio population fitness maximised, investment evolve extreme (complete uniparental care)? Plot mean_n_births × mean_energy (fitness proxy) female_investment. Tried . High investment (0.8) + disease (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): n = 110, infections = 23. Low investment (0.2) + disease: n = 105, infections = 11. High investment populations showed twice infection count (23 vs 11). Higher per-offspring investment may crowd parents higher-density configurations (reproducing slowly, remain together longer), increasing contact rates. Alternatively, high-investment parents expend energy per offspring, leaving energy-depleted thus susceptible disease mortality. direction (high investment = disease) counter naive prediction.

title: Phenotypic plasticity
text: models. heritable plasticity trait modulates strongly agent’s sensory input maps action. High plasticity agents responsive environmental cues; low plasticity agents rely heavily evolved baseline behaviour. evolution plasticity tracks environmental variability: plastic phenotypes favoured environments predictably unpredictable (DeWitt & Scheiner 2004). Key parameters. Expected output. mean_plasticity evolves upward seasonal environments stays lower (drifts) stable environments — season length ≤ agent lifetime. DeWitt- Scheiner prediction within-lifetime environmental variability: agent lives one seasonal state, genetic adaptation state just good plasticity, selection gradient. See “found” 2026-04-17 season-length sweep surfaced constraint.
code: s <- fast_specs()                     # max_age = 30, ~66 generations in 2000 ticks s$phenotypic_plasticity    <- TRUE s$plasticity_init_mean     <- 0.3 s$plasticity_mutation_sd   <- 0.05 s$bnn_sigma_source         <- "trait" s$brain_energy_sigma_scale <- 0.05 s$seasonal_amplitude       <- 0.35 s$season_length            <- 10L     # < max_age so agents see multiple seasons per life  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env) cat("Final plasticity:", tail(data$ticks$mean_plasticity, 1L), "\n")

title: Phenotypic plasticity
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenario’s parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 38.1x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. fast_specs() + season_length = 10 demo (5 seeds × 2000 ticks = 66 generations). title: Population genetics and heritability
text: models. central quantity quantitative genetics narrow-sense heritability, h² — proportion phenotypic variance trait attributable additive genetic effects (Falconer & Mackay 1996; Lynch & Walsh 1998). trait respond natural selection unless h² > 0. estimate_heritability() function performs parent-offspring regression pedigree records accumulated simulation: offspring trait values regressed mid-parent values, slope estimates h². body_size_evolution = TRUE, body size polygenic trait encoded neural genome subject mutation selection, making suitable target heritability analysis within single run. Key parameters. Expected output. h² body_size substantially positive (0.3–0.7) early run, reflecting strong additive genetic architecture freshly initialised population. directional selection erodes genetic variance, h² may decline later ticks. 95% confidence interval regression exclude zero. h² energy expected lower, energy heavily influenced local environment. mean body-size trajectory whose lag-1 autocorrelation proxies narrow-sense heritability. high lag-1 value (>0.9) consistent strong parent-offspring resemblance; direct replacement parent-offspring regression slope. found. Running body_size_evolution = TRUE, 100 agents, 200 ticks (seed 42): body size showed lag-1 autocorrelation 0.985, high heritability proxy consistent strong parent–offspring resemblance. Mean body size evolved 1.001 1.033 (+3.3%) 200 ticks, trait SD = 0.086 across run. high autocorrelation reflects mean_body_size changes smoothly ticks (one generation turns per ticks), parent–offspring regression slope fall range 0.5–0.8 computed directly pedigree records. get_run_data() returns tick-level summary; construct actual parent–offspring regression, collect agent’s body_size parent_id death env$deaths, match offspring parent body size, use lm(offspring_size ~ parent_size).
code: library(clade)  s <- fast_specs()                     # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks s$body_size_evolution <- TRUE  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  # `estimate_heritability()` returns a lag-1 autocorrelation proxy for # narrow-sense heritability on the population mean trajectory. For the # parent-offspring regression itself, use `heritability_estimate()` on # `env$deaths`, which requires a trait column logged at death. h2_proxy <- estimate_heritability(data, trait = "body_size") cat("Heritability proxy (lag-1 autocorrelation) for body_size:",     round(h2_proxy$h2, 3), "\n") cat("n =", h2_proxy$n, "  method =", h2_proxy$method, "\n")  # Mean trajectory — the quantity the proxy is computed on. plot(data$ticks$t, data$ticks$mean_body_size, type = "l",      xlab = "Tick", ylab = "Mean body size",      main = "Body size evolution (proxy for heritable response)")

title: Predation and neural evolution
text: models. Predation among strongest directional selective forces acting prey phenotypes, effects neural architecture particularly well documented empirical comparative studies. predator population present (n_predators_init > 0), prey agents faster, spatially accurate cognition survive longer leave offspring, predators evolve improved pursuit strategies selection-mutation-reproduction cycle. scenario compares two regimes — predators versus co-evolving predator guild — measures predation pressure alters distribution neural genotypes across prey population. Key parameters. Two historical claims (one holds, one retracted). scenario historically associated two separate predictions: () Demographic top-control (Williams 1966, Lotka–Volterra): predation reduces prey equilibrium population size directly mortality. claim holds realistic scale (t = −3.64 across 8 seeds × 2 conditions; see “found” ). (b) Directional-selection diversity preservation: predation maintains generates genetic variance neural genotypes imposing strong selection cognition. claim retracted realistic scale (t = −0.90, null). clade’s kernel, brain-weight diversity mutation-bounded (mutation_sd = 0.05 generates variance predation can remove), directional selection predation push diversity mutation floor. Keeping claims together overstate scenario. vignette now documents claim () primary fidelity signal; claim (b) included honestly null finding. Expected output (top panel): genetic diversity higher predation, reflecting stronger directional selection prey cognition. Bottom panel: mean energy lower predation prey pay evasion costs removed reaching peak foraging efficiency. found (2026-04-18, realistic_specs(), 8 seeds × 2 conditions, 60×60 grid, 2000 ticks). realistic-scale preset 30 predators vs predators, 16 runs viable (crashes). Two claims tested 2 σ bar: Honest conclusion. demographic claim — predation reduces prey equilibrium population size (Williams 1966, standard predator–prey theory) — robustly reproduced t = −3.64, 15% drop. older framing predation increases genetic diversity imposing directional selection cognition supported realistic scale: diversity essentially flat across conditions. clade’s kernel, brain-weight diversity appears mutation-bounded rather selection-controlled, diversity-preservation argument doesn’t emerge. promotes scenario ⚪ N/🟠 passed-consistent: demographic prediction carries primary claim, diversity prediction explicitly retracted. See dev/audit/fidelity/predation_neural.md full protocol per-seed results.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  make_s <- function(n_pred) {   s <- fast_specs()                   # ~66 generations in 2000 ticks   s$n_predators_init <- n_pred   s }  results <- batch_alife(   list(no_predators = make_s(0L), predators = make_s(10L)),   n_cores = 2L )  plot_df <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, nm) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     t                = d$t,     genetic_diversity = d$genetic_diversity,     mean_energy      = d$mean_energy,     condition        = nm   ) }, results, names(results), SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  p1 <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = t, y = genetic_diversity, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(no_predators = "#4dac26",                                  predators    = "#d01c8b")) +   labs(title = "Predation and neural genome diversity",        x = "Tick", y = "Genetic diversity", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = t, y = mean_energy, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(no_predators = "#4dac26",                                  predators    = "#d01c8b")) +   labs(x = "Tick", y = "Mean energy", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

Predation selected slightly larger brains short run. Energy stress predation mortality leaves agents less able sustain metabolic surcharge large brain; shorter expected lifespans also reduce time window cognitive returns accumulate. prediction “predation selects larger brains” requires either longer runs higher brain_size_sensing_exponent make evasion advantage outweigh metabolic cost. RL × predation Add rl_mode = "actor_critic". within-lifetime learning allow prey adapt predator patterns within generation, reducing -generation genetic diversity response? Compare genetic_diversity predation without RL — prediction RL substitutes genetic diversity adaptive mechanism. Tried . Three brain types predation (5 predators, 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): ANN: n = 66, killed = 89, energy = 127; BNN: n = 103, killed = 64, energy = 115; CTRNN: n = 57, killed = 94, energy = 134. BNN dramatically outperformed architectures predation (n = 103 vs 57–66): Bayesian uncertainty estimation allows prey conservative uncertain situations, effectively reducing engagement predator-rich zones. ANN CTRNN achieved higher mean energy per surviving agent suffered higher absolute mortality — fast--risky strategy. Social learning evasion Add social_learning = TRUE. Can prey copy evasion strategies successful survivors, reducing per-capita predation mortality without costs individual learning? Compare n_deaths predation (logged) proxy via n_agents trajectory social learning baseline conditions. Tried . Predation + RL combined (5 predators, rl_mode = "actor_critic", 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): prey n = 98, prey_killed = 67 vs predation : n = 110, prey_killed = 67. Adding RL reduced final prey population slightly (98 vs 110) total kills identical (67 conditions). RL improve survival predation — actor-critic exploration cost may increase exposure predators learning phase. RL may require ≥ 300-tick runs within-lifetime adaptation benefit outweigh exploration cost.

title: Predator-prey dynamics
text: models. Predator-prey coevolution one oldest empirically grounded problems population biology. classical Lotka-Volterra equations predict sustained sinusoidal oscillations predator abundance tracks prey abundance quarter-cycle lag (Lotka 1925; Volterra 1926). Crucially, mean-field prediction assumes fixed, mass-action predators. clade, predators carry evolving neural-network genome — learn, mutate, adapt across generations. single change enough break textbook LV signature, specific well-documented way (see “found” ). Key parameters. Expected output (evolutionary ABM, mean-field LV). Watch n_agents (prey) n_predators time calibrated parameters . Two distinct phases emerge: Bootstrap (t ≈ 0–30). Initial predators random neural genomes hunt poorly. Prey climb rapidly toward grass-imposed carrying capacity. Predators survive long enough reproduce pass slightly-better--random hunting brains. Arms-race equilibrium (t ≈ 30+). Predator brains evolve toward efficient pursuit; prey brains evolve toward avoidance. Predators saturate cap (predator_max_agents) stay even prey sparse — efficient hunters extract enough food non-zero prey density maintain cap-limited population. Prey continue oscillate (driven grass renewal × predation pressure), classic quarter-cycle predator lag textbook LV muted: predator decline phase LV requires suppressed ongoing pursuit-trait evolution. documented behaviour evolutionary predator-prey ABMs (see predator-prey fidelity report cross-reference alifeR prototype, contains explanation §12 showcase vignette). recover textbook sustained sinusoidal LV, need fixed-policy predator (yet exposed spec) non-toroidal patchy spatial structure (Huffaker 1958; future scenario). Calibrated predator-prey regime (seed 42, 1000 ticks). Predators bootstrap 10 cap 100 tick ~30 stay remainder — arms-race equilibrium. Prey climb peak ~460 tick 200, crash ~250 predator pressure builds, continue oscillating amplitude ±30 around mean ~300. classic LV quarter-cycle predator lag muted evolved predators track prey efficiently regardless density. found. calibrated parameters , seed 42, 1000 ticks: predators bootstrap 10 cap 100 tick ~30 remain saturated rest run — arms-race equilibrium, kernel limit hit (multi-seed audit confirmed runs predator_max_agents = 800 show identical saturation behaviour ~6–12 individuals energy granted kills). Prey climb 150 466 tick 200, crash 245 ticks 200–250 cap-limited predators consume faster grass renewal can replace , continue oscillating around ~300 amplitude ±30. Across 10 seeds prey oscillation score (magnitude negative autocorrelation lag-20--lag-100 range tick 100) 0.39 ± 0.14 — reproducible cycle. Predator oscillation score ≈ 0 design: evolved, track prey efficiently never enter LV decline phase. expected signature evolutionary predator-prey ABM, matches alifeR R prototype’s documented behaviour (see alifeR/vignettes/showcase.Rmd §12 — package author’s explanation evolving predators suppress textbook LV oscillations). Lotka 1925’s mean-field sustained sinusoidal result, longer claim . Full cross-reference table audit protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/predator_prey.md.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s <- default_specs() s$n_predators_init          <- 10L s$n_agents_init             <- 100L s$grid_rows                 <- 30L s$grid_cols                 <- 30L s$predator_energy_gain      <- 30 s$predator_min_repro_energy <- 50 s$predator_max_agents       <- 100L s$grass_rate                <- 0.20 s$max_ticks                 <- 1000L s$random_seed               <- 42L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  ggplot(data$ticks, aes(x = t)) +   geom_line(aes(y = n_agents,    colour = "Prey"),     linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_line(aes(y = n_predators, colour = "Predators"), linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Prey" = "#2196F3", "Predators" = "#F44336"),     name   = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Population size",     title = "Evolutionary predator-prey: prey oscillate, predators arms-race to cap"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

title: Predator-prey dynamics
text: calibrated regime reproduces documented evolutionary-ABM predator-prey signature: prey oscillate, predators saturate cap. Three open directions going beyond: Can textbook LV recovered? Lotka 1925’s sinusoidal cycles require per-capita attack rates depend density, accumulated hunting skill. Natural LV systems (lynx–hare, Gause’s Didinium–Paramecium) work predator hunting efficiency evolves slowly relative cycle period. Tried (4 conditions × 5 seeds, 2000 ticks, realistic_specs). evolving (default): predator_mutation_sd = 0.1, predator_max_age = 60 — predators stabilise ~30 within 30 ticks; pred_var = 0 t=500 onwards. Arms-race--equilibrium. frozen: predator_mutation_sd = 0 — predators stabilise ~30; pred_var = 0. Freezing mutation doesn’t help selection initial brain variance still runs completion. longlived (slow pace--life): predator_max_age = 300 (10× prey lifespan; lynx > hare). Also pred_var = 0. Slower evolution still produces saturation. fast_turnover: short-lived + easy reproduction (predator_max_age = 20, predator_min_repro_energy = 60, predator_mutation_sd = 0). Predators now cycle — cycle boom-bust runaway, LV: population explodes 40 10,000 (cap) t ≈ 900 prey crash 150 ~5. phase plot shows one-way trajectory, closed LV loop. textbook LV doesn’t emerge . LV requires dP/dt ∝ prey × predator — predator births proportional prey density. clade’s predator reproduction energy-threshold-gated: predator reproduces energy exceeds predator_min_repro_energy, regardless current prey density. produces either saturation (threshold hard, easy predators can always find enough top ) runaway (threshold easy, boom prey extinction), never density-matched cycling. get LV, need either: predator_repro_rate = kP * mean(prey_density_in_territory) — explicit density-dependent reproduction rate, chemostat-style steady-state predator mortality forced track prey scarcity (e.g. harsh per-tick metabolic cost exceeds marginal kills can cover). Neither current kernel. lynx-hare cycles exist nature lynx reproduction density-dependent gestation/provisioning constraints; clade’s predator module like bacterial predator (constant metabolic cost, eat- -encountered) mammalian carnivore. Deferred potential 0.6+ kernel extension. Companion figure: vignettes/figures/showcase_14b_predators_lv_comparison.png (4-panel time series + phase plot fast_turnover boom-bust). Audit script: predator_prey_lv.R. Oscillation strength vs predator density. increasing predator density produce stronger LV-like oscillations? Measured oscillation score (magnitude negative autocorrelation lags 20–100 tick 50) across 4 predator densities, 10 seeds , 500 ticks. Tried . n_predators_init ∈ {5, 10, 20, 40}, 100 prey, 30×30 grid, grass_rate = 0.15: oscillation scores = 0.217, 0.248, 0.221, 0.257. Moderate oscillation (~0.22–0.26) across densities strong trend. oscillation amplitude insensitive predator count evolved predators efficiently track prey regardless density — saturate behaviourally rather dynamically. clade produces damped LV rather sustained: predator arms race removes phase lag sustains textbook sinusoidal cycles. Prey body-size evolution predation. predation select larger smaller prey? Run 5 seeds 0, 5, 15 predators 600 ticks body_size_evolution = TRUE. Tried . n_pred=0: Δbody_size = +0.080 ± 0.014; n_pred=5: Δ = +0.082 ± 0.021; n_pred=15: Δ = +0.115 ± 0.007. three conditions drift upward (Cope’s rule). 5 seeds predation-accelerates trend (n_pred=15 > n_pred=0) suggestive, 0.5.2 body-size P2 16-seed audit showed direction statistically robust — “predation accelerates” “predation slows” framings inside 5-seed noise band. honest finding: predation modulate body-size drift default parameters. See s-body-size full 16-seed resolution. Spatial heterogeneity resource layering. Two separate theoretical predictions spatial structure: Huffaker (1958) said dispersal barriers patches sustain predator-prey cycles, Rosenzweig (1971) — “paradox enrichment” — said richer prey carrying capacity amplifies oscillations even well-mixed world. clade exposes toroidal first-class spec (TRUE = wraparound, FALSE = clamped boundaries) complex_landscape module adds shrub + canopy resource layers top ground grass. 2×2 tests predictions . Tried . 2×2 factorial toroidal ∈ {TRUE, FALSE} × complex_landscape ∈ {FALSE, TRUE}, 50×50 grid, 250 prey init, 25 predators init, max_agents = 1000 (lifted cycling isn’t clipped cap), 5 seeds × 800 ticks . Prey oscillation scores: Two surprises, mechanism neither clean Huffaker clean Rosenzweig. () complex_landscape within toroidal world DOUBLES oscillation score 50×50 (0.338 → 0.650) raises prey equilibrium (663 → 841). follow-30×30 replicate shows effect grid-scale dependent: 30×30, patchy vs flat gives essentially change cycling (0.307 vs 0.310). amplification appears 50×50. rules pure Rosenzweig-1971 enrichment (amplify scale K raised) instead points spatial- decoupling mechanism: complex_landscape adds resource layers (shrubs + canopy — see inst/julia/src/modules/complex_landscape.jl) create local resource-density pockets, pockets matter grid large enough predator prey trajectories can desynchronise across . 30×30 board small predators prey “lose” patches; 50×50 patches far enough apart local predator depletion prey recovery run phase. clade’s first mechanism partially defeats predator arms-race absorption seen Exps 1–3 6. also strongest, cleanest LV-like cycling clade regime produced — requires resource layering enough grid area layering create effective spatial decoupling. (b) Non-toroidal zeros sustained cycling (osc = 0.00 landscape conditions). clean Huffaker refutation kernel’s non-toroidal implementation clamps boundaries (wrap_or_clamp → clamp(x, 1, n) inst/julia/src/Clade.jl:48) — agents try step across edge end pinned edge rather move rejected. result pile-perimeter, halves effective prey population (276 vs 643) damps ACF monotonically (see figure : bounded panels visible early transients declining trend dominates autocorrelation never dips zero). proper Huffaker test needs reflective boundaries (reject--blocked), clamp. Filed kernel-polish item dev/docs/kernel--biology/ tick.md §6 Variants. Net finding: resource layering within connected (toroidal) world amplifies LV cycling — clade’s first clean Rosenzweig 1971 enrichment signature, first manipulation partially defeats predator arms-race absorption seen every experiment . Deserves dedicated s-spatial-heterogeneity scenario. Huffaker 1958 refugia prediction remains untested reject--blocked boundary implemented. Prey trajectories across 2×2 Huffaker audit (seed 7, 800 ticks, max_agents = 1000). Top-right (toroidal + patchy) condition sustained cycling — three clear peak-trough excursions. Top-left (toroidal + flat) shows one overshoot--damp. bottom row (bounded) show one transient excursion followed monotonic decline; ACF-based oscillation score reads 0.00 declining trend dominates autocorrelation even though visible mini-cycles present early . Grass density × predator density — LV damping depend resources? LV damping strongest prey resource-limited (small amplitude) weakest prey resource-rich (runaway growth, crashes). Tested grass_rate ∈ {0.05, 0.15, 0.30} × n_predators_init ∈ {5, 20} × 5 seeds × 800 ticks. Tried . Prey oscillation scores (mean ± sd across 5 seeds): result non-monotone grass density: oscillation peaks moderate grass (~0.15) damps extremes. scarce grass (0.05), prey stay near ~40 individuals — coherent cycling. rich grass (0.30), prey saturate near ~315 — near ceiling, little room oscillate. moderate regime (grass = 0.15, prey ~190) sits sweet spot carrying-capacity × predation pressure produces cleanest cycle. Predator initial count negligible effect within grass level (arms-race saturation Exp 2 holds: evolved predators fill cap regardless starting number). Group defense × LV — Hamilton (1971) dilution. Hamilton’s selfish-herd dilution arguments predict group defense reduces per-prey attack risk, dampen LV oscillations reducing predator efficiency. Tested group_defense ∈ {FALSE, TRUE} (radius 2, strength 0.3) × 5 seeds × 800 ticks 20 predators. Tried . Prey oscillation score: 0.307 ± 0.013 () vs 0.300 ± 0.020 (). Effectively identical (difference = 0.007, inside 0.02 standard deviation either condition). Prey mean size: 241 vs 245 — also effectively unchanged. ? Evolved predators appear compensate behaviourally per-attack risk reduction: hunt-trait evolution already tracks prey availability density density, uniform reduction attack success absorbed arms-race saturation mechanism flattens predator-count effect Exp 2. Hamiltonian dilution signal likely require either frozen predators (arms-race absorption) (b) variance defense within prey population differential benefit clustering can detected genetically. Neither currently exposed.

title: Within-lifetime reinforcement learning
text: models. agent uses REINFORCE--baseline (actor-critic; Williams 1992) update brain’s output layer based energy reward signals lifetime. implements Baldwin effect (Hinton & Nowlan 1987): individually-learned behaviours can stabilise genetically generations learning relaxes requirement genes encode exact optimal behaviour. demographic signature clade measures — population size rising actor_critic — follows Baldwin framework rather algorithm paper . Key parameters. Expected output (corrected 2026-04-18). REINFORCE within-lifetime learning produces robust demographic advantage — actor_critic agents sustain ~17% larger equilibrium population non-learning controls — actions allowed exploit learned posterior mean rather fully re-randomised Thompson sampling tick. default BNN sampling fully couples action noise sigma, cancels within-lifetime policy improvement; RL pays bnn_action_noise_scale < 1.0. found (2026-04-18 realistic_specs() + BNN sigma decoupling, 16 seeds × 2 conditions, 60×60 grid, 2000 ticks). Kernel settings required: promotes scenario 🟠 ✅. Williams 1992’s mechanism works; needs agent actually use learns. energy-per-agent metric flat — RL reallocates foraging efficiency reproduction, increasing population size per-capita energy. See dev/audit/fidelity/rl_realistic.md protocol per-seed results. Historical audits. 2026-04-17 144-run sweep (freq × lr) showed significant effect reframed scenario 🟠. right call legacy BNN-coupling — actor_critic’s learned mu washed sigma-driven action noise tick. decoupling unlocks canonical signal. 0.4.1 audit (3 seeds × 3 BNN sample frequencies × RL /× 500 ticks). bnn_sample_freq=1 BNN resamples weights every tick, washing REINFORCE gradient updates (null Δ). freq=5 sample persists long enough gradients compound — Δn = +5.2 (✅). freq=20 sample rigid populations crash. Scenarios combining BNN brains rl_mode=‘actor_critic’ use freq=5. Earlier audit (2026-04-16, since superseded). 3-seed sweep bnn_sample_freq ∈ {1, 5, 20} reported Δn = +5.2 agents freq = 5, scenario labelled ✅ regime. 2026-04-17 8-seed × 3×3 rl_update_freq × learning_rate sweep () reproduce significant positive Δ tested cell, earlier 3-seed claim retracted. current honest position documented paragraph . Baldwin canalisation interaction documented separately s-baldwin — sigma coupling behavioural variance creates kernel-limitation caveat, independent RL gradient channel.
code: s$bnn_action_noise_scale <- 0.7    # actions ≈ mu + 0.7·sigma·z s$bnn_sample_freq        <- 5L     # resample Thompson every 5 ticks s$rl_update_freq         <- 5L     # REINFORCE update every 5 ticks s$learning_rate_init_mean <- 0.005 s$complex_landscape       <- TRUE   # RL needs a non-trivial policy s <- default_specs() s$rl_mode   <- "actor_critic" s$max_ticks <- 300L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)

title: Within-lifetime reinforcement learning
text: baseline result shows agents RL adapt within lifetime local resource distribution, Baldwin Effect visible BNN prior sigma narrows. go beyond: RL × brain size Add brain_size_evolution = TRUE. within-lifetime RL reduce selection pressure brain size (learning compensates cognitive limitations) amplify (larger-brained agents learn efficiently via sensing advantage)? Compare mean_brain_size tick 500 without rl_mode = "actor_critic". Tried . brain_size_evolution = TRUE, brain_size_cost_scale = 1.5, 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: -RL final brain = 1.034 (n = 88); RL final brain = 1.012 (n = 97). RL reduced selection pressure genetic brain size: agents compensate moderate cognitive limitations learning within lifetimes, weakening fitness gradient genetically encoded large brains. Population size increased RL (+10%), brain size decreased — RL genetic brain evolution substitutable rather complementary parameters. RL × social learning Add social_learning = TRUE. agents can learn individually (RL) copy socially converge faster either mechanism alone? Watch mean_energy genetic_diversity: combination flatten genetic diversity reducing -individual variance foraging success either mechanism separately? Tried . Four learning rates tested (lr = 0.001, 0.01, 0.05, 0.10; 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): gd = 0.183, 0.185, 0.192, 0.184; mean_energy = 118, 120, 116, 119; n = 104, 106, 120, 108. Learning rate = 0.05 produced highest genetic diversity (0.192) largest population (n = 120), mean energy lowest — fast-exploring policy creates phenotypic variance larger populations cost per-agent energy efficiency. diversity increase RL (0.183–0.192 vs ~0.185 baseline without RL) consistent cross-module gallery finding. RL seasonality Add seasonal_amplitude = 0.8. within-lifetime RL improve survival resource troughs allowing agents adapt foraging strategy winter conditions within single season? Compare mean agent survival rates across seasonal cycle without RL. Tried . RL + epigenetics combined (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): mean_sigma = 0.275 vs RL : sigma = 0.333, gd = 0.191 vs 0.180. Adding epigenetics reduced BNN sigma increased genetic diversity simultaneously. Epigenetic inheritance sigma values allows offspring start partially canalized priors (lower sigma = narrower uncertainty), accelerating Baldwin Effect. gd increase (0.191 vs 0.180) consistent epigenetic variation adding additional heritable dimension beyond genetic variation alone.

title: Scavenging and carrion dynamics
text: models. Scavenging — consumption carrion left dead conspecifics — widespread foraging strategy provides resource buffer primary productivity low (DeVault et al. 2003). clade, scavenging = TRUE, agent deaths deposit fraction remaining energy carrion grid cell died. Carrion decays tick rate carrion_decay_rate can consumed agent moves onto cell, yielding carrion_eat_gain energy units. Key parameters. Expected output (latest: 0.5.x ✅ via realistic_specs + predator guild). Earlier default-scale sweeps found DeVault 2003 energy benefit carrion supply thin (small grid, short runs, sparse predators). realistic_specs (60×60, 2000 ticks, 30 init predators, predator_max_agents = 120, predator_energy_gain = 20) predator guild produces enough carrion scavenging channel express measurable energy boost: DeVault 2003 holds predator guild supplies adequate carcasses. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/scavenging_realistic.md. Earlier (pre-realistic-specs, 🟠). 2×3×2 parameter sweep (192 runs, scavenging_strength_sweep.R) grass_rate ∈ {0.05, 0.10} × carrion_eat_gain ∈ {3, 8, 15} × carrion_fraction ∈ {0.5, 1.0} found cell giving Δenergy > 0 t ≥ 2 canonical direction. Diagnosis: carrion supply thin default_specs scale (30×30 grid, 500-tick runs) — scavenging population-density × grid-size × run-length phenomenon needs realistic regime express.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  base_specs <- function() {   s <- default_specs()   s$n_agents_init <- 100L   s$grid_rows     <- 30L   s$grid_cols     <- 30L   s$grass_rate    <- 0.15   s$max_ticks     <- 400L   s }  s_no <- base_specs(); s_no$scavenging <- FALSE s_sc <- base_specs() s_sc$scavenging        <- TRUE s_sc$carrion_fraction  <- 0.5 s_sc$carrion_decay_rate <- 0.1 s_sc$carrion_eat_gain  <- 3.0  d_no <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_no))$ticks d_sc <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_sc))$ticks  d_no$condition <- "No scavenging" d_sc$condition <- "Scavenging" dat <- rbind(d_no, d_sc)  ggplot(dat, aes(x = t, y = mean_energy, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("No scavenging" = "#E53935", "Scavenging" = "#FB8C00"),     name   = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Mean agent energy",     title = "Scavenging sustains energy budgets under resource scarcity"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

Scavenging gives ~4% energy ~11% population size. promotes scenario 🟠 ✅ — earlier 2026-04-17 null result (192-run sweep × 12 cells default 30×30 / 500-tick scale) scale-limited: predator guild default scale sparse generate detectable carrion channel. realistic_specs() scale (60×60 grid, 30 predators predator_max_age = 60, 2000 ticks) carrion accumulates measurable foraging channel. title: Scavenging and carrion dynamics
text: baseline result shows scavenging sustains higher mean energy baseline identical low-grass conditions. go beyond: Carrion pathogen reservoir disease = TRUE carrion_transmission_prob > 0, agents die infected deposit flagged carcass; agent eats becomes infected specified probability. models fomite transmission observed vultures, hyenas, obligate scavengers (Ogada et al. 2012). question: carrion-mediated transmission reverse previously observed anti-epidemic effect scavenging? Expected output. carrion_transmission_prob = 0.3, n_new_infections higher safe-carrion baseline, particularly population troughs scavenging rates highest. carrion_decay_rate acts natural quarantine: fast decay reduces fomite persistence dampens transmission. Watch whether epidemic peak shifts earlier (higher early transmission dense infection) later (carcasses accumulate epidemic matures). Kin scavenging Add kin_selection = TRUE. kin altruism scavenging buffer energy deficits — additive (help independently) redundant (populations kin altruism scavenge less energy transfers substitute carrion)? Watch n_altruistic_acts mean_energy jointly combined single-module conditions. Tried . scavenging + disease enabled (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): despite scavenge_events = 0, disease transmission 37% lower scavenging condition (22 vs 35 infections). scavenging module requires larger populations (≥ 200 agents) longer runs (≥ 500 ticks) consistent dead-agent turnover generates detectable carrion. epidemiological signal likely reflects energy-state differences conditions rather literal carrion contact. Scavenging × learning Add rl_mode = "actor_critic". Can within-lifetime RL discover scavenging opportunities faster pure genetic evolution can encode behaviour? Compare mean_energy trajectories RL -RL conditions scavenging-enabled world tick 100 (early learning phase) vs tick 400 (late genetic phase). Tried . Combining scavenging kin selection (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): kin altruism operated normally (1278 acts, n = 132), scavenge_events = 0. modules function independently small population scales — kin altruism operates via live energy transfers scavenging requires dead-agent corpse density. two energy-buffering mechanisms additive principle non-overlapping conditions activate .
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_specs <- function(carrion_tp = 0.0) {   s <- default_specs()   s$n_agents_init           <- 200L   s$grid_rows               <- 25L   s$grid_cols               <- 25L   s$grass_rate              <- 0.08   s$max_ticks               <- 300L   s$scavenging              <- TRUE   s$carrion_fraction        <- 0.5   s$carrion_decay_rate      <- 0.1   s$disease                 <- TRUE   s$transmission_prob       <- 0.15   s$disease_seed_prob       <- 0.03   s$carrion_transmission_prob <- carrion_tp   s }  d_safe <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_specs(0.0)))$ticks d_risk <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_specs(0.3)))$ticks  d_safe$condition <- "Carrion safe (prob = 0)" d_risk$condition <- "Carrion risky (prob = 0.3)" dat <- rbind(d_safe, d_risk)  ggplot(dat, aes(x = t, y = n_new_infections, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Carrion safe (prob = 0)" = "#43A047",                "Carrion risky (prob = 0.3)" = "#E53935"),     name = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "New infections per tick",     title = "Carrion as pathogen reservoir amplifies epidemics"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

title: Seasonal dynamics
text: models. Seasonality imposes periodic variation resource availability mortality risk drives boom-bust dynamics across wide range taxa (Boyce 1979). clade, grass productivity oscillates sinusoidally period season_length amplitude seasonal_amplitude. winter phases, grass regrowth slows per-tick death probability (winter_death_prob) adds additional mortality pressure. result structured environment agents must survive periods scarcity reproduce periods abundance — minimal model temperate seasonality. Key parameters. Expected output. Population size (n_agents) track grass coverage short lag, producing recurrent crashes winter recoveries spring. Multiple complete cycles visible 500 ticks given season_length = 100. found. seasonal_amplitude = 0.6, winter_death_prob = 0.01, 100 agents 30×30 grid, 500 ticks (seed 42): population oscillated 13 223 agents (mean 80). Grass coverage tracked 100-tick sinusoidal cycle clearly — minimum ticks 75, 175, 275 (winter phase) maximum 25, 125, 225 (summer). Population lagged grass approximately 15–20 ticks. aggressive parameters (seasonal_amplitude = 0.8, winter_death_prob = 0.05), population crashed 0 ticks mean dropped 35 — parameter calibration matters substantially observing smooth seasonal cycles rather extinction dynamics. Expected output: population size (blue) tracks scaled grass productivity (green) short lag, producing recurrent winter crashes spring recoveries approximately five complete cycles.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(tidyr)  s <- default_specs() s$seasonal_amplitude <- 0.8 s$season_length      <- 100L s$winter_death_prob  <- 0.05 s$n_agents_init      <- 100L s$grid_rows          <- 30L s$grid_cols          <- 30L s$max_ticks          <- 500L  env  <- run_alife(s) tks  <- get_run_data(env)$ticks  tks$grass_scaled <- tks$grass_coverage /   max(tks$grass_coverage, na.rm = TRUE) *   max(tks$n_agents,       na.rm = TRUE)  dat_long <- pivot_longer(   tks[, c("t", "n_agents", "grass_scaled")],   cols      = c("n_agents", "grass_scaled"),   names_to  = "variable",   values_to = "value" ) dat_long$variable <- ifelse(dat_long$variable == "n_agents",                             "Population size", "Grass coverage (scaled)")  ggplot(dat_long, aes(x = t, y = value, colour = variable)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Population size" = "#1565C0",                "Grass coverage (scaled)" = "#2E7D32"),     name = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Value (agents / scaled grass)",     title = "Seasonal boom-bust dynamics"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

title: Seasonal dynamics
text: baseline result shows population size tracking grass productivity short lag, producing recurrent winter crashes spring recoveries. go beyond: Life history × seasonality Compare life_history = "semelparous" vs "iteroparous" seasonal_amplitude = 0.8. Semelparous organisms time reproduction resource peaks; semelparous life history produce sharper synchrony birth events spring grass peak? Compute cross-correlation n_births grass_coverage life history. Tried . seasonal_amplitude = 0.8, 60 agents, 300 ticks, seed 42: r(births, grass_coverage) = 0.193 iteroparous vs 0.307 semelparous. Semelparous reproduction 59% tightly coupled resource availability iteroparous. Mean births per tick also much higher semelparous (2.1 vs 0.6), mass reproduction events occur grass peaks cease — producing pulses rather steady trickle iteroparous strategy. Dispersal bet-hedging Add dispersal_evolution = TRUE. seasonal environments, dispersal spreads risk across patches different resource phases. evolved mean_dispersal increase seasonal_amplitude across five-level batch_alife() sweep, consistent dispersal bet-hedging strategy? Tried . Across four seasonal amplitudes (0, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9; 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): population size insensitive amplitude (n = 104–112). Genetic diversity peaked moderate amplitude (gd = 0.188 amp = 0.3) remained stable higher amplitudes. extreme amplitude (amp = 0.9) drove grass coverage low 7% populations persisted — agents buffer seasonal troughs within generation rather tracking demographically 200-tick timescales. Brain size cognitive buffering Add brain_size_evolution = TRUE. cognitive buffering hypothesis (van Schaik et al. 2023) predicts seasonality selects larger brains cognitive flexibility helps navigate variable resource distributions. mean_brain_size final tick increase monotonically seasonal_amplitude? Tried . Adding parental_care = TRUE alongside seasonality (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): final population identical (n = 111 vs 111), genetic diversity marginally increased care (gd = 0.191 vs 0.188). brain-size cognitive buffering hypothesis requires longer runs: 200 ticks, seasonal amplitude (0.3–0.9) produced measurable increase evolved brain size relative -seasonality baseline. 5-seed cross-module gallery found 0.6% brain-size difference stable seasonal environments.

what: Signals and mate choice: sexual selection
title: Signals and mate choice
text: models. Sexual selection can produce elaborate traits reduce survival increase mating success. Two contrasting mechanisms proposed. Zahavi’s (1975) handicap principle requires signals condition-dependent costly maintain, high-quality individuals can afford elaborate signals; signal magnitude honest indicator genetic quality. Fisher’s (1930) runaway process operates genetic correlation signal elaboration preference: females prefer trait, sons choosy females inherit trait daughters inherit preference, driving fixation. scenario enables multi-dimensional signals female preference, allowing mechanisms operate, tracks whether signal magnitude remains correlated mean_energy (consistent honest signalling) becomes decoupled (consistent runaway dynamics). Key parameters. Expected output. mean_signal_magnitude increases time sexual selection drives signal elaboration. signal_cost sufficient maintain honesty, mean_signal_magnitude correlates positively mean_energy: agents better condition carry elaborate signals. weak zero signal_cost allows decoupling signal condition, consistent runaway dynamics.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  # default_specs() — fast_specs() crashes this scenario (n_final < 5 # across 5 seeds per 2026-04-17 crash_audit.R). Signal cost + sexual # selection intensity combine to overwhelm the short fast_specs # lifespan. s <- default_specs() s$signal_dims          <- 3L s$signal_cost          <- 0.05 s$mate_choice_mode     <- "preference" s$mate_choice_strength <- 0.7 s$max_ticks            <- 400L s$random_seed          <- 21L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  p1 <- ggplot(data$ticks, aes(t, mean_signal_magnitude)) +   geom_line(colour = "#984ea3") +   labs(title = "Signal elaboration under sexual selection",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean signal magnitude") +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(data$ticks, aes(mean_energy, mean_signal_magnitude)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8, colour = "#984ea3") +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "#333333", linewidth = 0.7) +   labs(title = "Signal magnitude vs mean energy (honest signalling check)",        x = "Mean energy", y = "Mean signal magnitude") +   theme_minimal()  print(p1) print(p2)

title: Signals and mate choice
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenario’s parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 6.5x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. Zahavi handicap (3 seeds × 500 ticks × 4 signal_cost levels). Top: signal magnitude near-flat (~1.07) across cost levels — signals maintained ceiling. Bottom: population decreases 196→179 cost rises (−9%) — demographic cost honest signalling visible Zahavi effect. found (2026-04-15 audit). 5-seed multi-seed run signal_dims = 3, signal_cost = 0.05, mate_choice_mode = "preference", 120 agents, 500 ticks. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/signals.md. Zahavi honesty test. Within-run Spearman correlation mean_energy mean_signal_magnitude averaged ρ = 0.25 across 5 seeds — positive consistent Zahavi handicap (better-conditioned agents carry elaborate signals). Cost sweep (3 seeds × 5 values signal_cost): signal magnitude flat ~1.0 across entire range 0.0 0.20. Cost affect population size (247 → 197), equilibrium signal magnitude. clade-specific finding: drift determines signal stationary distribution, cost manifests demographic attrition rather signal reduction. strict textbook Zahavi dynamics (cost reduces signal magnitude) kernel extension heritable individual-level preferences needed.

Without drift, signal trait heritable variation selection act. signal_evolution_drift = TRUE signal_drift_sd = 0.05, signals begin diverge within 100 ticks; adding resource stress allows honest-signal correlation measured. Adding signals slightly reduced evolved toxicity (0.018 vs 0.023 without signals), suggesting mild trade-two costly traits. Avoided attacks remained 0 conditions, confirming predator avoidance learning requires longer runs accumulate sufficient signal-toxicity correlations Batesian mimicry dynamics emerge.

title: Social learning
text: models. Agents can copy output-layer weights successful neighbours (energy threshold). model vertical- horizontal-cultural transmission: information actions rewarding propagates socially, accelerating adaptation relative genetic evolution alone (Henrich & McElreath 2003; Boyd & Richerson 1985). Mechanism note: clade’s copy rule energy-threshold biased — successful neighbours copied higher rates unsuccessful ones. differs conformist-bias prestige-bias mechanisms Henrich-McElreath Boyd-Richerson analyse formally. clade reproduces generic “social-learning beats asocial-learning” claim literature, specific bias-type predictions. Key parameters. Expected output. Mean energy may higher baseline run identical specs, particularly novel changing environments. benefit visible mutation_sd low (little genetic variation) successful behaviours exist population. Boyd & Richerson (1985) cultural transmission (5 seeds × 500 ticks, brain_type=ann, n=150). Top: population +6.5% social learning (successful foraging strategies propagate). Bottom: genetic diversity similar conditions. found (2026-04-17 sweep). social_learning_freq matters lot. 8-seed × 3×3 sweep (social_learning_sweep.R) freq ∈ {5, 20, 50} × n_agents_init ∈ {80, 150, 250} found: freq=50, n_init=150: Δmean_energy = +3.3, t = 2.27 (significant, regime demo chunk uses). aggressive frequencies (freq = 5 20), copying fires neighbours’ behaviours stabilised effect null weakly negative. higher density (n_init=250), copying partners noisier advantage saturates. 0.4.x default social_learning_freq = 10 landed “-aggressive” regime, original 8-seed direction check (Tier C batch 1) reported null. Status ✅ freq=50 regime; see dev/audit/fidelity/social_learning_sweep.rds full table. Earlier finding (original audit). effect social learning depends critically brain type. brain_type = "ann" (deterministic weights), social learning produced +4.5% mean population advantage (mean 158 vs 151, 2 replicates, 300 ticks): copied output-layer weights persist network benefit subsequent actions. brain_type = "bnn" (default stochastic weights), advantage zero (mean 183 conditions): BNN agents resample weights prior distribution tick, diluting policy copied social model can influence behaviour. reveals important constraint cultural transmission model: social learning operates output-layer weights, BNN agents retain weights ticks. interaction social_learning = TRUE + rl_mode = "actor_critic" predicted restore social learning benefits RL updates (applied BNN sample) reinforce copied policy within agent’s lifetime next sampling step.
code: s <- default_specs() s$social_learning      <- TRUE s$social_learning_freq <- 50L    # 0.5.6 audit: freq=10/20 give noise-dominated                                  # copying; freq=50 is the viable regime s$n_agents_init        <- 150L   # social learning requires sufficient density s$max_ticks            <- 400L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)

title: Social learning
text: baseline result shows mean energy genetic diversity maintained higher levels baseline, successful foraging behaviours propagate socially. go beyond: Social learning × stress hypermutation Add stress_hypermutation = TRUE. Social copying propagates existing strategies; hypermutation generates novel ones. interact synergistically (novel strategies discovered hypermutation spread faster socially) antagonistically (social copying incumbent strategies disrupted hypermutation noise)? Tried . social_learning = TRUE, grass_rate = 0.05, 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: without hypermutation — mean energy = 109.4, diversity = 0.1821, final n = 101. stress_hypermutation = TRUE — mean energy = 109.8, diversity = 0.1805, final n = 104. combination mildly synergistic population size (+3%) energy, diversity slightly lower. Hypermutation generates novel strategies social learning rapidly spreads, reducing range strategies circulation — mild homogenisation effect rather expected diversity amplification. Social learning × speciation Add speciation = TRUE. social learning slow speciation homogenising behaviours across incipient species (cultural gene flow), accelerate allowing rapid behavioural niche partitioning without genetic change? Watch whether n_species grows faster slower social learning enabled. Tried . Four social_learning_freq values (1, 5, 10, 20; 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): sl_events = 0 conditions. Social learning events logged 0 throughout — sl_events column suggests social copying threshold triggered 50-agent density. Population size (99–110) genetic diversity (0.184–0.185) nearly identical regardless learning frequency. social learning module appears require either larger populations closer-proximity agents trigger copying events reliably. Learning radius × genetic diversity Vary social_learning_radius 1 10 across batch_alife(). Theory predicts large learning radius homogenises behaviour reduces genetic diversity spreading successful strategies. genetic diversity vs radius relationship monotone, small radius also reduce diversity (insufficient copying spread beneficial innovations)? Tried . Social learning + RL combined (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): gd = 0.190, sl_events = 0, vs RL : gd = 0.190. Neither combination social learning alone showed active sl_events. RL social learning appear operate independently population scale — social learning copying RL-updated weights competing RL exploration. zero sl_events limitation means genetic diversity comparison reflects RL dynamics , predicted social-genetic interaction.
